sources.source_name: Wikipedia (English)
sources.generation_script: Wikipedia REST extracts (natural-start paragraphs) + Claude review & translation
sources.notes: English paragraph extracts from Wikipedia, vocabulary-leveled, human-reviewed (dropped poems/fragments/broken), Chinese by Claude.

paragraphs.text: Every Saturday morning, my mother and I walk to the market near the river. We bring two big bags and a small list of things to buy. The air is cool, and the street is full of people, bikes, and the smell of fresh bread.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每个周六早上，我和妈妈步行去河边的市场。我们带上两个大袋子和一张小购物清单。空气凉爽，街上满是行人、自行车和新鲜面包的香味。

paragraphs.text: We always stop at the same fruit stand first. The old man there knows us well, and he often gives me an apple for free. Mother picks the vegetables slowly and asks about the price of fish.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们总是先在同一个水果摊停下。摊主老爷爷很熟悉我们，常常免费送我一个苹果。妈妈慢慢挑着蔬菜，还问鱼的价格。

paragraphs.text: On the way home, the bags are heavy, but I like to carry them. We talk about what to cook for lunch, and the morning always feels too short.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 回家的路上袋子很重，但我喜欢提着它们。我们聊着午饭做什么，总觉得早晨过得太快。

entries.title: My First Day at a New School

paragraphs.text: Last year my family moved to a new city because of my father's work. I was happy about the bigger house, but I was afraid of one thing: the new school. I did not know anyone there, and I am not good at talking to new people.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 去年因为爸爸的工作，我们全家搬到了一个新城市。我对更大的房子很满意，但有一件事让我害怕：新学校。那里我谁都不认识，而且我不擅长和陌生人说话。

paragraphs.text: On the first morning, I stood at the school gate for a long time. Students walked past me in groups, laughing and talking. I felt very small. Then a tall boy stopped and asked if I was new. He said his name was Daniel and that his family had moved here two years ago, so he knew how I felt.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第一天早上，我在校门口站了很久。学生们成群结队地走过，又说又笑。我觉得自己很渺小。这时一个高个子男生停下来，问我是不是新来的。他说他叫丹尼尔，两年前他家也搬来这里，所以他懂我的感受。

paragraphs.text: Daniel showed me where my classroom was and sat with me at lunch. In the afternoon, he introduced me to his friends, and one of them lived on my street. By the end of the day, the school did not feel so strange anymore.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 丹尼尔带我找到教室，午饭时还和我坐在一起。下午他把我介绍给他的朋友们，其中一个就住在我家那条街上。一天结束时，学校已经不再那么陌生了。

paragraphs.text: Now, when I see a new student standing alone at the gate, I always walk over and say hello first. I remember how much one small hello can change a whole year.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在，每当我看到新同学独自站在校门口，我总会主动走过去打招呼。我记得一句小小的问候能改变整整一年。

paragraphs.text: Our class started a small garden behind the school library last spring. The idea came from our science teacher, Miss Lee. She said that we could learn more from growing real plants than from reading about them in books. At first, many of us thought it would be boring work.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 去年春天，我们班在学校图书馆后面开辟了一个小菜园。这个主意来自我们的科学老师李老师。她说，亲手种植物比在书本上读到的学得更多。起初，我们很多人觉得这会是无聊的活儿。

paragraphs.text: We were wrong. There was so much to do and to decide. Which plants need full sun? How often should we water them? Why did the tomatoes grow well on one side but not on the other? Every week brought a new problem, and we had to find the answers together.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们错了。要做的事和要做的决定太多了。哪些植物需要充足的阳光？多久浇一次水？为什么西红柿在一边长得好，另一边却不行？每周都有新问题，我们必须一起寻找答案。

paragraphs.text: The hardest time came in July. The weather was very hot, and school was closed for the holiday. We made a plan: every student took two days each month to come and water the garden. Nobody wanted to be the one who let the plants die, so everyone kept their promise.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最难的时候是七月。天气非常热，学校又放假了。我们订了一个计划：每个学生每月负责两天来浇水。谁都不想成为让植物枯死的那个人，所以每个人都信守了承诺。

paragraphs.text: In September, we picked our first full harvest: tomatoes, beans, and more green vegetables than our class could eat. We sold some at the school gate and gave the rest to the teachers. With the money, we bought seeds for next year.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 九月，我们迎来了第一次大丰收：西红柿、豆角，还有多到全班吃不完的绿叶蔬菜。我们在校门口卖掉了一些，剩下的送给了老师们。用赚来的钱，我们买了来年的种子。

paragraphs.text: Looking back, I think Miss Lee was right, but not only about science. The garden taught us how to share work, how to keep going when things get hard, and how good it feels to build something with your own hands.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 回头看，我觉得李老师说得对，但不仅仅是关于科学。菜园教会了我们如何分担工作，如何在困难时坚持下去，以及亲手创造一样东西的感觉有多好。

entries.title: Why I Stopped Buying Coffee Every Day

paragraphs.text: For three years, I bought a cup of coffee on my way to work every single morning. It was a small pleasure, and I never thought about the cost. Then one evening, I did the math on my phone: the habit was costing me more than a holiday trip every year.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 整整三年，我每天早上上班路上都会买一杯咖啡。这是个小小的享受，我从没想过它的花销。直到一天晚上，我用手机算了一笔账：这个习惯每年花掉的钱比一次度假旅行还多。

paragraphs.text: I did not give up coffee. Instead, I bought a simple machine and started making it at home. The first week felt strange, but soon I actually began to enjoy the quiet ten minutes in my kitchen before leaving the house.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我没有戒掉咖啡，而是买了一台简单的咖啡机，开始在家自己做。第一周感觉很别扭，但很快我竟开始享受出门前在厨房里安静的十分钟。

paragraphs.text: Now I put the money I save into a small account. It is not about the coffee anymore. It is about noticing the small choices that quietly shape a whole year.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在我把省下的钱存进一个小账户。这已经不再是咖啡的问题，而是学会留意那些悄悄塑造一整年的小选择。

paragraphs.text: Last month, I left my phone in a taxi. I realized it only after the car had driven away, and for a moment I just stood on the street, not knowing what to do. My whole life seemed to be in that phone: photos, messages, bank cards, and the numbers of everyone I know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 上个月，我把手机落在了出租车上。直到车开走我才发现，那一刻我呆站在街头，不知所措。我的整个生活似乎都在那部手机里：照片、信息、银行卡，还有所有认识的人的号码。

paragraphs.text: I borrowed a stranger's phone to call my own number. No one answered the first time, or the second. I was ready to give up when, on the third try, a voice said hello. It was the driver. He had found the phone on the back seat and had already turned around to look for me.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我借了一位路人的手机拨打自己的号码。第一次没人接，第二次也没有。我正准备放弃时，第三次拨打有人应了一声。是那位司机。他在后座发现了手机，并且已经掉头回来找我。

paragraphs.text: Twenty minutes later, he handed it back and refused to take any money. He said that last year someone had returned his daughter's lost bag, and he was simply passing the kindness on. I thanked him again and again, and he just smiled and drove away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 二十分钟后，他把手机还给我，并拒绝收任何酬谢。他说去年有人把他女儿丢失的包送了回来，他只是把这份善意传递下去。我一再道谢，他只是笑笑，开车走了。

paragraphs.text: That evening I added a new contact to my phone: the driver's number, saved under the name A Friend. The city feels different when you know it is full of people like him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那天晚上，我在手机里存了一个新联系人：那位司机的号码，名字存的是“一位朋友”。当你知道城市里满是这样的人时，它给你的感觉都不一样了。

entries.title: Learning to Cook Changed My Life

paragraphs.text: When I moved to the city for my first job, I knew how to cook exactly three things: instant noodles, fried eggs, and rice. For the first few months, I ate out for almost every meal. It was easy and fast, but by the end of each month, my wallet was empty and I felt tired all the time.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 刚到城市开始第一份工作时，我只会做三样东西：泡面、煎蛋和米饭。头几个月，我几乎顿顿在外面吃。这样又方便又快，但每到月底钱包就空了，而且我总觉得疲惫。

paragraphs.text: The change started with a video my sister sent me. It showed a simple tomato soup, made in fifteen minutes with five things from any supermarket. I tried it on a Sunday evening. The kitchen was a mess afterwards, and the soup was too salty, but I had made it myself, and it was warm and real.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 改变始于姐姐发给我的一个视频。视频里是一道简单的番茄汤，用超市随处可买的五样东西，十五分钟就能做好。我在一个周日晚上试了试。事后厨房一片狼藉，汤也太咸了，但那是我亲手做的，温暖而真实。

paragraphs.text: After that, I cooked one new dish every weekend. Some were terrible. I once forgot a chicken in the oven for two hours, and my smoke alarm became famous in the building. But slowly, I built a small list of meals I could make well, and then a bigger one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从那以后，我每个周末学做一道新菜。有些做得很糟。有一次我把一只鸡忘在烤箱里两个小时，烟雾报警器在整栋楼都出了名。但慢慢地，我攒下了一小份拿手菜清单，后来清单越来越长。

paragraphs.text: Cooking changed more than my dinner. I started inviting friends over instead of meeting them in noisy restaurants. We talked more and spent less. I began to visit the morning market near my street, where shopping is slower but the food is fresher and the sellers remember your face.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 做饭改变的不只是我的晚餐。我开始邀请朋友来家里，而不是在嘈杂的餐馆见面。我们聊得更多，花得更少。我开始逛家附近的早市，在那里买东西节奏更慢，但食材更新鲜，摊主还会记住你的脸。

paragraphs.text: Two years later, I still keep the first recipe my sister sent me. The paper is marked with oil and sauce, and the soup is still sometimes too salty. But every time I make it, I remember that big changes often begin with one small, simple step.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 两年后的今天，我仍然保留着姐姐发给我的第一份食谱。纸上沾满了油渍和酱汁，汤有时还是会太咸。但每次做这道汤，我都会想起：大的改变往往始于一个小而简单的开始。

entries.title: The Quiet Power of Walking Meetings

paragraphs.text: My manager has an unusual habit: when a discussion needs only two people, she refuses to book a meeting room. Instead, we walk. There is a small park behind our office building, and one slow loop around it takes about twenty minutes, which is exactly the length of a useful conversation.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我的经理有个不寻常的习惯：只需要两个人讨论的事情，她从不订会议室，而是带你去散步。我们办公楼后面有个小公园，慢慢绕一圈大约二十分钟，恰好是一次有效谈话的长度。

paragraphs.text: At first I found it strange to discuss serious work without a screen between us. But walking side by side changes the conversation. Nobody checks messages. Difficult points feel less like an attack when you are both facing the same direction.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 起初我觉得不对着屏幕谈正事很奇怪。但并肩行走改变了谈话本身。没有人查看消息。当两个人面朝同一个方向时，尖锐的问题听起来也不那么像指责了。

paragraphs.text: Research supports her habit, apparently, but she never quotes it. She simply says that the best ideas in her career arrived somewhere between the second corner and the third bench.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 据说研究也支持她的习惯，但她从不引用。她只是说，她职业生涯中最好的想法，都出现在第二个拐角和第三条长椅之间的某个地方。

paragraphs.text: The storm hit our neighborhood on a Friday night, and by ten the whole district had lost power. At first, everyone treated it as a small adventure. We lit candles, found the old radio, and the children thought the dark staircase was the best game of the year.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 暴风雨在周五晚上袭击了我们的街区，十点钟整个片区都停了电。起初，大家都把这当成一场小小的冒险。我们点起蜡烛，翻出旧收音机，孩子们觉得漆黑的楼梯是今年最好玩的游戏。

paragraphs.text: By the second day, the adventure had worn off. Food in the fridge was going bad, phones were dead, and the elderly couple on the fourth floor could not manage the stairs in the dark. That was when the building chat group, usually full of complaints about parking, quietly changed its character.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 到了第二天，新鲜感消失了。冰箱里的食物开始变质，手机没了电，四楼的老两口在黑暗中爬不了楼梯。就在这时，平时满是停车抱怨的楼栋群，悄悄变了样。

paragraphs.text: Someone with a gas stove offered to boil water for the whole floor. A young man collected everyone's power banks and charged them at his office across the city. Two teenagers made a list of elderly neighbors and knocked on their doors twice a day, carrying water and asking what they needed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有煤气灶的人主动为整层楼烧开水。一个小伙子收齐了大家的充电宝，带到城另一头的办公室去充电。两个中学生列了一份独居老人名单，每天敲两次门，送水并询问他们需要什么。

paragraphs.text: Power returned on the fourth evening, and the building lit up window by window to the sound of cheering. The chat group went back to arguing about parking spaces within a week. But something had changed: now the arguments end sooner, and they always end politely.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第四天傍晚来电了，整栋楼的窗户在欢呼声中一扇接一扇亮起。不到一周，群里又开始为车位争吵。但有些东西变了：如今的争吵结束得更快，而且总是以客气收场。

entries.title: The Second Life of an Old Factory

paragraphs.text: The textile factory at the edge of our city closed down when I was still in primary school. For fifteen years, the long brick buildings stood empty behind a rusting fence, and the only visitors were stray cats and teenagers looking for somewhere to be alone. Most people assumed the site would eventually be cleared for apartment towers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我还在上小学时，城边的那家纺织厂就倒闭了。十五年里，长长的砖楼空荡荡地立在锈蚀的围栏后面，唯一的访客是流浪猫和想找地方独处的少年。大多数人以为这块地最终会被推平，盖起公寓楼。

paragraphs.text: The plan that actually arrived was stranger and better. A group of young architects rented the main hall for almost nothing and turned one corner into a workshop. Furniture makers followed, then a bakery that needed cheap space for its ovens, then a climbing gym under the high ceilings where machines once stood.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 实际到来的计划更出人意料，也更好。一群年轻建筑师以近乎免费的价格租下主厂房，把一个角落改成了工作室。家具匠人跟着来了，然后是一家需要廉价空间放烤炉的面包店，再后来，曾经摆放机器的高大车间里开起了攀岩馆。

paragraphs.text: The city government, to its credit, resisted the urge to over-plan. It repaired the roofs, installed proper wiring, and then largely stepped back. Rents stayed low on one condition: every business had to open its doors to the public a few days each month, whether for classes, tours, or simple conversation.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 值得称道的是，市政府克制住了过度规划的冲动。他们修好屋顶，装好电路，然后基本退到一旁。低租金只有一个条件：每家店每月必须向公众开放几天，无论是办课程、做参观，还是单纯聊天。

paragraphs.text: My grandmother worked in that factory for twenty years, and last spring I finally brought her back. She walked through the bakery that now fills her old workshop, touched the brick wall by the window, and stood quietly for a while. Then she bought two loaves of bread and told the young baker, with great authority, that the light in this corner had always been the best in the building.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我祖母曾在那家工厂工作了二十年，去年春天我终于带她故地重游。她穿过如今开在她旧车间里的面包店，摸了摸窗边的砖墙，安静地站了一会儿。然后她买了两条面包，以十足权威的口吻告诉年轻的面包师：这个角落的光线从来都是整栋楼里最好的。

paragraphs.text: Cities are often praised for what they build and criticized for what they tear down. The old factory taught our city a third option: keep the walls, change the life inside them, and let memory and the future share the same address.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 城市常因建造了什么而受赞扬，因拆除了什么而被批评。这座老工厂教会了我们的城市第三种选择：留住墙体，更换墙内的生活，让记忆与未来共用同一个地址。

paragraphs.text: Our department tried a small experiment last term: a reading group where doctoral students from completely different fields discussed one difficult paper each week. A historian, two biologists, an economist, and a computer scientist around one table sounds like the opening of a joke, and for the first month it often felt like one.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 上学期我们系做了个小实验：组织一个读书会，让来自完全不同领域的博士生每周讨论一篇难懂的论文。一位历史学家、两位生物学家、一位经济学家和一位计算机科学家围坐一桌，听起来像笑话的开头——头一个月也确实常常像个笑话。

paragraphs.text: The discomfort turned out to be the point. Forced to explain methods without jargon, we discovered how many of our certainties were habits rather than arguments. The economist's questions about my data were naive in form and devastating in substance.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但这种不适恰恰是关键。被迫用大白话解释研究方法时，我们发现自己的许多确信只是习惯，而非论证。经济学家对我数据的提问，形式上天真，实质上致命。

paragraphs.text: Attendance was voluntary, and yet nobody quit. Whatever we lost in comfort, we gained in sharper questions, and sharper questions, we slowly realized, are the true currency of research.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 读书会自愿参加，却没有一个人退出。我们失去的是舒适，换来的是更锐利的问题——而我们慢慢明白，更锐利的问题才是研究的真正通货。

paragraphs.text: Midway through my second year, my supervisor gave me the strangest advice I had received in graduate school: start writing down your failures, she said, with the same care you give your results. Not as confession, but as data. I thought the idea was slightly absurd and started the log mainly to please her.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 研二过半时，导师给了我读研以来最奇怪的建议：开始记录你的失败，她说，要像对待成果一样认真。不是当作忏悔，而是当作数据。我觉得这主意有点荒唐，记日志最初主要是为了让她满意。

paragraphs.text: The early entries were painful to write. A promising method that collapsed after three weeks of work. A beautiful hypothesis killed by an ugly control experiment. An interview study that taught me mostly about my own poor questions. Reading them back, I expected to feel discouraged.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最初的几条写起来很痛苦。一个看起来大有希望的方法，三周的工作后宣告失败。一个漂亮的假设，被一个难看的对照实验干掉。一项访谈研究，主要教会了我自己的提问有多差。重读这些记录时，我以为自己会泄气。

paragraphs.text: Instead, patterns appeared. My failures were not random; they clustered. I trusted clean simulations too early and real participants too late. I designed experiments to confirm ideas rather than to break them. The log did not make me fail less, at first, but it made me fail differently, which turned out to matter more.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 结果却浮现出规律。我的失败不是随机的，而是成簇出现的。我过早相信干净的模拟，过晚才接触真实的参与者。我设计实验是为了证实想法，而不是推翻想法。这本日志起初并没有让我少失败，但让我以不同的方式失败——事实证明这更重要。

paragraphs.text: I still keep the log, now in its fourth notebook. The results sections of my papers are built, page by page, on everything those notebooks contain and the papers never mention.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这本日志我至今还在记，已经是第四个本子了。我论文里的结果部分，正是一页一页建立在这些本子所记录、而论文从不提及的一切之上。

entries.title: What the Archive Did Not Say

paragraphs.text: I went into the city archive looking for a clean story. My thesis, in its early outline, argued that the great school reform of the 1950s had been designed in the capital and simply rolled out across the provinces. The official documents, neatly bound and stamped, seemed to agree with me. Plans were issued; circulars followed; reports of successful implementation flowed back. On paper, the machine turned smoothly.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我走进市档案馆时，想找的是一个干净利落的故事。我的论文初稿主张：上世纪五十年代的大教育改革由首都设计，然后简单地推行到各省。那些装订整齐、盖着公章的官方文件似乎也同意我的观点。计划下发，通知跟进，成功执行的报告源源不断地回流。在纸面上，这部机器运转顺畅。

paragraphs.text: The trouble began in a box of unsorted correspondence that the archivist almost did not show me. Letters from school principals, written in careful and formal language, kept asking the same strange question in different ways: how were they to apply rules written for large city schools in villages where one teacher taught four grades in a single room? The capital's replies were polite and almost entirely empty.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 麻烦始于一箱未经整理的来往信件——档案管理员差点没拿给我看。校长们的来信措辞谨慎而正式，却用不同的方式反复追问同一个奇怪的问题：为大城市学校制定的规则，要如何应用到一位教师在一间教室里同时教四个年级的乡村学校？而首都的回复客气，却几乎全是空话。

paragraphs.text: Reading further, I found the real mechanism. Provincial inspectors, caught between impossible instructions and real classrooms, had quietly developed their own translations of the reform. They reported compliance upward in the language the capital expected, while teaching principals, in person and off the record, which parts of the rules could be bent and which must be obeyed. The reform that reached the children was written neither in the capital nor in the provinces, but in that unrecorded space between them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 继续读下去，我找到了真正的机制。夹在不可能完成的指令与真实课堂之间的省级督学，悄悄发展出了他们自己对改革的“翻译”。他们用首都期待的语言向上汇报合规情况，同时当面、不留记录地告诉校长们：规则的哪些部分可以变通，哪些必须遵守。最终抵达孩子们的那场改革，既不是在首都写就的，也不是在各省写就的，而是诞生于两者之间那个没有记录的空间。

paragraphs.text: My thesis survived, but only after its central claim had been turned inside out. The reform succeeded, where it did, not because the plan was strong but because the system quietly allowed it to be weak in the right places. The stamped documents told the story the state wished to remember; the unsorted box held the story as it happened.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我的论文活了下来，但其核心论点被彻底翻转。改革在成功的地方之所以成功，不是因为计划强大，而是因为这个体系悄悄允许它在恰当的地方保持弱势。盖章的文件讲述的是国家希望被记住的故事；那箱未经整理的信件里，装着事情真实发生的样子。

paragraphs.text: The discovery changed my method as much as my argument. I stopped reading reports as records of events and began reading them as performances, asking in each case who the intended audience was and what the writer needed that audience to believe. A document, I learned, is an action before it is a source, and the question of what it does must come before the question of what it says.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个发现改变我的方法，正如它改变我的论点。我不再把报告当作事件的记录来读，而是当作表演来读，每一次都追问：预期的读者是谁，写作者需要这位读者相信什么。我学到：一份文件在成为史料之前，首先是一个动作；先问它做了什么，才能问它说了什么。

paragraphs.text: I now tell new students in our group two things before their first archive visit. First, read everything that was filed, because that is the skeleton of your story. Second, ask the archivist what nobody has requested in years, because somewhere in the unrequested boxes lives the part of the past that did not fit the forms.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如今，每当组里有新生第一次去档案馆，我都会叮嘱两件事。第一，把归档的东西全部读完，因为那是你故事的骨架。第二，问问档案管理员有什么是多年来无人调阅的——因为在那些无人问津的箱子里，住着那段装不进表格的过去。

paragraphs.text: Every transit map lies a little. Lines that twist through old neighborhoods are drawn straight; stations separated by a difficult hill appear side by side. Designers defend these lies as kindness: a map that showed every curve would be accurate, unreadable, and useless to the passenger trying to catch a train.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每一张交通图都会撒一点谎。穿过老城区的曲折线路被画成直线；隔着一座难爬的山丘的两个车站，看起来近在咫尺。设计师为这些谎言辩护，称之为善意：一张画出每个弯道的图固然准确，却没法阅读，对赶车的乘客毫无用处。

paragraphs.text: The interesting question is where kindness ends. A famous redesign once made the river vanish entirely, and tourists, trusting the map over the ground, walked confidently into dead ends for years.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有趣的问题在于善意的边界在哪里。一次著名的改版曾让整条河从图上彻底消失，而相信地图胜过相信脚下的游客们，多年来自信地走进一条条死路。

paragraphs.text: Good maps, like good explanations, simplify the truth without betraying it. The test is simple: does the lie help the reader arrive, or only help the drawing look clean?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 好地图和好的解释一样，简化真相而不背叛真相。检验标准很简单：这个谎言是帮读者抵达目的地，还是只是让图面显得干净？

entries.title: The Slow Death of the Phone Call

paragraphs.text: My grandmother treats an unanswered phone call as a minor emergency; my younger colleagues treat an unannounced one as a minor offense. Between these two reactions lies a quiet revolution in manners. In barely two decades, the voice call has moved from the default form of distant communication to something close to an intrusion, reserved for emergencies, deliveries, and the very old.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我祖母把没人接的电话当作小型紧急事件；我的年轻同事则把未经预约的来电当作一种轻微的冒犯。在这两种反应之间，藏着一场礼仪的静悄悄的革命。短短二十年间，语音通话从远距离交流的默认方式，变成了近乎打扰的东西——只留给急事、快递和年纪很大的人。

paragraphs.text: The usual explanation blames convenience: text can wait, voice cannot. But convenience alone misses the deeper shift. A call demands that two attentions meet at the same moment; a message politely declines that demand. We have collectively decided that another person's time is not ours to claim without warning, and the decision has costs as well as courtesies.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 通常的解释归因于便利：文字可以等，语音不能。但仅凭便利无法说明更深层的变化。一通电话要求两个人的注意力在同一时刻相遇；而一条消息礼貌地拒绝了这种要求。我们集体决定：他人的时间不容我们不打招呼就占用。这个决定带来了礼貌，也带来了代价。

paragraphs.text: Something is lost when every conversation is scheduled. The accidental call that ran long, the voice that betrayed worry beneath cheerful words, the argument resolved in ten minutes because neither side could hide behind careful editing: these were not interruptions of relationships but part of their fabric.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当每一次交谈都需要预约时，有些东西就丢失了。那通意外聊了很久的电话，那个在欢快言辞下泄露出担忧的声音，那场十分钟就化解的争执——因为谁也无法躲在字斟句酌的编辑背后。这些从来不是关系的打扰，而是关系的织体本身。

paragraphs.text: I do not expect the trend to reverse, and mostly I do not want it to. But once a month I call an old friend without warning, and we both pretend, for an hour, that it is still an ordinary thing to do.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我不指望这个趋势逆转，大体上也不希望它逆转。但每个月我都会不打招呼地给一位老友打个电话，然后我们俩在一个小时里假装：这仍是一件再平常不过的事。

paragraphs.text: There is a moment, familiar to every serious reader, when a book stops cooperating. The sentences grow long and strange; the argument disappears around a corner; the characters refuse to explain themselves. The modern instinct, trained by interfaces that apologize for every second of friction, is to conclude that the book has failed and to reach for something smoother. I want to argue the opposite: that this moment of resistance is precisely where the value of reading begins.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每个认真的读者都熟悉这样一个时刻：一本书突然不再配合。句子变得冗长而陌生；论证在某个拐角消失不见；人物拒绝解释自己。被那些为每一秒钟阻力道歉的界面训练出来的现代直觉会断定：这本书失败了，然后伸手去拿更顺滑的东西。我想论证的恰恰相反：这个产生抗拒的时刻，正是阅读价值的起点。

paragraphs.text: Ease is a wonderful property in tools and a suspicious one in ideas. A difficult book forces a confession that easy media never demand: the admission that your current mental furniture cannot seat this thought. Confusion, in this light, is not a failure state but a diagnostic one. It marks, with great precision, the exact edge of what you currently understand, which is information no comfortable book can provide.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 省力是工具的美德，却是思想的可疑品质。一本难读的书会迫使你做出轻松媒介从不要求的坦白：承认你现有的思维家具安放不下这个想法。这样看来，困惑不是失败状态，而是诊断状态。它以极高的精度标出了你当前理解力的确切边界——这是任何一本舒适的书都无法提供的信息。

paragraphs.text: The skill that difficult books train is not intelligence but a kind of tolerance: the ability to keep walking while lost. Researchers call this staying in the problem; readers know it as the strange discipline of continuing past the point where understanding fails, trusting that the fog is part of the landscape and not a sign that the road has ended. Every demanding field, from mathematics to medicine, ultimately rests on this tolerance, and almost nothing in contemporary life exercises it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 难读的书训练的不是智力，而是一种耐受力：迷路时继续行走的能力。研究者称之为“待在问题里”；读者则知道这是一种奇特的纪律——在理解失效之处继续前行，并相信迷雾是风景的一部分，而非道路终结的标志。从数学到医学，每一个高要求的领域最终都建立在这种耐受力之上，而当代生活中几乎没有什么东西在锻炼它。

paragraphs.text: None of this is a defense of obscurity for its own sake. Some books are difficult because they protect weak ideas with strong vocabulary, and learning to tell honest difficulty from decorative difficulty is itself part of the discipline. The honest kind repays every hour: the argument that finally assembles itself, three chapters late, arrives with a force that no summary could carry, precisely because you built the road it traveled on.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这一切并不是在为故弄玄虚辩护。有些书之所以难读，是因为它们用强悍的词汇保护贫弱的思想；而学会分辨诚实的困难与装饰性的困难，本身就是这门纪律的一部分。诚实的困难会偿还你的每一个小时：那个迟到了三章才终于自行组装完成的论证，抵达时携带着任何摘要都无法承载的力量——恰恰因为它走过的路是你亲手修建的。

paragraphs.text: So I keep a shelf of books that have defeated me, and I return to it the way climbers return to a mountain that turned them back. Some will defeat me again. But every few years, one of them quietly opens, and I am reminded that the border of the readable is not fixed; it moves outward exactly as far as we are willing to push it, and each book that opens makes the next one on the shelf look a little less impossible than it did before.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 所以我专门留了一格书架，放那些曾经打败过我的书，并像登山者重返曾把自己劝退的山峰那样回到它面前。有些书会再次打败我。但每隔几年，其中一本会悄然敞开。它提醒我：可读性的边界并非固定——我们愿意推多远，它就向外移多远；而每一本敞开的书，都会让书架上的下一本看起来不再那么高不可攀。

entries.title: American Inventors: Rocket Scientist Robert Goddard

paragraphs.text: Today rocket launches and space missions are common. But in the early 1900s, space travel seemed like a dream.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如今，火箭发射和太空任务已是寻常事。但在二十世纪初，太空旅行还像是一场梦。

paragraphs.text: One of the most influential people in the field of rocket science was American Robert Goddard.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在火箭科学领域，最有影响力的人物之一是美国人罗伯特·戈达德。

paragraphs.text: The American space agency NASA describes Goddard as "the father of modern rocket propulsion ."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 美国航天局 NASA 称戈达德为“现代火箭推进之父”。

paragraphs.text: Robert Goddard once said that "the dream of yesterday is the hope of today and the reality of tomorrow." His scientific work gave hope to many dreams about space travel. He turned some of those dreams into reality.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 罗伯特·戈达德曾说：“昨天的梦想是今天的希望，也是明天的现实。”他的科学工作给许多太空旅行的梦想带来了希望。他还把其中一些梦想变成了现实。

paragraphs.text: More than one hundred years ago, Goddard carried out studies and tests of rocket engines.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一百多年前，戈达德就开展了火箭发动机的研究和试验。

paragraphs.text: He developed and flew many rockets that got their power from solid fuels: chemicals that formed a hard substance. In 1925, he made and tested the first rocket engine using a soft chemical fuel. The next year, he successfully launched the world's first liquid-fuel rocket.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他研制并试飞了多枚以固体燃料——一种凝固成硬块的化学物质——为动力的火箭。1925 年，他制造并测试了第一台使用软质化学燃料的火箭发动机。次年，他成功发射了世界上第一枚液体燃料火箭。

paragraphs.text: Many historians consider liquid-fuel rocket flight to be as important as the first airplane flight by the American brothers Orville and Wilbur Wright.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多历史学家认为，液体燃料火箭的飞行与美国莱特兄弟的首次飞机飞行同等重要。

paragraphs.text: Goddard's work proved that machines could travel outside of Earth's atmosphere and into space.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 戈达德的工作证明，机器可以飞出地球大气层进入太空。

paragraphs.text: During his early research, he received money and support from the U.S. Smithsonian Institution. The Smithsonian published several reports about his efforts.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在早期研究中，他得到了美国史密森学会的资助和支持。学会发表了多份关于他研究工作的报告。

paragraphs.text: One publication, called "A Method of Reaching Extreme Altitudes," wrote about his search for ways to send weather recording instruments higher than balloons could fly. It described how he developed the mathematical theories for rocket flight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 其中一份题为《到达极限高度的方法》的出版物，介绍了他如何寻找把气象记录仪器送到比气球更高处的办法，并阐述了他为火箭飞行建立的数学理论。

entries.title: Knitting While Watching Movies Gains Popularity

paragraphs.text: Nearly 200 people in Vienna, Austria, gathered recently to enjoy two fun activities at the same time.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 近日，奥地利维也纳有近两百人聚在一起，同时享受两件趣事。

paragraphs.text: They worked with needles and yarn . And they watched a movie together called The Devil Wears Prada .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们一边摆弄棒针和毛线，一边一起观看电影《穿普拉达的女王》。

paragraphs.text: "It sounds a bit like a grandmother, but it's relaxing . And...what's wrong with letting the grandmother in us express herself," joked Austrian Alexander Koch. He is a 28-year-old who crochets .
paragraphs.translation_zh: “听起来是有点像老奶奶，但这很放松。再说……让我们心里的那位老奶奶表达一下自己，又有什么不好呢？”28 岁的奥地利人亚历山大·科赫开玩笑说。他的爱好是钩针编织。

paragraphs.text: Knitting or crocheting while watching movies has gained popularity in Europe.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一边看电影一边织毛线或钩花，正在欧洲流行起来。

paragraphs.text: Luisa Palmer was one of the organizers of the event. Palmer said that, during the COVID-19 pandemic, "A lot of people started knitting during the lockdown alone at home." Palmer launched the knitting evenings once a month at Votiv Kino, a place to watch movies in the center of the Austrian capital.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 路易莎·帕尔默是这次活动的组织者之一。她说，新冠疫情期间，“很多人在居家隔离时独自开始织毛线”。帕尔默在维也纳市中心的 Votiv Kino 影院发起了每月一次的编织电影夜。

paragraphs.text: "Now we need to find ourselves..." in real life, the 30-year-old told the French News Agency (AFP) before the event.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这位 30 岁的组织者在活动开始前对法新社说：“现在我们需要在现实生活中重新找到自己……”

paragraphs.text: People are knitting, eating, or even breastfeeding babies while watching a movie. This movement is the latest attempt to add different things to the movie-watching experience, said Lisa Stolze. She is a spokesperson for Votiv Kino, the movie house.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 影院发言人丽莎·施托尔策说，观众一边看电影一边织毛线、吃东西，甚至给婴儿喂奶。这场风潮是为观影体验添加新元素的最新尝试。

paragraphs.text: To avoid knitting mistakes, a soft light remains on during the movie. This also helps to increase exchanges between the attendees.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 为了避免织错针脚，放映期间会保留一盏柔和的灯。这也促进了观众之间的交流。

paragraphs.text: Organizers based their event on similar ones in the United States, Northern Europe, Germany and France. Since launching in December of 2024, the movie events in Vienna are usually full.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 组织者借鉴了美国、北欧、德国和法国的类似活动。自 2024 年 12 月启动以来，维也纳的这些电影场次通常场场爆满。

paragraphs.text: "I usually knit while watching movies or listening to podcasts at home, so movie theaters are the perfect place for this activity," said Kaja Vospernik. She is a 23-year-old fashion student.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我平时就在家里边看电影或听播客边织毛线，所以电影院正是做这件事的完美场所。”23 岁的时装专业学生卡娅·沃斯佩尼克说。

paragraphs.text: As the movie plays, the colorful projects take shape. The film takes a back seat, meaning it is less important than the work of knitting or crocheting. Everyone shows off their work. Experienced knitters and crocheters help beginners learn new skills.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 随着电影播放，五彩的织物渐渐成形。电影反而退居其次，没有手里的编织重要。每个人都展示自己的作品，熟手帮新手学习新技巧。

paragraphs.text: She is another organizer of the event.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她是这次活动的另一位组织者。

paragraphs.text: People attend the events for several reasons. Some say the events let them reconnect with something real that is not on a computer. Others say they can "slow down a little."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 人们参加活动的原因各不相同。有人说，这让他们重新接触到电脑之外真实的东西；也有人说，可以“让节奏慢下来一点”。

paragraphs.text: Palmer said, "Doing something with your ten fingers is still more rewarding ..." than working at a computer all day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 帕尔默说：“用自己的十根手指做点东西，还是比对着电脑坐一整天更有成就感……”

paragraphs.text: She added that picking the right movie is also important. Palmer likes to watch a good " cozy comedy" that she has already seen. She joked that it is best to avoid scary movies. They can make you jump causing you to lose your place in your knitting.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她补充说，选对电影也很重要。帕尔默喜欢看自己已经看过的“温馨喜剧”。她开玩笑说最好别放恐怖片——吓一跳的时候，手里的针脚就乱了。

entries.title: Researchers Uncover a Bathhouse Complex in Ancient Pompeii

paragraphs.text: Archeological research has uncovered another treasure of the ancient Roman city of Pompeii: a large private bathhouse .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 考古研究又发现了古罗马城市庞贝的一处珍宝：一座大型私人浴场。

paragraphs.text: The explosion of Mount Vesuvius in 70 AD buried Pompeii in thick ash, wreckage, and lava, or hot liquid rock. Archeologists are continuing to find remains of the city under the ground.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 公元 70 年，维苏威火山喷发，厚厚的火山灰、瓦砾和熔岩（炽热的液态岩石）掩埋了庞贝。考古学家仍在地下不断发现这座城市的遗迹。

paragraphs.text: The bathhouse was part of a large private house, the scientists said. The bathhouse holds a series of rooms that would have provided hot, warm, or cold conditions. The art and design of the complex, including mosaic works, also suggest the owner was wealthy.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 科学家说，这座浴场属于一处大型私宅。浴场内有一系列房间，可分别提供热水、温水或冷水。浴场的艺术与设计，包括马赛克镶嵌作品，也表明主人十分富有。

paragraphs.text: "We have here perhaps the largest thermal complex in a private house in Pompeii," said Gabriel Zuchtriegel, director of the Pompeii Archaeological Park.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 庞贝考古公园主任加布里埃尔·楚赫特里格尔说：“这里也许是庞贝私宅中最大的浴场建筑群。”

paragraphs.text: He explained that Pompeii's powerful and wealthy set up large spaces at their homes to hold major gatherings for ceremonies and other purposes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他解释说，庞贝的权贵富人会在家中布置大型空间，用于举办仪式等重要聚会。

paragraphs.text: "It was an opportunity to show the wealth in which they lived and also to have a nice thermal treatment," he said.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他说：“这是一个机会，既能炫耀他们的富足生活，又能享受舒适的温泉护理。”

paragraphs.text: The baths were unearthed in the so-called Regio IX, a large central area of Pompeii Park that is still unexplored.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 浴场是在所谓的第九区出土的。这是庞贝公园中部一大片尚未勘探的区域。

paragraphs.text: Recently, archeologists working in the same area found a bakery , a laundry and two homes. They also found the bones of three people who died in the volcanic burst.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最近，在同一区域工作的考古学家还发现了一家面包房、一家洗衣坊和两处住宅，并发现了三名死于火山喷发者的遗骨。

paragraphs.text: Pompeii was not the only city destroyed in the explosion. It also buried the city of Herculaneum.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 庞贝并非那次喷发中毁灭的唯一城市。赫库兰尼姆城也一同被掩埋。

paragraphs.text: Zuchtriegel said wealthy citizens of Pompeii often would first take a bath and then attend a banquet, or a big meal. The private thermal complex permitted them to do so together at the home where the banquet was held.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 楚赫特里格尔说，庞贝的富人往往先沐浴，再出席盛大的宴会。私人浴场让他们可以在举办宴会的宅邸里把两件事一并完成。

entries.title: What Is Art Deco and Why Is It Popular Again?

paragraphs.text: Art Deco is an international artistic style that was popular in the 1920s and 1930s.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 装饰艺术（Art Deco）是一种国际艺术风格，流行于二十世纪二三十年代。

paragraphs.text: Now, interest in the style and its qualities is growing. A new generation is enjoying the style's roots and turning it into something new.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如今，人们对这种风格及其特质的兴趣再度升温。新一代人欣赏它的根源，并把它变成新的东西。

paragraphs.text: A public showing, or exhibition, at the Museum of the City of New York provides a look at the style that helped define the city 100 years ago.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 纽约市博物馆的一场公开展览，让人们得以一窥一百年前塑造了这座城市的这种风格。

paragraphs.text: Famous Art Deco buildings include the Chrysler Building, the Empire State Building, Rockefeller Center and Radio City Music Hall.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 著名的装饰艺术建筑包括克莱斯勒大厦、帝国大厦、洛克菲勒中心和无线电城音乐厅。

paragraphs.text: The public showing, called "Art Deco City: New York Postcards from the Leonard A. Lauder Collection," also has clothing and film clips from the time.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这场名为“装饰艺术之城：伦纳德·A·劳德收藏中的纽约明信片”的展览，还展出了那个时代的服装和电影片段。

paragraphs.text: In London, the Victoria and Albert Museum has a collection of day and evening clothing, jewelry and more. The collection comes from Les Ballets Russes, a dance company that had a major effect on fashion .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在伦敦，维多利亚与艾尔伯特博物馆收藏了日装、晚装、珠宝等众多展品。这批藏品来自对时尚影响深远的俄罗斯芭蕾舞团。

paragraphs.text: And in Paris, organizers of the 2024 Olympics created Art Deco posters last year to celebrate the games and mark the one hundred years since the 1924 Olympics, which Paris also hosted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在巴黎，2024 年奥运会的组织者去年创作了装饰艺术风格的海报，以庆祝奥运会，并纪念巴黎主办 1924 年奥运会一百周年。

paragraphs.text: A style of the 1920s and 30s
paragraphs.translation_zh: 二三十年代的一种风格

paragraphs.text: Appearing after World War I, the first Art Deco era had a spirit of creativity and freedom. With the aim of appearing modern, the style became one of the most important forms of design in the 1920s and early 1930s.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第一个装饰艺术时代出现在第一次世界大战之后，洋溢着创造与自由的精神。这种以摩登为追求的风格，成为二十年代和三十年代初最重要的设计形式之一。

paragraphs.text: The Art Deco style is believed to have first appeared at the 1925 International Exhibition of Modern Decorative and Industrial Arts, in Paris.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 人们认为，装饰艺术风格最早亮相于 1925 年在巴黎举办的国际现代装饰与工业艺术博览会。

entries.title: Danish-Swedish Farmdog Is American Kennel Club's Newest Breed

paragraphs.text: The American Kennel Club, or AKC, has named another kind of dog to its registry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 美国养犬俱乐部（AKC）的名录中又添了一个犬种。

paragraphs.text: Say hello to the Danish-Swedish farmdog. Or maybe say "hej," the word for hello in Swedish and Danish.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 来跟丹麦-瑞典农场犬打个招呼吧。或许该说一声“hej”——这是瑞典语和丹麦语里的“你好”。

paragraphs.text: AKC officials announced the addition to its list on January 2. The recognition means the breed now may compete for many best in show prizes. It also will probably create wider interest in the small, lively dogs. Fans of the breed are happy for the honor, but some also worry about the effect increased attention will have on the dog.
paragraphs.translation_zh: AKC 官员于 1 月 2 日宣布了这一新增犬种。获得认可意味着该犬种今后可以角逐众多“全场总冠军”奖项，也可能让更多人关注这种活泼的小型犬。该犬种的爱好者为这份荣誉感到高兴，但也有人担心关注度上升会给这种狗带来影响。

paragraphs.text: Carey Segebart is one of the people who worked to get Danish-Swedish farmdogs recognized by the AKC. "We're excited about it. We're looking forward to it," she said. She plans to show one of her own at a dog show this month near her home in the American state of Iowa.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 凯莉·塞格巴特是推动丹麦-瑞典农场犬获得 AKC 认可的人士之一。“我们很兴奋，也很期待。”她说。她打算本月在艾奥瓦州家附近的犬展上展示自己的一只爱犬。

paragraphs.text: Still, she thinks more attention can have both good and bad results for the breed. "We don't want the breed to just explode too quickly," she said.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不过她认为，更多的关注对这个犬种来说有利也有弊。“我们不希望这个犬种一下子火得太快。”她说。

paragraphs.text: The Danish-Swedish Farmdog Club of America says the breed, DSF for short, goes back centuries in parts of what are now Denmark, southern Sweden and some other European countries.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 美国丹麦-瑞典农场犬俱乐部介绍说，这个简称 DSF 的犬种，在如今的丹麦、瑞典南部及欧洲一些其他地区已有数百年历史。

paragraphs.text: "They're interesting, fun little dogs," said Segebart, who has owned them since 2011 and is the club's incoming president. She said they will try anything and "succeed at most everything."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “它们是有趣又好玩的小狗。”塞格巴特说。她从 2011 年起饲养这种犬，即将出任俱乐部主席。她说它们什么都愿意尝试，而且“几乎样样都能做好”。

paragraphs.text: In their homelands, the dogs' main job was to control small animals called rodents. However, they also can herd , act as watchdogs and be playful with children. The club says some Danish-Swedish farmdogs have even performed in circuses .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在原产地，这种狗的主要工作是控制啮齿类小动物。不过它们也会放牧、看家护院，还能陪孩子玩耍。俱乐部说，有些丹麦-瑞典农场犬甚至上过马戏团的舞台。

paragraphs.text: After Denmark and Sweden became more developed in the 20th century, DSF fans decided to make the breed official in both nations. Kennel clubs there began registering the breed in 1987.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 二十世纪，丹麦和瑞典的发展程度提高之后，DSF 的爱好者决定让这一犬种在两国都获得官方地位。两国的养犬俱乐部于 1987 年开始为该犬种登记。

paragraphs.text: In the United States, many of the about 350 Danish-Swedish farmdogs nationwide already compete in agility , obedience or other sports that are open to all dogs, including mixed breeds.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在美国，全国约 350 只丹麦-瑞典农场犬中，许多已经在敏捷、服从等向所有犬只（包括混种犬）开放的运动项目中参赛。

paragraphs.text: Until now, the dogs could not enter the traditional breed-by-breed judging that leads to best in show prizes at major dog shows. They may appear later this year at two major televised events, the National Dog Show and the AKC National Championship.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在此之前，这些狗无法参加按犬种逐一评审、最终决出全场总冠军的大型犬展传统赛制。今年晚些时候，它们可能亮相两大电视转播赛事：全国犬展和 AKC 全国锦标赛。

paragraphs.text: The Danish-Swedish farmdog is the AKC's 202nd breed and "a wonderful addition to a family that is able to provide it with the exercise and mental stimulation that it needs," said the AKC's Gina DiNardo.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 丹麦-瑞典农场犬是 AKC 认可的第 202 个犬种。AKC 的吉娜·迪纳尔多说，对能够满足其运动量和动脑需求的家庭来说，它是“极好的新成员”。

paragraphs.text: Danish-Swedish farmdog lovers talked for several years about whether they should try to get AKC recognition and the attention that is likely to come with it, Segebart said. The number of people wanting to buy a young farmdog, or puppy, has grown quickly over the last ten years. The wait for a DSF puppy is usually a year or more, she said.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 塞格巴特说，丹麦-瑞典农场犬的爱好者们讨论了好几年，才决定是否争取 AKC 认可以及随之而来的关注。过去十年，想买一只农场犬幼犬的人迅速增多，如今等一只 DSF 幼犬通常要一年以上。

paragraphs.text: Fans of the breed worry that its appealing, relatively easy-care nature could make it quickly become too popular for its own good. They are not the first to worry. Similar concerns surround the French bulldog, which the AKC now says is the most popular breed in the country. Breeding for desired qualities has led to many health problems for the dogs.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 该犬种的爱好者担心，它讨喜又相对好养的天性，可能让它红得太快反受其害。有这种担忧的不止他们。类似的忧虑也围绕着法国斗牛犬——AKC 称其如今是全美最受欢迎的犬种，而对特定特征的选育已给这种狗带来了许多健康问题。

entries.title: Green Means Spring and Other Things

paragraphs.text: Green is an important color in nature. It is the color of grass and the leaves on trees. It is also the color of most growing plants.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 绿色是大自然中重要的颜色。它是青草和树叶的颜色，也是大多数生长中的植物的颜色。

paragraphs.text: Sometimes, the word green means young, fresh and growing. Sometimes, it describes something that is not yet ripe or finished.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有时，green 这个词表示年轻、新鲜、正在成长；有时，它形容尚未成熟或尚未完成的东西。

paragraphs.text: For example, a greenhorn is someone who has no experience, who is new to a situation. In the fifteenth century, a greenhorn was a young cow or ox whose horns had not yet developed. A century or so later, a greenhorn was a soldier who had not yet had any experience in battle. By the eighteenth century, a greenhorn had the meaning it has today - a person who is new in a job.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 比如，greenhorn 指没有经验的新手。十五世纪时，greenhorn 是指犄角还没长出来的小牛。大约一个世纪后，它指还没上过战场的士兵。到了十八世纪，greenhorn 有了今天的含义——工作上的新人。

paragraphs.text: About one hundred years ago, greenhorn was a popular expression in the American west. Old-timers used it to describe a man who had just arrived from one of the big cities back east. The greenhorn lacked the skills he would need to live in the hard, rough country.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 大约一百年前，greenhorn 在美国西部是个流行词。老住户用它形容刚从东部大城市来的人——这种新来的人缺乏在艰苦粗粝的乡野生存所需的本领。

paragraphs.text: Someone who has the ability to grow plants well is said to have a green thumb. The expression comes from the early 1900s.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 擅长种植物的人，我们说他有 green thumb（绿拇指）。这个说法出现于二十世纪初。

paragraphs.text: A person with a green thumb seems to have a magic touch that makes plants grow quickly and well. You might say that the woman next door has a green thumb if her garden continues to grow long after your plants have died.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有绿拇指的人仿佛有一双魔法之手，能让植物长得又快又好。如果你的植物早就枯死了，隔壁太太的花园却生机勃勃，你就可以说她有绿拇指。

paragraphs.text: The Green Revolution is the name given some years ago to the development of new kinds of rice and other grains. The new plants produced much larger crops. The Green Revolution was the result of hard work by agricultural scientists who had green thumbs.
paragraphs.translation_zh: Green Revolution（绿色革命）是多年前人们给水稻等新品种谷物的培育起的名字。新品种带来了大得多的收成。绿色革命正是那些有绿拇指的农业科学家辛勤工作的成果。

paragraphs.text: Green is also the color used to describe the powerful emotion, jealousy. The green-eyed monster is not a frightening creature from outer space. It is an expression used about four hundred years ago by British writer William Shakespeare in his play "Othello."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 绿色还被用来形容一种强烈的情绪——嫉妒。green-eyed monster（绿眼怪物）不是来自外太空的可怕生物，而是大约四百年前英国作家莎士比亚在戏剧《奥赛罗》中用过的一个说法。

paragraphs.text: It describes the unpleasant feeling a person has when someone has something he wants. A young man may suffer from the green-eyed monster if his girlfriend begins going out with someone else. Or, that green-eyed monster may affect your friend if you get a pay raise and she does not.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 它描述的是别人拥有你想要的东西时，你心里那种不舒服的感觉。女朋友开始和别人约会，小伙子可能会被绿眼怪物缠上；你加了薪而朋友没有，绿眼怪物也可能找上她。

paragraphs.text: In most places in the world, a green light is a sign to move ahead. A green light on a traffic signal means your car can continue on. In everyday speech, a green light means approval to continue with a project. We want you to know we have a green light to continue this series next week.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在世界上大多数地方，绿灯都是前进的信号。交通信号的绿灯亮起，车辆就可以继续行驶。在日常用语里，green light 表示对一个项目的放行批准。

paragraphs.text: Food is a popular subject with many people around the world. It is fun to talk about our favorite dishes. It is even more fun to share a favorite dish with others.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 食物是世界各地许多人喜爱的话题。聊聊自己最爱的菜式很有乐趣，与人分享一道拿手菜就更有乐趣了。

paragraphs.text: Today we talk about the word "dish." A dish can be the object on which you serve food. But, a dish can also mean a specially prepared food.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 今天我们来聊聊 dish 这个词。dish 可以指盛食物的器皿，也可以指一道精心烹制的菜肴。

paragraphs.text: Do you have a signature dish ? Our signature is how we sign our names. So, a signature dish is a dish we are known for making well. In a way, it identifies us.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你有 signature dish（招牌菜）吗？signature 本指我们的签名，所以招牌菜就是我们以做得好而出名的菜。某种意义上，它代表了我们。

paragraphs.text: My signature dish is sweet potato rosemary dinner rolls. I am often asked to make them, especially for holiday parties. In my circle of friends, I am known for this dish. When the rolls are on the table, people know that I made them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我的招牌菜是迷迭香红薯餐包。朋友们常请我做这道点心，尤其是节日聚会的时候。在我的朋友圈里，我以这道菜闻名。餐包一上桌，大家就知道是我做的。

paragraphs.text: A tasty dish is something that makes a table look good and inviting. Sometimes the same can be said about a person. Someone who is good-looking can be called a dish. While this usage might be a bit dated, it is still used.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一道漂亮的菜能让餐桌赏心悦目、令人胃口大开。有时这话也可以用来形容人：长得好看的人也可以被称作 a dish。这个用法虽然有点过时，但仍有人在用。

paragraphs.text: Now, let's talk about the verb "dish."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在来说说动词 dish。

paragraphs.text: "To dish" can mean to talk about private or personal information. It might be gossip . But I can also dish about myself. For example, a friend may ask me to dish about my new job.
paragraphs.translation_zh: to dish 可以指谈论私密或个人的信息，可能是说八卦，但也可以是聊自己。比如，朋友可能让我 dish 一下我的新工作。

paragraphs.text: I could also use a phrasal verb form and say my friend want me to dish out all the interesting details.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我也可以用短语动词的形式，说朋友想让我 dish out（和盘托出）所有有趣的细节。

paragraphs.text: Now, to dish up something means to put food on a dish for someone to eat. However, it can also mean sharing personal details, our own and others.
paragraphs.translation_zh: to dish up 的本义是把食物盛进盘子端给别人吃，但它同样可以表示分享个人内幕——自己的或别人的。

paragraphs.text: Technology is always progressing. Today, many modern devices can be controlled by touch or voice. This has led to some traditional electronic controls - such as dials and switches - being replaced with new technologies.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 技术总在进步。如今，许多现代设备可以用触摸或语音控制，这让一些传统的电子控制部件——比如旋钮和开关——被新技术取代。

paragraphs.text: Digital displays have also become increasingly common. For example, digital number readouts have long replaced the hands on many clocks.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 数字显示屏也越来越常见。例如，数字读数早已取代了许多钟表上的指针。

paragraphs.text: Many other devices also now operate purely with digital tools instead of mechanical systems.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多其他设备如今也完全靠数字工具运行，而不再使用机械系统。

paragraphs.text: But some expressions and idioms used by English speakers still describe older technologies.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但英语使用者的一些习语和表达，描述的仍然是旧技术。

paragraphs.text: First, let's talk about dials. These controls are used for changing settings on a piece of equipment, like a radio or old television. We use the dial to tune in to a specific channel.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 先说说旋钮（dial）。这种控制部件用来调节设备的设置，比如收音机或老式电视机。我们转动旋钮，调到想听的频道。

paragraphs.text: That's where the expressions dialed in and tuned in come from. If we are dialed in or tuned in to a radio or television program, we can hear it clearly. There is no noise or static .
paragraphs.translation_zh: dialed in 和 tuned in 这两个表达就来源于此。如果我们 dialed in 或 tuned in 到某个广播或电视节目，就能听得清清楚楚，没有杂音和干扰。

paragraphs.text: The same can be said when using these terms in personal conversations . For example, if you are dialed in to a project or problem, you know exactly what needs to be done. You are thinking clearly about it and remain very focused .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在日常交流中使用这两个词组也是同样的意思。比如，如果你对一个项目或问题 dialed in，你清楚地知道该做什么，思路清晰，注意力高度集中。

paragraphs.text: And when you are tuned in , you understand clearly what is going on around you.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 而当你 tuned in 时，你对周围发生的事情了然于心。

paragraphs.text: "Don't touch that dial" and "stay tuned" are two ways to tell someone to keep watching or listening to a program.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “别转台”（Don't touch that dial）和“锁定频道”（stay tuned）是让人继续收看或收听节目的两种说法。

paragraphs.text: Now, let's talk about switches.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 接下来聊聊开关（switch）。

paragraphs.text: These are built to either be off or on. There are no in-between settings like with a dial. So, when we flip the switch , we make something happen quickly and easily. It can also mean that we suddenly change something.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 开关只有开和关两个状态，不像旋钮有中间档位。所以当我们 flip the switch（按下开关）时，是让某件事快速轻松地发生，也可以表示突然改变某件事。

entries.title: What Does It Take to Be a 'Power Couple'?

paragraphs.text: In American English, we use many expressions to describe when two people have a lot of similarities or are very close. Here are some examples.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在美式英语中，我们用许多表达来形容两个人非常相似或非常亲密。下面是一些例子。

paragraphs.text: If we say two people are joined at the hip, they are nearly always together. If we describe individuals as thick as thieves , it means they are very close. People considered two peas in a pod have many things in common. And we use the term kindred spirits to describe people who often share the same opinions and interests.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果说两个人 joined at the hip（连在胯上），就是说他们几乎形影不离。形容两个人 thick as thieves（亲如窃贼），意思是他们关系非常紧密。被称作 two peas in a pod（一荚双豆）的人有许多共同点。而 kindred spirits（灵魂相契的人）指观点和兴趣常常一致的人。

paragraphs.text: These expressions can represent people who grew closer for different reasons. Maybe they complemented each other well in certain ways. Or maybe their situations brought them together for the purpose of completing something important or meaningful.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这些表达描述的人因为不同的原因而走近：也许他们在某些方面互补，也许境遇让他们为完成某件重要的事走到了一起。

paragraphs.text: But today we are going to discuss a very special kind of relationship - one that involves two people who are romantically involved and very successful.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但今天我们要谈一种非常特别的关系——两个人既是恋人，又都事业有成。

paragraphs.text: People in such relationships can be described as power couples .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 处于这种关系中的人，可以被称作 power couple（强强联合的夫妻档）。

paragraphs.text: There are many examples of famous power couples, including successful movie stars, musicians and others in the performing arts. But power couples can exist in many other industries and communities, as well. And, the two people do not have to work in the same industry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 著名的 power couple 例子很多，包括成功的影星、音乐人和其他表演艺术领域的名人。但权力夫妻档也存在于许多其他行业和圈子，而且两个人不一定在同一行业。

paragraphs.text: If they do, however, that can make them even more powerful. For example, a well-known architect who builds homes could be partnered with a famous designer who decorates them. Or, one person in a power couple may be a music producer, while the other is a famous singer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不过若是同行，他们可能更加强强联手。比如，著名建筑师盖房子，伴侣是著名设计师做装潢；或者一位是音乐制作人，另一位是著名歌手。

paragraphs.text: Even though power couples involve highly successful people, one person could describe their partner as their better half . This is a way to compliment their "other half" in the relationship. For example, one might say during a meeting with a friend, "Hello! How are you? And more importantly, how is your better half?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 尽管 power couple 说的是两位高度成功的人，其中一方仍可能称另一方为 better half（更好的那一半）。这是对感情中“另一半”的恭维。比如和朋友见面时可以说：“你好！最近怎么样？更要紧的是，你那位‘更好的一半’怎么样？”

paragraphs.text: Now, let's hear two friends use some of these expressions.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 现在来听两位朋友怎么使用这些表达。

paragraphs.text: A: Hey, I'm not used to seeing you alone. Where's your sidekick ?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 甲：嘿，很少见你一个人啊。你的跟班呢？

paragraphs.text: B: Huh? Who are you talking about?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乙：啊？你说谁？

paragraphs.text: A: Ben. You guys have been joined at the hip for weeks. Looks like you're thick as thieves.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 甲：本啊。你们俩这几周都快长在一起了，简直亲如窃贼。

paragraphs.text: B: Well, the project is pretty important for the company. So, we're putting in a lot of hours.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乙：嗯，这个项目对公司很重要，所以我们投入了很多时间。

paragraphs.text: A: Are you becoming kindred spirits from working so much together?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 甲：天天泡在一起，都成灵魂知己了吧？

paragraphs.text: B: No. It's just keeping us both very busy.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乙：没有，只是把我们俩都忙坏了。

paragraphs.text: A: Busy trying to become the new power couple in the office?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 甲：忙着成为办公室新晋的最强组合？

paragraphs.text: B: What are you talking about?? We're not thick as thieves or kindred spirits, and we're definitely not a power couple. We are just working hard on the same project!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乙：你说什么呢？？我们既不是什么亲如窃贼，也不是灵魂知己，更绝对不是什么最强组合。我们只是在努力做同一个项目！

paragraphs.text: Today we talk about different kinds of politics.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 今天我们聊聊几种不同的“政治”。

paragraphs.text: We start with kitchen-table politics . The kitchen is often considered the hub, or center, of the home. It is where we gather to not only share a meal but to talk about the events of the day. We often discuss issues of importance with our family and friends around the dining room table.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 先说 kitchen-table politics（餐桌政治）。厨房常被视为家庭的中枢。我们不仅在那里吃饭，也在那里谈论一天的见闻。我们常常围着餐桌与家人朋友讨论要紧的事。

paragraphs.text: So, kitchen-table issues are those that affect individuals and their families daily. A family might discuss these issues at the kitchen table during mealtimes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 所以，kitchen-table issues（餐桌议题）指那些每天影响个人和家庭的事务。一家人吃饭时，可能就在餐桌上讨论这些问题。

paragraphs.text: Rising gas and food prices are two good examples of kitchen-table politics. These issues affect decisions we make day to day. They are sometimes also called dinner -table politics. Or we could describe them as bread-and-butter issues.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 油价和食品价格上涨就是餐桌政治的两个好例子。这些问题影响我们每天的决定。它们有时也被称为 dinner-table politics，或者 bread-and-butter issues（面包黄油议题）。

paragraphs.text: Merriam Webster dictionary defines bread-and-butter issues as "being as basic as the earning of one's livelihood ." The online Cambridge Dictionary defines a bread-and-butter issue as "a subject or problem that relates directly to most people's needs and interests."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 《韦氏词典》将 bread-and-butter issues 定义为“与谋生同样基本的事务”。《剑桥在线词典》则解释为“与大多数人的需求和利益直接相关的话题或问题”。

paragraphs.text: For example, affordable food prices and availability of good healthcare are the kinds of bread-and-butter political issues that people vote on.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 例如，亲民的食品价格、便利的优质医疗，都是人们投票时关心的面包黄油式政治议题。

paragraphs.text: The noun bread and butter means a way of making a living. For example, for a creative writer, her ideas are her bread and butter.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 名词 bread and butter 指谋生的手段。例如，对一位创意作家来说，她的点子就是她的饭碗。

paragraphs.text: So, bread-and-butter political issues deal with our ability to provide for the needs of ourselves and our families.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 因此，面包黄油式的政治议题，关乎我们养活自己和家人的能力。

paragraphs.text: Next, let's talk about green politics . These political issues deal with caring for the natural environment. Green politics can cover ideas such as environmental sustainability , recycling , and threats to wildlife. The natural world is central to green politics.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 接下来说 green politics（绿色政治）。这类政治议题关注爱护自然环境，涵盖环境可持续、回收利用、野生动物面临的威胁等议题。自然界是绿色政治的核心。

paragraphs.text: Today we talk about our busy lives. Many people have family and work responsibilities. We might have religious or community activities. Taking care of our health is also important. And let's not forget about our personal projects, our interests, and our friendships.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 今天聊聊我们忙碌的生活。许多人有家庭和工作的责任，可能还有宗教或社区活动。照顾好身体同样重要。更别忘了个人项目、兴趣爱好和友情。

paragraphs.text: With everything on our plate , it is easy to feel overwhelmed , overextended, and overcommitted .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 盘子里堆了这么多事（everything on our plate），人很容易感到不堪重负、分身乏术、应接不暇。

paragraphs.text: However, if we try to do too much, we may spread ourselves too thin . This expression means we have taken on too much. We are pushed to the limit .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然而，如果试图做太多事，我们就可能 spread ourselves too thin（把自己摊得太薄）。这个表达的意思是揽下了过多的事情，被逼到了极限。

paragraphs.text: People who are spread too thin , take on too many jobs, projects, or goals. The heavy load requires more energy or attention than they possess.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 把自己摊得太薄的人，接了太多工作、项目或目标。沉重的担子需要的精力和注意力超出了他们的所有。

paragraphs.text: If we work on many projects simultaneously, or at the same time, it is possible that none will get done well, if they get done at all. And it is possible such multitasking could stress us out.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如果同时（simultaneously）推进许多项目，很可能没有一件能做好——如果还能做完的话。这样多线作战还可能让我们压力爆表。

paragraphs.text: Being spread too thin can also be described as biting off more than we can chew . When we bite off more than we can chew, we attempt to do more than is possible.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 把自己摊得太薄，也可以说成 biting off more than we can chew（咬下嚼不动的一大口）：企图做超出自己能力的事。

entries.title: Knee Jerk and Gut Reactions: When We Are Not in Control

paragraphs.text: In life, some of our decisions and actions are well thought out. They involve research, examination, and time for thought. And other decisions and actions are not well thought out. We make them quickly, without thinking, almost like a pure physical reaction.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 人生中，我们有些决定和行动经过深思熟虑，有研究、有考察、有思考的时间；而另一些决定和行动则未经斟酌——我们不假思索地快速做出，几乎像纯粹的生理反应。

paragraphs.text: We can call these decisions, actions or reactions knee-jerk .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这类决定、行动或反应，我们可以称为 knee-jerk（膝跳式）。

paragraphs.text: This term comes from a medical process. Meriam Webster defines the noun "knee jerk" as "an involuntary forward kick produced by a light blow on the tendon below the kneecap." It is an involuntary action. It's automatic.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个词来自一种医学检查。《韦氏词典》把名词 knee jerk 定义为“轻敲膝盖骨下方肌腱引起的不自主前踢”。它是不自主的、自动发生的。

paragraphs.text: The words "involuntary" and "automatic" are important when understanding the term "knee jerk" and when to use it. Knee-jerk reactions seem to happen automatically without a decision-making process.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “不自主”和“自动”是理解和使用 knee jerk 的关键。膝跳式反应仿佛自动发生，不经过决策过程。

paragraphs.text: We often use "knee jerk" as an adjective.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我们常把 knee-jerk 用作形容词。

paragraphs.text: Like hitting your kneecap to move your leg, knee-jerk reactions or statements also seem like an involuntary reflex .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就像敲击膝盖腿就会动一样，膝跳式的反应或言论也像一种不受控制的反射。

paragraphs.text: "Knee jerk" also describes predictable behavior. Again, hit the knee, the leg will kick every time. So "knee jerk" describes someone who always reacts quickly and without enough thought.
paragraphs.translation_zh: knee jerk 还形容可以预料的行为——敲一下膝盖，腿每次都会踢。所以它也形容那种总是反应飞快却欠考虑的人。

paragraphs.text: For example, let's say you are on a team with someone who never faces responsibility for their mistakes in the game. If brought to account, the player always blames the dropped ball on other teammates. You can say that player is having a knee-jerk reaction to criticism. They have a go-to response. It's like a reflex they can't control.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 举个例子。假设你的队友从不为自己在比赛中的失误承担责任，一被追问，就总把丢球怪到其他队友头上。你可以说这名球员对批评有膝跳式反应：他有一套现成的应对，就像无法控制的反射。

paragraphs.text: We often use knee-jerk in a criticizing or negative way.
paragraphs.translation_zh: knee-jerk 通常带着批评或负面的意味。

paragraphs.text: Now, a gut reaction also happens without thinking. But it is a bit different than a knee-jerk response. A gut reaction is a feeling. When we have a gut reaction, our instincts are telling us that something may not be right. Gut reactions are instinctual warnings.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 而 gut reaction（直觉反应）同样不经思考，却与膝跳式反应有些不同。直觉反应是一种感觉：当我们产生直觉反应时，是本能在告诉我们事情可能不对劲。直觉反应是发自本能的警告。

paragraphs.text: So, we also use this expression a bit differently than "knee jerk." We usually talk about our own gut reactions or impulses, not others.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 因此这个表达的用法也和 knee jerk 略有不同：我们通常谈论自己的直觉反应或冲动，而不是别人的。

paragraphs.text: One day, I was hiking on a mountain in California. I had hiked for about two hours and was near the top. The view, I was told, was truly beautiful. I couldn't wait to see it! Then, suddenly, I had a strange feeling. Something just didn't feel right. I can't explain why, but my gut instinct was to turn around and head back down the mountain. I later found out that a dangerous bear had been sighted in the same area that I had fled. I am so thankful I listened to my gut instinct.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，我在加利福尼亚爬山。爬了大约两小时，快到山顶了。听说山顶的景色美极了，我迫不及待想看到。突然，我产生了一种奇怪的感觉——说不上为什么，就是觉得不对劲。我的直觉让我掉头下山。后来我得知，就在我逃离的那片区域，有人目击到一头危险的熊。我真庆幸自己听从了直觉。

entries.title: 'The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County,' by Mark Twain

paragraphs.text: A friend of mine in the East asked me to visit old Simon Wheeler, to ask about my friends friend, Leonidas W. Smiley. I did as my friend asked me to do and this story is the result.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 东部的一位朋友托我去拜访西蒙·威勒老头，打听他朋友的朋友列昂尼达斯·W·斯迈利的下落。我照朋友的嘱托做了，这篇故事就是结果。

paragraphs.text: I found Simon Wheeler sleeping by the stove in the ruined mining camp of Angels.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我在天使矿营的废墟里找到了西蒙·威勒，他正靠着火炉打盹。

paragraphs.text: I saw that he was fat and had no hair, and had a gentle and simple look upon his peaceful face. He woke up, and gave me "good-day."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我看他身材肥胖，头顶光秃，平和的脸上带着安详质朴的神情。他醒过来，向我道了声“日安”。

paragraphs.text: I told him a friend had asked me to find out about a friend named Leonidas W. Smiley, who he heard was at one time living in Angels Camp. I added that if Mr. Wheeler could tell me anything about this Leonidas W. Smiley, I would feel a great responsibility to him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我告诉他，一位朋友托我打听一个叫列昂尼达斯·W·斯迈利的人，听说此人一度住在天使营。我还说，要是威勒先生能告诉我这位斯迈利的任何消息，我将感激不尽。

paragraphs.text: Simon Wheeler forced me into a corner with his chair and began telling me this long story. He never smiled, he never frowned. But all through the endless story there was a feeling of great seriousness and honesty. This showed me plainly that he thought the heroes of the story were men of great intelligence.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 西蒙·威勒用椅子把我逼到墙角，开始讲这个长长的故事。他从不微笑，也从不皱眉。但在这没完没了的故事里，始终有一种郑重其事的诚恳。这分明让我看出，他真心认为故事里的主人公都是了不起的聪明人。

paragraphs.text: I let him go on in his own way, and never stopped him once. This is the story Simon Wheeler told.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 我由着他按自己的方式讲下去，一次也没打断。下面就是西蒙·威勒讲的故事。

paragraphs.text: Leonidas W. .... hm... Le... well, there was a man here once by the name of Jim Smiley, in the winter of 1849 -- or may be it was the spring of 1850. Anyway, he was the strangest man. He was always making money on anything that turned up if he could get anybody to try to make money on the other side. And if he could not do that, he would change sides.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 列昂尼达斯·W……嗯……列……对了，这儿从前倒有过一个叫吉姆·斯迈利的人，那是 1849 年冬天——也可能是 1850 年春天。反正他是个最古怪的人。只要能找到人跟他对赌，碰上什么事他都要赌钱；要是找不着，他就换到另一边去赌。

paragraphs.text: And he was lucky, uncommon lucky. He most always was a winner. If there was a dog-fight, he would try to win money on it. If there was a cat-fight, he would take the risk. If there was a chicken-fight, he would try to win money on it. Why, if there was two birds setting on a fence, he would want you to decide which one would fly first so he could win money.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 而且他手气好，出奇地好，十赌九赢。有斗狗的，他要赌钱；有斗猫的，他也敢下注；有斗鸡的，他还是要赌。嘿，就是两只鸟落在篱笆上，他也要你猜哪只先飞，好赌上一把。

paragraphs.text: Lots of the boys here have seen that Smiley and can tell you about him. Why, it did not matter to him. He would try to make money on anything. He was the most unusual man. Parson Walker's wife was very sick once, for a long time, and it seemed as if they were not going to save her.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这儿的小伙子很多都见过那个斯迈利，都能给你讲讲他。嘿，对他来说赌什么都无所谓，什么都能拿来赌钱，他就是这么个怪人。沃克牧师的太太有一回病得很重，病了好久，眼看就救不活了。

paragraphs.text: But one morning he come in, and Smiley asked him how was his wife, and he said she was better, thank God. And Smiley, before he thought, says, "Well, I'll risk my money she will not get well."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 可是有一天早上牧师走进来，斯迈利问他太太怎么样了。牧师说好多了，感谢上帝。斯迈利想都没想，张口就说：“好吧，我押钱赌她好不了。”

paragraphs.text: And Smiley had a little small dog. To look at the dog, you would think he was not worth anything but to sit around and look mean and look for a chance to steal something. But as soon as there was money, he was a different dog. Another dog might attack and throw him around two or three times. Then all of a sudden Smiley's dog would grab that other dog by his back leg and hang on till the men said it was over.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 斯迈利还养过一条小狗。光看模样，你会觉得这狗一文不值，只会闲坐着等机会偷东西。可是一旦押上了钱，它就变成另一条狗了。别的狗能把它扑倒，甩来甩去摔上两三回，然后冷不防地，斯迈利的狗就咬住对方的后腿不松口，直到人们认输为止。

entries.title: Raise Your English Level with More Vocabulary

paragraphs.text: Let's look at two English learners. One knows about 500 words and understands how they are used in a sentence. The other knows about 1,000 words but has some problems with grammar.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 来看两位英语学习者：一位认识大约 500 个单词，并且理解它们在句子里的用法；另一位认识约 1000 个单词，但语法上有些问题。

paragraphs.text: Which person can understand and communicate more in English?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哪一位能用英语理解和交流得更多？

paragraphs.text: Clearly, it is the person who knows 1,000 words.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 显然是认识 1000 个单词的那位。

paragraphs.text: Some studies have shown that for learners to fully understand a written work, they need to know 95 percent or more of the words in it. That means that if there are only five or more words you do not know in a 100-word text, you might not really understand it!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一些研究表明，学习者要完全读懂一篇文章，需要认识其中 95% 以上的单词。也就是说，一篇 100 词的文章里只要有五个以上生词，你可能就读不太懂了！

paragraphs.text: In today's program, we will give you some advice for building up your vocabulary. "Vocabulary" can mean a single word, two words such as phrasal verbs , or many words together, such as an expression.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在今天的节目里，我们给你一些扩充词汇量的建议。“词汇”可以是单个单词，可以是短语动词这样的两个词，也可以是多个词构成的表达。

paragraphs.text: There are two important things to keep in mind when trying to learn new vocabulary.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 学习新词汇时，有两件重要的事要记住。

paragraphs.text: First, connect new words to a story or a situation. This makes it easier to remember vocabulary than studying words from lists.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第一，把生词和一个故事或情境联系起来。这比对着单词表死记硬背更容易记住。

paragraphs.text: Second, be active , not passive . Make yourself use the new words.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二，要主动，不要被动。逼自己使用新学的词。

paragraphs.text: In today's workplace, what you learned in the usual college courses may not be very useful later in life.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在当今的职场，大学常规课程里学到的东西，日后可能派不上多大用场。

paragraphs.text: For example, in the field of information technology, workers might never use BASIC, the computer language that was taught in the 1990s. But they might need to know Python or SQL.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 比如在信息技术领域，员工可能永远用不上 BASIC——上世纪九十年代教的计算机语言，但他们可能需要会 Python 或 SQL。

paragraphs.text: The answer for students or workers is a professional certificate program, or what is called microcredential.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 对学生和职场人来说，答案是职业证书课程，也就是所谓的微证书（microcredential）。

paragraphs.text: UNESCO, the world's education agency, defines microcredential as professional certification that centers on a set of learning outcomes in a narrow field. It is completed over a shorter time than a traditional university program, from a week to a month.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 联合国教科文组织把微证书定义为聚焦于某个狭窄领域一组学习成果的职业认证。它的学时比传统大学课程短，从一周到一个月不等。

paragraphs.text: The most popular fields in which microcredentials appear are data analytics , information technology support and cybersecurity . The courses are often developed by technology businesses like Google, Salesforce, Meta, Intuit and IBM. The businesses provide a certificate when a student finishes the course.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 微证书最热门的领域是数据分析、信息技术支持和网络安全。这些课程通常由谷歌、Salesforce、Meta、Intuit 和 IBM 等科技企业开发，学生修完课程后由企业颁发证书。

paragraphs.text: Coursera, an online educational service, recently released a report on how microcredentials are preparing students for jobs and affecting higher education schools. It says that 51 percent of colleges in the United States that offer microcredentials also provide school credit and more plan to do so in the next few years.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在线教育平台 Coursera 最近发布报告，分析微证书如何帮学生为就业做准备，又如何影响高校。报告说，美国提供微证书的高校中有 51% 同时给予学分，未来几年还会有更多高校跟进。

paragraphs.text: Some colleges include one or more courses leading to microcredentials as part of their usual programs. At Hawaii Pacific University, school leaders say including professional certifications in its business program has sharply increased the number of interested students.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一些大学把通往微证书的课程纳入常规项目。夏威夷太平洋大学的负责人说，把职业认证纳入商科项目后，感兴趣的学生人数大幅增加。

paragraphs.text: How to find the right microcredential?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 如何找到合适的微证书？

paragraphs.text: For students, Credential Finder offers a way to search for the right microcredential offerings by field of study or industry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 学生可以用 Credential Finder 按学科或行业检索合适的微证书项目。

paragraphs.text: For example, if you are studying information science, you may take Google Data Analytics or an IBM Data Analyst course. If you are working toward a degree in business administration, you may take a Google Project Management course. Your university program's advisors should be helpful in identifying the best certificate programs for your area of study.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 比如，学信息科学的可以修谷歌数据分析或 IBM 数据分析师课程；攻读工商管理学位的可以修谷歌项目管理课程。所在院系的导师也能帮你找到最适合你专业方向的证书项目。

paragraphs.text: Be sure to check with your country's government for more information on special training programs. One example is Ukraine, which is aiming to meet its shortage of skilled workers with its ReSkill UA initiative . It provides help with finding training and employment.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 还可以向所在国家的政府了解专项培训计划。乌克兰就是一例：该国正通过 ReSkill UA 计划应对技术工人短缺，提供培训和就业方面的帮助。

paragraphs.text: How much do microcredentials cost?
paragraphs.translation_zh: 微证书要花多少钱？

paragraphs.text: Many of the course providers permit learners to use the materials for free, but to earn a certification, payment is required.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多课程提供方允许免费使用学习材料，但要获得认证就需要付费。

paragraphs.text: Coursera has a monthly fee of $59 or a yearly fee of $399 for its Coursera Plus membership. For courses that universities offer for credit, the payment will depend on the price set by the school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: Coursera 的月费为 59 美元，Coursera Plus 会员年费 399 美元。大学提供学分的课程，费用由学校定价。

entries.title: In Uganda, Cost of Attending School Keeps Children Home

paragraphs.text: The World Bank said last year that 54 percent of adults in Africa south of the Sahara Desert ranked the issue of paying school costs higher than medical or other costs.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 世界银行去年表示，撒哈拉以南非洲有 54% 的成年人认为，支付学费的压力比医疗等其他开支更大。

paragraphs.text: The aid organization said school fees were the biggest financial worry for 40 percent of people in Uganda. In that country, a top government-supported school reportedly costs $700 for a three-month term. That is a lot of money in a country where yearly economic activity per person was $864 in 2023.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 该机构说，在乌干达，学费是 40% 的人最大的经济顾虑。据报道，该国一所顶尖公立学校一个学期（三个月）的费用是 700 美元——在一个 2023 年人均年经济产出仅 864 美元的国家，这是一大笔钱。

paragraphs.text: World Bank information shows that African countries in the area also have the highest rates of children out of school in the world. Parents in Uganda report unpredictable increases in the cost of attending school, or tuition, as a cause of tension. Some critics are calling for laws to protect parents from exploitation.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 世界银行的数据显示，该地区的非洲国家失学儿童比例也是全球最高的。乌干达的家长反映，上学费用（学费）的上涨难以预料，造成了紧张情绪。一些批评者呼吁立法保护家长免受盘剥。

paragraphs.text: The Equal Opportunities Commission is a government agency that reports on the issues of inequality and discrimination. It released a report in September that called for punishment against government-supported schools that demand fees that are too high. It warned that arbitrarily raising fees can force children to drop out of school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 平等机会委员会是负责报告不平等和歧视问题的政府机构。它在九月发布报告，呼吁惩处收费过高的公立学校，并警告说任意涨价会迫使孩子辍学。

paragraphs.text: The Uganda Bureau of Statistics says that children's attendance in early grades is about 68 percent. But the percentage falls to 22 percent for secondary school. The main reason given is financial difficulty.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌干达统计局称，低年级儿童的入学率约为 68%，但到中学阶段骤降至 22%，主要原因就是经济困难。

paragraphs.text: Uganda has a program of secondary education for all students. It was introduced in 2007. However, schools are not in good condition. The schools usually do not require tuition , but parents must pay for uniforms , textbooks and other costs.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌干达 2007 年起推行面向所有学生的中等教育计划。然而学校条件不佳。这些学校通常不收学费，但家长必须支付校服、课本等费用。

paragraphs.text: Private schools are more popular but also more costly. Spokesperson for the Ministry of Education Dennis Mugimba said the government is not interested in making rules about prices. Mugimba said setting private school fees is "purely administrative and it is adjusted according to the business environment." But he said costs linked to "capital development" should not be the responsibility of parents.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 私立学校更受欢迎，但也更昂贵。教育部发言人丹尼斯·穆吉姆巴说，政府无意对价格定规矩。他说私立学校的收费“纯属行政事务，会根据经营环境调整”，但与“基本建设”相关的费用不应由家长承担。

paragraphs.text: The number of private schools across Uganda has increased. The Associated Press (AP) reports that they are now a majority of the country's schools. But some people with ties to education say placing importance on the profitability of education as a business is worrying.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌干达各地的私立学校数量不断增加。美联社报道说，私立学校如今已占全国学校的多数。但一些教育界人士担心，把教育当生意、把盈利放在首位的倾向令人忧虑。

paragraphs.text: Fagil Mandy is a former inspector of schools. Mandy said there should be standardized rules to make school fees predictable for everyone.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 法吉尔·曼迪曾任学校督学。他说应当制定统一规则，让所有人都能预知学费。

paragraphs.text: It is normal for both private and government-supported schools to request money for many projects. These can include buying a bus or purchasing laboratory equipment.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 公立和私立学校为各种项目筹钱都很常见，比如购买校车或实验室设备。

paragraphs.text: Shalom Mirembe is a student who spoke to the AP. Her mother takes care of four children. Her father recently died.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 沙洛姆·米伦贝是一位接受美联社采访的学生。她的母亲要照顾四个孩子，父亲不久前去世了。

paragraphs.text: The school Mirembe attends near Kampala costs $300 per term. But her mother sends Mirembe to school without paying. She is depending on the mercy of officials.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 米伦贝就读的学校在坎帕拉附近，每学期收费 300 美元。母亲没交钱就把她送去上学，全凭校方的怜悯。

paragraphs.text: But Joanita Seguya, a teacher at Wampewo Ntakke Secondary, said schools are taking measures to bar students from school if their parents do not pay. Seguya said of the school's 2100 students, 400 are from families that struggle to pay fees.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但万佩沃·恩塔克中学的教师乔安妮塔·塞古亚说，学校正在采取措施，把家长欠费的学生拒之门外。她说全校 2100 名学生中，有 400 人来自交费困难的家庭。

paragraphs.text: Twenty-year-old Mirembe is taking her final exams this year. At one point, school officials were going to bar her from school because her mother had failed to pay the fees. But when school administrators found out that her father had died recently, they permitted her to return. Some of her teachers attended the funeral.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 二十岁的米伦贝今年要参加毕业考试。学校一度因为母亲欠费要将她拒之门外，但当校方得知她父亲刚刚去世后，允许她返校。她的几位老师还参加了葬礼。

paragraphs.text: But other families are not so fortunate.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但其他家庭就没有这么幸运了。

paragraphs.text: Moses Serikomawa is jobless with seven children. He said paying $200 in school fees each term is too much when the family lacks food sometimes. But he added, "The children still want to go back to school."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 莫塞斯·塞里科马瓦失业在家，有七个孩子。他说家里有时连饭都吃不上，每学期 200 美元的学费实在难以负担。但他补充道：“孩子们还是想回学校。”

paragraphs.text: Suzanne Meyer, the assistant director of the English Language Institute at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, says that many students need to develop critical thinking skills.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 田纳西大学诺克斯维尔分校英语语言学院助理主任苏珊娜·迈耶说，许多学生需要培养批判性思维能力。

paragraphs.text: In other words, students need to learn how to collect information, and then evaluate the information and apply it to a new context .
paragraphs.translation_zh: 换句话说，学生需要学会收集信息，然后评估信息，并把它应用到新的语境中。

paragraphs.text: "We need to get students actively engaged in inquiry , from the beginning levels on, into those levels where they really are close to professional and university contexts."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我们要让学生从入门阶段起就主动参与探究，一直到接近专业和大学语境的阶段。”

paragraphs.text: One way to develop critical thinking skills, Meyer says, is to teach students to be creative about the types of questions that they ask.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 迈耶说，培养批判性思维的一种方法，是教学生在提问的类型上发挥创造力。

paragraphs.text: Benefits of question-asking for English learners
paragraphs.translation_zh: 提问对英语学习者的好处

paragraphs.text: Meyer says that there are many benefits to using question-asking activities with English learners. Question-asking activities help students to become better language learners.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 迈耶说，提问练习对英语学习者益处很多。它能帮助学生成为更好的语言学习者。

paragraphs.text: "Question-asking overall helps students be better language learners. Not for the obvious, because you ask a question you get an answer. Often, questions are the first point of contact. And so if my question is not good, then that's going to direct the level of our conversation."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “总体而言，提问能让学生更善于学语言。原因不只是显而易见的‘有问必有答’。问题往往是交流的第一触点。如果我的问题不好，对话的层次也就上不去。”
