paragraphs.text: Meyer also says that question-asking may help develop critical thinking skills by allowing for more interactions :
paragraphs.translation_zh: 迈耶还说，提问能带来更多互动，从而有助于培养批判性思维：

paragraphs.text: "People who can ask questions are very confident. And when you are confident, you can have more interaction. That extra interaction might lead to critical thinking. Because you are more likely to actually integrate in some fashion into a new setting if you are in an ESL scenario. So, I think overall, just the whole question-asking idea has so many benefits."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “会提问的人很自信。自信了，互动就多。额外的互动可能催生批判性思维。因为如果你身处第二语言环境，互动越多，你就越可能真正融入新环境。所以总的来说，提问这件事好处太多了。”

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 8. THE CROW AND THE PITCHER

paragraphs.text: Once a Crow who was very thirsty found a pitcher with a little water at the bottom which he was unable to reach. He tried to overturn the pitcher but it was too heavy. "Ah! Ah! I know what I'll do," he said. So he gathered up pebbles from the ground, and one after another dropped them into the pitcher until the water gradually reached the top. Then the wise Crow was able to drink all the water he wanted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只非常口渴的乌鸦发现一个罐子,罐底只有一点水,它却怎么也够不着。它想推倒罐子,可罐子太重了。“啊!啊!我有办法了。”它说。于是它从地上衔来一颗颗石子,接连丢进罐里,水位便渐渐升到了罐口。聪明的乌鸦这才喝到了想喝的水。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A very young Fox, who had never before seen a Lion, happened to meet one in the forest. A single look was enough to send the Fox off at top speed for the nearest hiding place.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只从未见过狮子的小狐狸，在森林里偶然遇到了狮子。只看了一眼，小狐狸就吓得拼命逃向最近的藏身处。

paragraphs.text: The second time the Fox saw the Lion he stopped behind a tree to look at him a moment before slinking away. But the third time, the Fox went boldly up to the Lion and, without turning a hair, said, "Hello, there, old top."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二次见到狮子时，小狐狸躲在树后偷偷看了一眼才溜走。但第三次，它大胆地走到狮子面前，面不改色地说：“嘿，老兄，你好啊。”

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 14. THE CONCEITED GRASSHOPPER

paragraphs.text: One day a very young Grasshopper and an old Rooster met out in a field. "I can jump higher than anybody," chirped the Grasshopper. "All right; let me see you do it," said the Rooster, at the same time opening his mouth wide as if he meant to yawn. "Here I go, then," cried the Grasshopper. He jumped so high he landed right in the mouth of the Rooster, who gulped him down. That was the end of the boasting Grasshopper.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天，一只小蚱蜢和一只老公鸡在田野相遇。“我跳得比谁都高，”蚱蜢唧唧叫道。“好啊，让我看看，”公鸡说着，张大嘴巴像要打哈欠。“那我跳了！”蚱蜢大喊。它跳得太高，直接落进公鸡嘴里，被一口吞下。爱吹牛的蚱蜢就此完蛋。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A Wolf had stolen a Lamb and was carrying it off to his lair to eat it. But his plans were very much changed when he met a Lion, who, without making any excuses, took the Lamb away from him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼偷了一只羊羔，正叼回巢穴准备吃掉。但当他遇到一头狮子时，计划彻底改变——狮子二话不说，直接夺走了羊羔。

paragraphs.text: The Lion looked back, but as the Wolf was too far away to be taught a lesson without too much inconvenience, he said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狮子回头看了看，但狼已跑得太远，不便费事教训它，于是说道：

paragraphs.text: "Your property? Did you buy it, or did the Shepherd make you a gift of it? Pray tell me, how did you get it?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你的财产？是你买的，还是牧羊人赠予你的？请告诉我，你是怎么得到的？”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FISHERMAN AND THE LITTLE FISH

paragraphs.text: A poor Fisherman, who lived on the fish he caught, had bad luck one day and caught nothing but a very small fry. The Fisherman was about to put it in his basket when the little Fish said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个靠捕鱼为生的穷渔夫，某天运气不佳，只捕到一条非常小的鱼。渔夫正要把它放进篮子时，小鱼说：

paragraphs.text: "Please spare me, Mr. Fisherman! I am so small it is not worth while to carry me home. When I am bigger, I shall make you a much better meal."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "渔夫先生，请饶了我吧！我这么小，带回家也不划算。等我长大些，能让你吃得更饱。"

paragraphs.text: "How foolish I should be," he said, "to throw you back. However small you may be, you are better than nothing at all."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "把你放回去，我岂不是太傻了？"他说，"再小也比没有强。"

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE QUACK TOAD

paragraphs.text: An old Toad once informed all his neighbors that he was a learned doctor. In fact he could cure anything. The Fox heard the news and hurried to see the Toad. He looked the Toad over very carefully.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只老蟾蜍向所有邻居宣称自己是个博学的医生,声称什么病都能治。狐狸听到这消息,急忙赶来,把蟾蜍仔仔细细打量了一番。

paragraphs.text: "Mr. Toad," he said, "I've been told that you cure anything! But just take a look at yourself, and then try some of your own medicine. If you can cure yourself of that blotchy skin and that rheumatic gait, someone might believe you. Otherwise, I should advise you to try some other profession."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “蟾蜍先生,”他说,“听说你什么病都能治!可你先看看自己,尝尝你自己开的药吧。要是你能治好自己那满是斑点的皮肤和那一瘸一拐的步子,或许还有人会信你。不然的话,我劝你还是趁早改行吧。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE BOY AND THE FILBERTS

paragraphs.text: A Boy was given permission to put his hand into a pitcher to get some filberts. But he took such a great fistful that he could not draw his hand out again. There he stood, unwilling to give up a single filbert and yet unable to get them all out at once. Vexed and disappointed he began to cry.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个男孩获准把手伸进罐子拿榛子。可他抓了满满一把，手却抽不出来了。他站在那里，既不愿放弃一颗榛子，又无法一次全拿出来。又气又失望，他哭了起来。

paragraphs.text: "My boy," said his mother, "be satisfied with half the nuts you have taken and you will easily get your hand out. Then perhaps you may have some more filberts some other time."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "孩子，"母亲说，"只拿一半榛子，手就能轻松抽出来。改天或许还能再吃到更多榛子。"

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE SHEEP

paragraphs.text: A Wolf had been hurt in a fight with a Bear. He was unable to move and could not satisfy his hunger and thirst. A Sheep passed by near his hiding place, and the Wolf called to him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼在与熊的搏斗中受了伤。他动弹不得，饥渴难耐。一只羊从他藏身处附近经过，狼便叫住了他。

paragraphs.text: "Please fetch me a drink of water," he begged, "that might give me strength enough so I can get me some solid food."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “请给我打点水来喝吧，”他恳求道，“那样我或许就有力气去弄点正经的吃食了。”

paragraphs.text: "Solid food!" said the Sheep. "That means me, I suppose. If I should bring you a drink, it would only serve to wash me down your throat. Don't talk to me about a drink!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “正经的吃食！”羊说，“那说的怕就是我了吧。我要是给你打了水来，也不过是顺着你的喉咙把我自己冲下去罢了。别跟我提什么喝水！”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE CROW AND THE PITCHER

paragraphs.text: In a spell of dry weather, when the Birds could find very little to drink, a thirsty Crow found a pitcher with a little water in it. But the pitcher was high and had a narrow neck, and no matter how he tried, the Crow could not reach the water. The poor thing felt as if he must die of thirst.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在一段干旱的日子里，鸟儿们很难找到水喝，一只口渴的乌鸦发现了一个水罐，里面有一点水。但水罐很高，罐口又窄，无论乌鸦怎么努力，都够不到水。可怜的家伙觉得自己快要渴死了。

paragraphs.text: Then an idea came to him. Picking up some small pebbles, he dropped them into the pitcher one by one. With each pebble the water rose a little higher until at last it was near enough so he could drink.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他灵机一动，捡起几颗小石子，一颗颗投进水罐。每投一颗，水面便升高一点，直到最后水位够近，他终于喝到了水。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FROGS AND THE OX

paragraphs.text: An Ox came down to a reedy pool to drink. As he splashed heavily into the water, he crushed a young Frog into the mud. The old Frog soon missed the little one and asked his brothers and sisters what had become of him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一头牛来到芦苇丛生的水塘边喝水。它笨重地踏入水中时，将一只小青蛙踩进了泥里。老青蛙很快发现小青蛙不见了，便问它的兄弟姐妹们小青蛙出了什么事。

paragraphs.text: "A _great big_ monster," said one of them, "stepped on little brother with one of his huge feet!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “一个巨大的怪物，”其中一个说道，“用它的大脚踩到了小弟弟！”

paragraphs.text: "He could not have been bigger than this," she said. But the little Frogs all declared that the monster was _much, much_ bigger and the old Frog kept puffing herself out more and more until, all at once, she burst.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “它不可能比这更大，”老青蛙说。但小青蛙们都坚持说怪物要大得多，老青蛙便不停地把自己鼓得越来越大，直到突然之间，它胀破了。

paragraphs.text: All states, all powers, that have held and hold rule over men have been and are either republics or principalities.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 所有统治过或正在统治人类的国家和政权，要么是共和国，要么是君主国。

paragraphs.text: The new are either entirely new, as was Milan to Francesco Sforza, or they are, as it were, members annexed to the hereditary state of the prince who has acquired them, as was the kingdom of Naples to that of the King of Spain.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 新的君主国,要么是全新的,例如米兰之于弗朗切斯科·斯福尔扎;要么像是被并入征服者原有世袭领地的新成员,例如那不勒斯王国之于西班牙国王的领地。

paragraphs.text: Such dominions thus acquired are either accustomed to live under a prince, or to live in freedom; and are acquired either by the arms of the prince himself, or of others, or else by fortune or by ability.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这样得来的领地,要么本就习惯于在君主统治下生活,要么习惯于自由自在;而获取它们,要么靠君主自己或他人的武力,要么凭机运或才能。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE CAT AND THE BIRDS

paragraphs.text: A Cat was growing very thin. As you have guessed, he did not get enough to eat. One day he heard that some Birds in the neighborhood were ailing and needed a doctor. So he put on a pair of spectacles, and with a leather box in his hand, knocked at the door of the Bird's home.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只猫日渐消瘦。你猜对了，他吃不饱。一天他听说附近有些鸟生病了需要医生。于是他戴上眼镜，拎着皮箱，敲响了鸟儿的家门。

paragraphs.text: The Birds peeped out, and Dr. Cat, with much solicitude, asked how they were. He would be very happy to give them some medicine.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 鸟儿们探头张望，猫医生关切地问候他们，表示很乐意给他们开药。

paragraphs.text: "Tweet, tweet," laughed the Birds. "Very smart, aren't you? We are very well, thank you, and more so, if _you_ only keep away from here."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "啾啾，"鸟儿们笑道，"真聪明，不是吗？我们很好，谢谢，而且只要你离远点，我们会更好。"

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FARMER AND THE STORK

paragraphs.text: A Stork of a very simple and trusting nature had been asked by a gay party of Cranes to visit a field that had been newly planted. But the party ended dismally with all the birds entangled in the meshes of the Farmer's net.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只单纯轻信的鹳被一群欢快的鹤邀请去一片新播种的田地。但结局很惨，所有鸟都被农夫网住了。

paragraphs.text: "Please let me go," he pleaded. "I belong to the Stork family who you know are honest and birds of good character. Besides, I did not know the Cranes were going to steal."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “请放了我吧，”他恳求道，“我属于鹳鸟家族，您知道鹳鸟是诚实、品行端正的鸟。而且，我并不知道鹤要去偷东西。”

paragraphs.text: "You may be a very good bird," answered the Farmer, "but I caught you with the thieving Cranes and you will have to share the same punishment with them."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你也许是一只很好的鸟，”农夫回答说，“但我抓住你时，你正和偷窃的鹤在一起，你必须和他们受到同样的惩罚。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLVES AND THE SHEEP

paragraphs.text: A pack of Wolves lurked near the Sheep pasture. But the Dogs kept them all at a respectful distance, and the Sheep grazed in perfect safety. But now the Wolves thought of a plan to trick the Sheep.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一群狼潜伏在羊群的牧场附近。但牧羊犬让它们始终不敢靠近，羊群得以安然吃草。然而，狼群想出了一条欺骗羊群的计策。

paragraphs.text: "Why is there always this hostility between us?" they said. "If it were not for those Dogs who are always stirring up trouble, I am sure we should get along beautifully. Send them away and you will see what good friends we shall become."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “为什么我们之间总是充满敌意？”它们说，“如果不是那些总是惹是生非的狗，我相信我们一定能和睦相处。把狗赶走，你们就会看到我们会成为多么好的朋友。”

paragraphs.text: The Sheep were easily fooled. They persuaded the Dogs to go away, and that very evening the Wolves had the grandest feast of their lives.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 羊群轻易地被骗了。它们说服牧羊犬离开，就在那天晚上，狼群享受了一生中最丰盛的盛宴。

entries.title: The Yellow Fairy Book: THE DOG AND THE SHADOW

paragraphs.text: It happened that a Dog had got a piece of meat and was carrying it home in his mouth to eat it in peace. Now on his way home he had to cross a plank lying across a running brook. As he crossed, he looked down and saw his own shadow reflected in the water beneath. Thinking it was another dog with another piece of meat, he made up his mind to have that also. So he made a snap at the shadow in the water, but as he opened his mouth the piece of meat fell out, dropped into the water and was never seen more.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狗得到了一块肉，叼在嘴里想带回家安静地吃。回家路上，它必须经过一块横跨溪流的木板。过桥时，它低头看到水中自己的倒影，以为那是另一只狗叼着另一块肉，便决定也要得到那块肉。于是它朝水中的影子猛咬一口，但一张嘴，肉掉进水里，再也找不到了。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: ONE OF AESOP'S FABLES

paragraphs.text: One day the Mice met to talk about the great harm that she was doing them. Each one told of some plan by which to keep out of her way.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天,老鼠们聚在一起,商讨那只猫给它们带来的大祸。每只老鼠都说出一个躲开它的法子。

paragraphs.text: "Do as I say," said an old gray Mouse that was thought to be very wise. "Do as I say. Hang a bell to the Cat's neck. Then, when we hear it ring, we shall know that she is coming, and can scamper out of her way." "Good! good!" said all the other Mice; and one ran to get the bell. "Now which of you will hang this bell on the Cat's neck?" said the old gray Mouse.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “照我说的做，”一只被认为很聪明的老灰鼠说。“照我说的做。给猫脖子上挂个铃铛。这样，当我们听到铃声，就知道它来了，可以赶紧跑开。”“好！好！”其他老鼠都说；一只老鼠跑去拿铃铛。“现在，你们谁去把这个铃铛挂到猫脖子上？”老灰鼠说。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE GOATHERD AND THE WILD GOATS

paragraphs.text: One cold stormy day a Goatherd drove his Goats for shelter into a cave, where a number of Wild Goats had also found their way. The Shepherd wanted to make the Wild Goats part of his flock; so he fed them well. But to his own flock, he gave only just enough food to keep them alive. When the weather cleared, and the Shepherd led the Goats out to feed, the Wild Goats scampered off to the hills.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个寒冷的暴风雨天，牧羊人赶着山羊进山洞避雨，洞里已有几只野山羊。牧羊人想让野山羊归入自己的羊群，于是把它们喂得饱饱的，却只给自家的羊群仅够活命的食物。天气转晴后，牧羊人领着羊群出去吃草，野山羊却撒腿逃回了山里。

paragraphs.text: "Do not expect us to join your flock," replied one of the Wild Goats. "We know how you would treat us later on, if some strangers should come as we did."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “别指望我们加入你的羊群，”一只野山羊回答，“我们知道，如果以后有陌生山羊像我们一样到来，你会怎样对待我们。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE LEAP AT RHODES

paragraphs.text: A certain man who visited foreign lands could talk of little when he returned to his home except the wonderful adventures he had met with and the great deeds he had done abroad.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 某人游历异国后回到家乡，除了讲述自己遇到的奇遇和在外做出的伟大事迹外，几乎不谈其他事情。

paragraphs.text: One of the feats he told about was a leap he had made in a city Called Rhodes. That leap was so great, he said, that no other man could leap anywhere near the distance. A great many persons in Rhodes had seen him do it and would prove that what he told was true.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他吹嘘的壮举之一，是自己在一座叫罗德斯的城里完成的一次跳跃。他说那一跳极远，没有别人能跳出接近的距离。罗德斯城里有许多人都亲眼见过，能证明他所言非虚。

paragraphs.text: "No need of witnesses," said one of the hearers. "Suppose this city is Rhodes. Now show us how far you can jump."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “用不着什么证人，”一位听众说，“就当这座城是罗德斯吧，现在让我们瞧瞧你能跳多远。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE MAN AND THE LION

paragraphs.text: A Lion and a Man chanced to travel in company through the forest. They soon began to quarrel, for each of them boasted that he and his kind were far superior to the other both in strength and mind.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一头狮子和一个人偶然结伴穿越森林。他们很快争吵起来，因为双方都吹嘘自己和同类在力量和智慧上远胜对方。

paragraphs.text: Now they reached a clearing in the forest and there stood a statue. It was a representation of Heracles in the act of tearing the jaws of the Nemean Lion.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们来到林间空地，那里矗立着一尊雕像，刻画的是赫拉克勒斯正在撕开涅墨亚雄狮血盆大口的场景。

paragraphs.text: "See," said the man, "that's how strong _we_ are! The King of Beasts is like wax in our hands!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看啊，”那人说道，“我们就是这么强大！万兽之王在我们手里如同一团软蜡，任人揉捏！”

paragraphs.text: "Ho!" laughed the Lion, "a Man made that statue. It would have been quite a different scene had a Lion made it!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: "哈！"狮子笑道，"那是人雕刻的雕像。要是让狮子来雕，画面可就大不相同了！"

entries.title: The Beacon Second Reader: THE WOLF IN SHEEP'S CLOTHING

paragraphs.text: A Wolf wrapped himself in the skin of a Sheep and by that means got admission into a sheep-fold, where he devoured several of the young Lambs. The Shepherd, however, soon found him out and hung him up to a tree, still in his disguise.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狼披上羊皮混进羊圈，吞食了几只小羊羔。牧羊人很快识破，把它吊在树上，仍披着那身伪装。

paragraphs.text: Some other Shepherds, passing that way, thought it was a Sheep hanging, and cried to their friend, "What, brother! is that the way you serve Sheep in this part of the country?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 路过的牧羊人以为是吊着的羊，对同伴喊道："兄弟，你们这儿就是这样对待羊的吗？"

paragraphs.text: "No, friends," cried he, turning the hanging body around so that they might see what it was; "but it is the way to serve Wolves, even though they be dressed in Sheep's clothing."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "不，朋友们，"他转动吊着的尸体让大家看清，"这是对付狼的办法，哪怕它们披着羊皮。"

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE TWO POTS

paragraphs.text: Two Pots, one of brass and the other of clay, stood together on the hearthstone. One day the Brass Pot proposed to the Earthen Pot that they go out into the world together. But the Earthen Pot excused himself, saying that it would be wiser for him to stay in the corner by the fire.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 炉边并立着两只罐子，一铜一陶。某日铜罐邀陶罐同游世界，陶罐婉拒道："我还是待在炉火旁的角落里更明智。"

paragraphs.text: "It would take so little to break me," he said. "You know how fragile I am. The least shock is sure to shatter me!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我脆弱得不堪一击，”他说，“你知道我有多易碎。最轻微的冲击都能让我粉身碎骨！”

paragraphs.text: "Don't let that keep you at home," urged the Brass Pot. "I shall take very good care of you. If we should happen to meet anything hard I will step between and save you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “别因此就待在家里,”铜罐劝道,“我会好好照顾你。万一遇上什么硬东西,我会挡在你我中间护着你。”

paragraphs.text: So the Earthen Pot at last consented, and the two set out side by side, jolting along on three stubby legs first to this side, then to that, and bumping into each other at every step.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 陶罐终于答应了,两人便并肩上路,用三条粗短的腿摇摇晃晃地走着,时而歪向这边,时而歪向那边,每走一步都互相磕碰。

paragraphs.text: The Earthen Pot could not survive that sort of companionship very long. They had not gone ten paces before the Earthen Pot cracked, and at the next jolt he flew into a thousand pieces.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 陶罐无法长久忍受这种陪伴。还没走出十步，陶罐就裂开了，下一次颠簸时便碎成了千百片。

entries.title: The Science of Human Nature: TO THE STUDENT

paragraphs.text: You are beginning the study of human nature. You can not study human nature from a book, you must study yourself and your neighbors. This book may help you to know what to look for and to understand what you find, but it can do little more than this. It is true, this text gives you many facts learned by psychologists, but you must verify the statements, or at least see their significance to _you_, or they will be of no worth to you. However, the facts considered here, properly understood and assimilated, ought to prove of great value to you. But perhaps of greater value will be the psychological frame of mind or attitude which you should acquire. The psychological attitude is that of seeking to find and understand the _causes of human action, and the causes, consequences, and significance of the processes of the human mind_. If your first course in psychology teaches you to look for these things, gives you some skill in finding them and in using the knowledge after you have it, your study should be quite worth while.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 你正在开始研究人性。你无法从书本中研究人性，必须研究自己和身边的人。这本书或许能帮你了解该寻找什么，并理解你所发现的东西，但它的作用也仅限于此。诚然，这本教材提供了许多心理学家发现的事实，但你必须验证这些论断，或至少看到它们对你的意义，否则它们对你毫无价值。然而，这里讨论的事实，如果正确理解并吸收，应该会对你大有裨益。但或许更有价值的是你应该获得的心理学思维模式或态度。心理学态度是寻求发现和理解人类行为的原因，以及人类心智过程的原因、后果和意义。如果你在心理学入门课程中学到了寻找这些事物的方法，掌握了一些发现它们以及运用知识的技巧，那么你的学习就相当值得了。

entries.title: The Yellow Fairy Book: By Sir George Webbe Dasent

paragraphs.text: One day the Bear met the Fox, who came slinking along with a string of fish he had stolen.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，熊遇到了狐狸，狐狸正叼着一串偷来的鱼悄悄溜过。

paragraphs.text: So the Bear had a mind to learn to fish too, and bade the Fox tell him how he was to set about it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是熊也想学钓鱼，便让狐狸告诉他该怎么做。

paragraphs.text: "Oh! It's an easy craft for you", answered the Fox, "and soon learned. You've only got to go upon the ice, and cut a hole and stick your tail down into it; and so you must go on holding it there as long as you can. You're not to mind if your tail smarts a little; that's when the fish bite. The longer you hold it there the more fish you'll get; and then all at once out with it, with a cross pull sideways, and with a strong pull too."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦！这对你来说很容易，”狐狸答道，“一学就会。你只需走到冰上，凿个洞，把尾巴伸进去；然后尽量长时间地保持不动。如果尾巴有点刺痛，别在意——那是鱼在咬钩。你坚持得越久，钓到的鱼就越多；然后猛地一拽，要横着使劲拉，还得用大力气。”

paragraphs.text: Yes; the Bear did as the Fox had said, and held his tail a long, long time down in the hole, till it was fast frozen in. Then he pulled it out with a cross pull, and it snapped short off. That's why Bruin goes about with a stumpy tail this very day.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 熊照狐狸说的做了，把尾巴在冰窟窿里浸了很久很久，直到冻得死死的。然后他横着用力一拽，尾巴啪地一声从根上断了。这就是为什么直到今天，狗熊还拖着一条秃尾巴到处走。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 20. THE MONKEY AND THE CATS

paragraphs.text: Two Cats who had stolen a large piece of cheese were quarreling over dividing it. At last they decided to refer the matter to a Monkey, who took a pair of scales and, breaking the cheese into two pieces, placed a piece in each scale. "Let me see," he said, taking out the heavier piece, "this piece weighs more than the other." Then he bit off quite a piece and put it back on the scale, and, of course, it was lighter than the other piece. So he took a mouthful from that side, and continued taking from first one side and then the other, until the Cats cried, "Hold! Hold! Give us the two pieces and we will be satisfied." "Not so fast," replied the Monkey, "justice must be given," and he continued to nibble one piece after another. The Cats saw their cheese was almost gone and begged for what was left. "No, no, my friends," said the Monkey, "what remains belongs to me for my pay!" So he crammed the rest into his mouth and munched it in hearty enjoyment as he solemnly dismissed the court.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 两只偷了一大块奶酪的猫为分赃争吵不休。最后它们决定请猴子裁决。猴子拿出天平，将奶酪掰成两块各放一端。"让我看看，"他取出较重的那块说，"这块比另一块重。"随即咬下一大块放回盘中，结果这块变轻了。于是他又从另一边咬一口，如此反复交替。直到猫儿们喊道："停！停！把两块给我们吧，我们认了。""别急，"猴子答道，"必须公正裁决。"他继续啃咬。猫儿们眼看奶酪快没了，哀求留下残渣。"不行不行，朋友们，"猴子说，"剩下的归我当酬劳！"说罢将残渣塞进嘴里大嚼，庄严地宣布退庭。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE BUNDLE OF STICKS

paragraphs.text: A certain Father had a family of Sons, who were forever quarreling among themselves. No words he could say did the least good, so he cast about in his mind for some very striking example that should make them see that discord would lead them to misfortune.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 某位父亲有一群儿子，他们总是争吵不休。任何劝诫都无济于事，于是他绞尽脑汁想找一个能让他们明白不和会导致不幸的生动例子。

paragraphs.text: One day when the quarreling had been much more violent than usual and each of the Sons was moping in a surly manner, he asked one of them to bring him a bundle of sticks. Then handing the bundle to each of his Sons in turn he told them to try to break it. But although each one tried his best, none was able to do so.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天，当争吵比往常更激烈，每个儿子都闷闷不乐时，他让一个儿子拿来一捆树枝。他依次把树枝递给每个儿子，让他们试着折断。尽管每个人都竭尽全力，却无人能折断。

paragraphs.text: The Father then untied the bundle and gave the sticks to his Sons to break one by one. This they did very easily.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 父亲随后解开绳子，把树枝分给儿子们一根根折断。这次他们轻而易举就做到了。

paragraphs.text: "My Sons," said the Father, "do you not see how certain it is that if you agree with each other and help each other, it will be impossible for your enemies to injure you? But if you are divided among yourselves, you will be no stronger than a single stick in that bundle."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “孩子们，”父亲说，“你们难道没看到吗？如果你们彼此和睦、互相帮助，敌人就绝不可能伤害你们。但如果你们内部不和，你们就会像那捆柴中的一根树枝一样脆弱。”

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 15. THE BLIND MEN AND THE ELEPHANT

paragraphs.text: Six blind beggars sitting by a roadside as an Elephant passed were told that they might touch it so that they would know what an Elephant was like. The first one touched only the Elephant's side and said, "He is like a wall!" The second one felt only his tusk and said, "No, no, he is like a spear." The third took hold of his trunk and said, "He is surely like a snake." "No such thing," cried the fourth, grasping one of his legs, "he is like a tree." The fifth was a tall man and took hold of his ear, and said, "All of you are wrong, he is like a big fan." The sixth man happened to catch hold of his tail, and cried, "O foolish fellows, he is not like a wall, nor a spear, nor a snake, nor a tree, nor a fan; he is exactly like a rope." So the Elephant passed on while the six blind men stood there quarreling, each being sure he knew exactly how the Elephant looked, and each calling the others hard names because the rest did not agree with him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 六名盲丐坐在路边，一头大象经过时，他们被告知可以触摸大象以了解其模样。第一个只摸到大象的侧身，说：“它像一堵墙！”第二个只摸到象牙，说：“不对不对，它像一根长矛。”第三个抓住象鼻，说：“它分明像一条蛇。”“胡说，”第四个抓住一条象腿喊道，“它像一棵树。”第五个身材高大，抓住象耳说：“你们都错了，它像一把大扇子。”第六个恰好抓住象尾，叫道：“蠢货们！它既不像墙、矛、蛇、树，也不像扇子；它完全像一根绳子。”大象继续前行，而六名盲人却站在那里争吵不休，每个人都坚信自己知道大象的模样，并因他人不赞同而互相辱骂。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE ASTROLOGER

paragraphs.text: A man who lived a long time ago believed that he could read the future in the stars. He called himself an Astrologer, and spent his time at night gazing at the sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 古时有一人，自认能从星辰中预见未来，自称占星家，每夜仰望星空。

paragraphs.text: One evening he was walking along the open road outside the village. His eyes were fixed on the stars. He thought he saw there that the end of the world was at hand, when all at once, down he went into a hole full of mud and water.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一晚，他行于村外旷野，双目紧盯星辰，忽见天象预示世界末日将至，不料一脚踏空，跌入泥水坑中。

paragraphs.text: There he stood up to his ears, in the muddy water, and madly clawing at the slippery sides of the hole in his effort to climb out.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他陷在齐耳的泥水里，疯狂抓挠滑溜的坑壁，拼命想爬出来。

paragraphs.text: His cries for help soon brought the villagers running. As they pulled him out of the mud, one of them said:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 呼救声引来村民，众人将他拉出泥潭时，一人说道：

paragraphs.text: "You pretend to read the future in the stars, and yet you fail to see what is at your feet! This may teach you to pay more attention to what is right in front of you, and let the future take care of itself."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你妄称从星辰预知未来，却连脚下之物都看不见！这该教你多留意眼前之事，未来自有其道。”

paragraphs.text: "What use is it," said another, "to read the stars, when you can't see what's right here on the earth?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 另一人接道：“连地上之物都看不清，观星又有何用？”

entries.title: The Pig Brother, and Other Fables and Stories: A FORTUNE

paragraphs.text: One day a man was walking along the street, and he was sad at heart. Business was dull; he had set his desire upon a horse that cost a thousand dollars, and he had only eight hundred to buy it with. There were other things, to be sure, that might be bought with eight hundred dollars, but he did not want those; so he was sorrowful, and thought the world a bad place.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一日，一人郁郁寡欢行于街头。生意萧条，他渴望一匹价值千元的马，却仅有八百元。固然八百元可购他物，但他心有不甘，遂觉世道不公。

paragraphs.text: As he walked, he saw a child running toward him; it was a strange child, but when he looked at it, its face lightened like sunshine, and broke into smiles. The child held out its closed hand.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 正行间，见一陌生孩童迎面跑来，面若朝阳，笑靥如花，紧握的小手向他伸来。

paragraphs.text: "Look!" it said; and the street rang with its happy laughter. The man looked, and in the child's hand lay a penny.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看！”它说，街上回荡着它快乐的笑声。男人看去，孩子手里躺着一枚便士。

paragraphs.text: Then they parted, and the child went and bought a stick of candy, and saw all the world red and white in stripes.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 随后两人分了手，孩子去买了一根条纹糖棒，眼里的整个世界都成了红白相间的条纹。

paragraphs.text: The man went and put his eight hundred dollars in the savings-bank, all but fifty cents, and with the fifty cents he bought a hobby-horse for his own little boy, and the little boy saw all the world brown, with white spots.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 男人去把八百美元存进储蓄银行，只留了五十美分，用这五十美分给自己的小儿子买了一匹木马，小儿子看到整个世界都是棕色带白点的。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 22. THE LARKS IN THE WHEAT-FIELD

paragraphs.text: Once a Lark and her little ones lived together in a nest in a field of ripened wheat. The mother bird was afraid the reapers might come before the young larks could fly. So every morning when she went for food she told them to listen carefully to all they heard and tell her when she returned. On the first evening they said, "We heard the farmer tell his son to ask the neighbors to help reap the wheat." "Oh, no danger yet," said Mother Lark. The next evening they said, "We heard the farmer tell his son to ask his uncle and cousins to help reap the wheat." "Oh, no danger yet!" said the mother. On the third evening they said, "To-day we heard the farmer say to his son, 'To-morrow we will reap the wheat ourselves!'" "Then," cried the mother, "we must fly away at once, for the wheat is sure to be cut now. When a man makes up his mind to do a thing himself, it is more likely to be done." She took her young ones away at once, and the next day the wheat was reaped by the farmer and his son.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，一只云雀和她的孩子们住在一片成熟麦田里的窝中。鸟妈妈担心收割的人会赶在小云雀会飞之前就来。所以每天早上出去觅食时，她都叮嘱孩子们仔细听清周围的动静，等她回来转告她。第一天晚上，它们说：“我们听到农夫叫儿子去请邻居来帮忙割麦子。”云雀妈妈说：“哦，还不要紧。”第二天晚上，它们说：“我们听到农夫叫儿子去请叔叔和堂兄弟们来帮忙割麦子。”妈妈说：“哦，还不要紧！”第三天晚上，它们说：“今天我们听到农夫对儿子说：‘明天咱们自己动手割麦子！’”妈妈便喊道：“那我们得马上飞走，因为这下麦子肯定要割了。一个人一旦下定决心亲自动手，事情就十有八九要办成。”她当即带着孩子们飞走了，第二天，农夫和儿子果然亲自把麦子收割了。

entries.title: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: DICK WHITTINGTON--III

paragraphs.text: Dick's cat was taken across the ocean. The ship sailed and sailed, until at last it came to a distant country.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 迪克的猫被带过了大洋。船航行啊航行，终于来到一个遥远的国度。

paragraphs.text: Now the king and queen of this country were very rich. When the captain was asked to show his goods before them he was very glad indeed to do so.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这个国家的国王和王后非常富有。当船长被要求在国王和王后面前展示他的货物时，他非常乐意这样做。

paragraphs.text: "Alas, we cannot help ourselves," said the king. "I would give half my kingdom to be rid of them."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “唉，我们也无能为力，”国王说，“只要能除掉这些老鼠，我愿拿出半个王国。”

paragraphs.text: "We must buy that little animal," said the queen. "I do not care how much she may cost."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我们必须买下那只小动物，”王后说。“我不在乎它要花多少钱。”

paragraphs.text: The captain could hardly carry all the jewels and gold that the king gave him for the cat.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 船长几乎拿不动国王赏赐给猫的所有珠宝和黄金。

paragraphs.text: Then the ship with Dick's money came back to London, and the captain told the story to Mr. Fitzwarren.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 随后，载着迪克钱财的船返回伦敦，船长向菲茨沃伦先生讲述了此事。

paragraphs.text: "We must take these jewels and all this gold at once to Mr. Whittington," said the honest man. "He is no longer a poor boy, for this has made him rich."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我们必须立刻把这些珠宝和黄金送到惠廷顿先生那里，”这位诚实的人说道，“他不再是穷小子了，这些财富已让他变得富有。”

paragraphs.text: "See what your cat has brought you," said Mr. Fitzwarren. "You are now a rich man and may yet be Lord Mayor of London."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看看你的猫给你带来了什么，”菲茨沃伦先生说，“你现在是个富人了，将来还可能成为伦敦市长。”

paragraphs.text: And it is true that after Dick Whittington became a man, he was made Lord Mayor of London.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 确实，迪克·惠廷顿长大后，真的当上了伦敦市长。

entries.title: The Ontario Readers: Fifth Book: LITTLE GOODY TWO SHOES

paragraphs.text: All the world must know that Two Shoes was not her real name. No; her father's name was Meanwell, and he was for many years a well-to-do farmer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 谁都知道“两只鞋”并不是她的真名。不,她父亲姓明威尔,做了多年家境殷实的农夫。

paragraphs.text: While Margery (for that was her real name) was yet a little girl her father became very poor. He was so poor that at last he and Margery's mother and Margery and her little brother were all turned out of doors. They did not have a roof to cover their heads.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 玛格丽（这是她的真名）还小时，父亲变得非常贫穷。最后穷到连他和玛格丽的母亲、玛格丽以及她的小弟弟都被赶出了家门，连遮头的屋顶都没有。

paragraphs.text: Margery's father felt so unhappy that at last he died, and only a few days later Margery's mother died, too. Poor little Margery and her brother were left alone in the wide world.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 玛格丽的父亲伤心欲绝，最终去世了，仅几天后母亲也离世了。可怜的小玛格丽和弟弟孤零零地留在了茫茫世间。

paragraphs.text: Their sorrow would have made you pity them, but it would have done your heart good to see how fond they were of each other. They always went about hand in hand, and when you saw one you were sure to see the other.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们的悲惨遭遇会让你心生怜悯，但看到他们彼此相依为命，又让人由衷欣慰。他们总是手牵手同行，见到一个就必定能看到另一个。

paragraphs.text: They were both very ragged, and though Tommy had two shoes, Margery had but one. They had nothing, poor little things, to live upon but what kind people gave to them. Each night they lay on the hay in just such a barn as you see here.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 两人都衣衫褴褛,汤米有两只鞋,玛格丽却只有一只。可怜的小家伙,除了好心人施舍的东西,没有别的活路。每天夜里,他们就睡在像图中这样的谷仓干草堆上。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 3. THE MAN WHO FIRST SAILED AROUND THE WORLD

paragraphs.text: Columbus said, "Give me money to buy ships, and I will prove that the earth is round by sailing around it." But he never did sail around the earth, after all. Nor did Americus Vespucius. This was left for another sailor, named Fernando Magellan. In 1519, twelve years after the death of Columbus, he started from Spain with a large fleet of ships, hoping to find, through this new land, a way by which he might sail around the world. He sailed directly across the Atlantic Ocean to America, looking up and down the coast for an opening to the other ocean which a sailor one day had seen. Finding no opening, he sailed down to the most southern point of South America, and after sailing around Cape Horn, he came out into the great ocean. When he saw it first it looked smiling and peaceful. So on account of its calm, sunny appearance, he named it the "Pacific," which means "peaceful." Sailing over the Pacific Ocean he came at last to the Indies, to India, and to Spain. Then he knew that he had sailed around the world. So what Columbus had said and believed so earnestly, Fernando Magellan proved at last to be true--the earth is round!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 哥伦布曾说：“给我钱买船，我将通过环航证明地球是圆的。”但他终究未能完成环球航行，亚美利哥·韦斯普奇也没有。这一壮举留给了另一位名叫斐迪南·麦哲伦的航海家。1519年，哥伦布去世十二年后，麦哲伦率庞大舰队从西班牙出发，希望借这片新大陆找到一条环游世界的航道。他径直横渡大西洋抵达美洲，沿着海岸上下搜寻一名水手曾经见过的、通往另一片大洋的出口。遍寻不得，他便南下航行至南美洲最南端，绕过合恩角，驶入了那片辽阔的大洋。初次望见时，洋面安详平静；因其宁静晴朗的模样，他将它命名为“太平洋”，意为“平静之洋”。横渡太平洋后，他最终抵达东印度群岛、印度，又回到西班牙。至此他明白，自己已经环航了地球。哥伦布当年如此真诚地宣称并坚信的事，终于由斐迪南·麦哲伦证实为真——地球是圆的！

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 22. BOONE AND HIS SWING

paragraphs.text: Daniel Boone was an early settler in Kentucky. He knew all about the woods and the ways of the animals and the Indians. Almost all the men that went with him into Kentucky were killed by the wild wolves or the savage Indians. One day when Daniel Boone was left alone in his cabin, four Indians came to kill him. He made his escape over a hill, but the Indians ran after him. He ran as fast as he could till he reached a wild grape-vine, which he saw reached to the top of a high tree, and was long enough to swing over a steep ravine. When he was a boy he had often made a swing of a wild grave-vine like this. So he quickly cut the vine off near the roots, took hold of it and swung out into the air with all his might. He was carried far out as he swung over the ravine. Then he let go, and as soon as he fell to the ground he ran away in a direction in which he knew the Indians could not find him. When the Indians came up to the place they could not find his tracks anywhere. So Daniel Boone was saved by a swing.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 丹尼尔·布恩是肯塔基的早期定居者。他深谙森林、动物习性和印第安人的生活方式。与他同去肯塔基的人几乎都被野狼或凶残的印第安人杀害。一天，当布恩独自留在小木屋时，四名印第安人来杀他。他翻山逃跑，但印第安人紧追不舍。他拼命奔跑，直到看见一根野葡萄藤——它高悬至树顶，长度足以荡过陡峭的峡谷。他幼时常像这样用野葡萄藤做秋千。于是他迅速割断藤根，抓住藤蔓奋力荡向空中。他荡过峡谷时被远远抛向对岸，随即松手落地，朝印第安人无法追踪的方向逃去。当印第安人赶到时，已找不到任何踪迹。丹尼尔·布恩就这样靠秋千得救了。

entries.title: The Pig Brother, and Other Fables and Stories: THE TANGLED SKEIN

paragraphs.text: "Oh dear! oh dear!" said the child. "No one ever had such a dreadful time before, I do believe, and it all comes of trying to be good. Oh dear! Oh dear! I wish I was bad; then I should not have all this trouble."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "天哪！天哪！"孩子说，"我相信从没有人像我这样倒霉，都是因为想做好人。天哪！天哪！我真希望自己是个坏孩子，那样就不会有这么多麻烦了。"

paragraphs.text: "Look!" said the child. "Mother gave me this skein to wind, and I promised to do it. But then father sent me on an errand, and it was almost school-time, and I was studying my lesson and going on the errand and winding the skein, all at the same time, and now I have got all tangled up in the wool, and I cannot walk either forward or back, and oh! dear me, what ever _shall_ I do?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “看！”孩子说，“妈妈给我这团线让我绕，我答应了。但爸爸又派我去跑腿，眼看要上学了，我一边学习、一边跑腿、一边绕线，结果把自己缠在了毛线里，进退不得。哎呀！天哪，我该怎么办？”

paragraphs.text: "Now sit still!" he said, and he began patiently to wind up the skein. It was wofully tangled, and knotted about the child's hands and feet; it was a wonder she could move at all; but at last it was all clear, and the Angel handed her the ball.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “现在坐好！”他说着，开始耐心地绕线。线团乱成一团，缠在孩子的手脚上，她能动弹已是奇迹。最后线终于理清了，天使把线球递给她。

paragraphs.text: "But you were doing them in the wrong way!" said the Angel. "It is good to do an errand, and it is good to go to school, but when you have a skein to wind you must sit still."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “但你做事的方法不对！”天使说，“跑腿和上学都是好事，可要绕线时，就得安安静静坐着。”

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 19. "LITTLE TEN MINUTES"

paragraphs.text: When the English were at war with the Zulus in South Africa, a French prince, named Louis Napoleon, enlisted, and one morning was riding outside the camp with a small company of soldiers. All about them in the open country they saw the Zulus. One of his friends said: "Louis, we had better go back to camp. We are in great danger here. The Zulus may come upon us any minute and kill us." "Oh, no danger," said the Prince, "let us stay here just ten minutes more, and drink our coffee." During that ten minutes the Zulus came upon them, and in the skirmish the Prince lost his life. When the news of his death was telegraphed to his widowed mother in London, England, she said: "That was always Louis' way. When he was a little boy he was never ready on time. He was always saying, 'Just ten minutes more.' Sometimes when I called him in the morning and he was too sleepy to speak he would lift his hands and spread out his ten fingers to show that he wanted ten minutes more. I used to call him 'Little Ten Minutes.' Those ten minutes have lost me my boy, and my boy his life. His fault has become his fate!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 英国与南非祖鲁人交战时，一位名叫路易·拿破仑的法国王子参军了。一天早晨，他带着一小队士兵在营地外骑行，开阔地带到处可见祖鲁人。一位朋友说：“路易，我们最好回营地，这里太危险了，祖鲁人随时可能冲过来杀死我们。”“哦，没危险，”王子说，“再待十分钟，喝完咖啡。”就在这十分钟里，祖鲁人突袭了他们，王子在混战中丧生。噩耗传到伦敦他寡居的母亲那里时，她说：“路易总是这样。他小时候从不准时，总说‘再等十分钟’。有时早上我叫他，他困得说不出话，就举起双手张开十根手指，表示还要十分钟。我常叫他‘小十分钟’。这十分钟让我失去了儿子，也让他丢了性命。他的缺点成了他的命运！”

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 8. GLADSTONE AND THE STREET-SWEEPER

paragraphs.text: The minister of a church in London was called one day to see a street-sweeper in his parish who was ill. Asking him if any one had been to see him, to the surprise of the minister, the sweeper replied, "Yes, Mr. Gladstone came to see me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天,伦敦某教堂的牧师被请去看望教区里一位生病的清道夫。牧师问他可曾有人来看过他,清道夫的回答让牧师吃了一惊:“有的,格莱斯顿先生来看过我。”

paragraphs.text: "Well," answered the boy, "he always had a nice word for me when he passed my crossing, and when I was not there he missed me. He asked my mate, who had taken my place, where I was, and when he heard I was ill he asked for my address, and he put it down on paper. So he called to see me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “是这样,”男孩答道,“他每回经过我扫的那个路口,总要对我说句和气话。我不在的时候,他还惦记着我,问替我的伙伴我上哪儿去了。一听说我病了,他就要了我的地址记在纸上,于是就来看我了。”

paragraphs.text: "He brought me some nice oranges," answered the boy, "and then he read to me some Bible and prayed, and it was so good!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “他给我带了几个好橘子，”男孩回答，“还给我读《圣经》、做祷告，感觉真好！”

paragraphs.text: To a man like Gladstone, living humbly, simply, and sincerely, it is as important and as interesting a deed to do a kindness to a poor street-sweeper, and to comfort his heart with sympathy and love, as to form a cabinet to govern the English Empire. In such service the words of George Herbert have their full realization:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 对格莱斯顿这样生活谦卑、简朴而真诚的人来说,善待一个穷苦的清道夫,用同情和爱去温暖他的心,与组建内阁治理大英帝国同样重要、同样有意义。在这样的奉献中,乔治·赫伯特的诗句得到了充分的印证:

paragraphs.text: A servant with this clause Makes drudgery divine; Who sweeps a room as for Thy laws, Makes that and th' action fine.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 心怀此念的仆人,能化苦役为神圣;为遵奉你的律法而扫地,这扫除与这屋子便都成了美事。

entries.title: The Science of Human Nature: GENERAL DIRECTIONS

paragraphs.text: The results of the mental tests in the school will be worse than useless unless the tests are given with the greatest care and scientific precision. Every test should be most carefully explained to the children so that they will know _exactly_ what they are to do. The matter must be so presented to them that they will put forth _all possible_ effort. They must take the tests seriously. Great care must be taken to see that there is no cheating. The work of each child should be his own work. In those tests in which time is an important element, the time must be _carefully kept_, with a stop watch if one is available. The papers should be distributed for the tests and turned face downward on the pupil's desk. The pupil, when all are ready to begin, should take the paper in his hand and at the signal "begin" turn it over and begin work, and when the signal "stop" is given, should quit work instantly and turn the paper over. Before the work begins, the necessary information should be placed on each paper. This information should be the pupil's name, age, grade, sex, and school. This should be on every paper. When the test is over the papers should be immediately collected.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 学校里的心理测试，若不以最大的谨慎和科学的精确来进行，得出的结果将比毫无用处更糟。每项测试都应向孩子们仔细讲解，让他们确切知道自己该做什么。题目的呈现方式必须能促使他们竭尽全力。他们必须认真对待测试。还须格外留意，杜绝任何作弊。每个孩子都必须独立完成自己的答卷。在那些以时间为重要因素的测试中，必须精确计时，若有秒表便用秒表。试卷分发后应正面朝下放在学生桌上。待大家都准备就绪，学生应把试卷拿在手中，听到“开始”的口令便翻过来作答，听到“停止”的口令则立即停笔，并把试卷翻回背面。作答之前，每张试卷上都应填好必要的信息：学生的姓名、年龄、年级、性别和学校。每张试卷都要填写。测试一结束，就应立即收卷。

entries.title: The Yellow Fairy Book: "SOMEBODY HAS BEEN LYING IN MY BED—AND HERE SHE IS!"

paragraphs.text: The little old woman had heard in her sleep the great, rough, gruff voice of the Great, Huge Bear, but she was so fast asleep that it was no more to her than the moaning of wind or the rumbling of thunder. And she had heard the middle voice of the Middle Bear, but it was only as if she had heard some one speaking in a dream. But when she heard the little, small, wee voice of the Little, Small, Wee Bear, it was so sharp and so shrill that it awakened her at once. Up she started, and when she saw the three bears on one side of the bed she tumbled herself out at the other and ran to the window. Now the window was open, because the Bears, like good, tidy bears as they were, always opened their bedchamber window when they got up in the morning. Out the little old woman jumped, and whether she broke her neck in the fall or ran into the wood and was lost there, or found her way out of the wood and was taken up by the constable and sent to the House of Correction for a vagrant as she was, I cannot tell. But the three Bears never saw anything more of her.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小老太婆在睡梦中听到了大熊粗哑的声音，但她睡得太沉，那声音对她来说不过是风声或雷鸣。她也听到了中熊的声音，但只像梦中有人说话。然而，当她听到小熊细小尖利的声音时，那声音如此刺耳，立刻惊醒了她。她猛地坐起，看到三只熊在床的一侧，便从另一侧滚下床，跑到窗前。窗户是开着的，因为熊们像整洁的熊一样，早上起床后总会打开卧室窗户。小老太婆跳了出去，至于她是摔断了脖子，还是跑进森林迷了路，或是走出森林被警察当作流浪汉送进教养院，我不得而知。但三只熊再也没有见过她。

entries.title: The Yellow Fairy Book: By Ramaswami Raju

paragraphs.text: A fox was once caught in a trap. A hungry Tiger saw him and said, "So you are here!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狐狸曾落入陷阱。一只饥饿的老虎看见它说：“原来你在这儿！”

paragraphs.text: "Why, you were complaining you could not get men to eat, so I got into this net to-day, that you may have the men when they come to take me," said the Fox, and gave a hint that if he would wait a while in a thicket close by he would point out the men to him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哎呀，你一直抱怨吃不到人，所以我今天才钻进这张网里，等人来抓我的时候，你就能逮到他们了。”狐狸说着，还暗示老虎只要在附近的灌木丛里躲上一会儿，它就把那些人指给老虎看。

paragraphs.text: "Why, you were complaining you could not get at the Tiger that has been devouring your cattle; I got into this net to-day that you may have him. As I expected, he came to eat me up, and is in yonder thicket," said the Fox, and gave a hint that if they would take him out of the trap he would point out the Tiger.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哎呀，你们一直抱怨抓不到那只吃掉你们家畜的老虎；我今天钻进网里，正是好让你们逮住它。果然，它来吃我，这会儿就在那边的灌木丛里。”狐狸说着，还暗示只要人们把它从陷阱里放出来，它就把老虎指给他们看。

paragraphs.text: "Certainly," said the Fox, while the men went with him in a circle to see that he did not escape.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “当然。”狐狸说。众人便押着它绕行一圈，看住它不让它逃跑。

paragraphs.text: Then the Fox said to the Tiger and the men, "Sir Tiger, here are the men; gentlemen, here is the Tiger."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后狐狸对老虎和人们说：“老虎先生，这些人在这里；先生们，老虎在这里。”

paragraphs.text: The men left the Fox and turned to the Tiger. The former beat a hasty retreat to the wood, saying, "I have kept my promise to both; now you may settle it between yourselves."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 众人放开狐狸，转向老虎。狐狸则匆匆逃回林中，说道：“我对双方都守了信用；现在你们自己了断吧。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FARMER AND THE CRANES

paragraphs.text: Some Cranes saw a farmer plowing a large field. When the work of plowing was done, they patiently watched him sow the seed. It was their feast, they thought.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 几只鹤看见农夫在耕一大片田。耕完地后，它们耐心地看他播种。它们心想，这是它们的盛宴。

paragraphs.text: So, as soon as the Farmer had finished planting and had gone home, down they flew to the field, and began to eat as fast as they could.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是，农夫一播完种回家，它们就飞下田里，开始拼命地吃。

paragraphs.text: The Farmer, of course, knew the Cranes and their ways. He had had experience with such birds before. He soon returned to the field with a sling. But he did not bring any stones with him. He expected to scare the Cranes just by swinging the sling in the air, and shouting loudly at them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 农夫当然了解鹤的习性。他以前就领教过这种鸟。他很快带着弹弓回到田里，但没带石子。他只想空甩弹弓并大声吆喝，吓走它们。

paragraphs.text: At first the Cranes flew away in great terror. But they soon began to see that none of them ever got hurt. They did not even hear the noise of stones whizzing through the air, and as for words, they would kill nobody. At last they paid no attention whatever to the Farmer.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 起初鹤惊恐地飞走了。但很快它们发现没有一只受伤，甚至听不到石子破空声，而喊话又伤不了谁。最后它们对农夫完全不理睬了。

paragraphs.text: The Farmer saw that he would have to take other measures. He wanted to save at least some of his grain. So he loaded his sling with stones and killed several of the Cranes. This had the effect the Farmer wanted, for from that day the Cranes visited his field no more.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 农夫明白必须另想办法。他想至少保住部分谷物。于是他在弹弓里装上石子，打死了几只鹤。这招奏效了，从此鹤再也不来他的田里。

entries.title: The Pig Brother, and Other Fables and Stories: THE SHADOW

paragraphs.text: An Angel heard a child crying one day, and came to see what ailed it. He found the little one sitting on the ground, with the sun at its back (for the day was young), looking at its own shadow, which lay on the ground before it, and weeping bitterly.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天，一位天使听到一个孩子在哭泣，便过来看看他怎么了。他发现小家伙坐在地上，背对着太阳（因为天刚亮），看着自己投在面前的影子，伤心地哭着。

paragraphs.text: "The world is so dark!" said the child. "See, it is all dusky gray, and there is no beauty in it. Why must I stay in this sad, gray world?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: "世界太黑暗了！"孩子说。"看，一切都是灰蒙蒙的，毫无美感。为什么我必须留在这个悲伤的灰色世界里？"

paragraphs.text: "Do you not hear the birds singing, and the other children calling at their play?" asked the Angel.
paragraphs.translation_zh: "你没听到鸟儿歌唱，其他孩子在玩耍时呼喊吗？"天使问道。

paragraphs.text: "Yes," said the child; "I hear them, but I do not know where they are. I cannot see them, I see only the shadow. Moreover, if they saw it, they would not sing and call, but would weep as I do."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "听到了，"孩子说，"但我不知道他们在哪里。我看不见他们，只能看见影子。而且，如果他们看到这影子，就不会唱歌呼喊，而是像我一样哭泣了。"

paragraphs.text: The Angel lifted the child, and set it on its feet, with its face to the early sun.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 天使把孩子抱起来，让他站好，脸朝向初升的太阳。

paragraphs.text: The child brushed away the tears from its eyes and looked. Before them lay the fields all green and gold, shining with dewdrops, and the other children were running to and fro, laughing and shouting, and crowning one another with blossoms.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 孩子擦去眼里的泪水,抬眼望去。眼前是一片碧绿金黄的田野,沾着露珠闪闪发亮,别的孩子跑来跑去,又笑又叫,还互相把鲜花戴到对方头上。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, no!" said the Angel; "the shadow is behind you, where it belongs. Run, now, and gather flowers for the littlest one, who sits in the grass there!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: "哦，不！"天使说，"影子在你身后，它本该在那里。快跑去为坐在草地上的那个小家伙采花吧！"

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 11. THE GOOD NEIGHBOR

paragraphs.text: One day as Jesus, the great Teacher, was speaking, a lawyer, who really wanted to know, said, "Who is my neighbor?" and Jesus told him this beautiful story:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天，伟大的导师耶稣正在讲道，一位真心求问的律法师说：“谁是我的邻舍呢？”耶稣便讲了这样一个美好的故事：

paragraphs.text: Once a man was journeying over a rough and lonely road. A band of robbers sprang upon him, struck him down, stole his money and clothes and left him bleeding and half dead on the road. A temple-priest happened to pass that way, and when he saw the wounded man lying there he said to himself, "No one can see what I do on this lonely road." So he crossed over to the other side, looked at the beautiful scenery, and passed on to the temple to prayer and sacrifice, leaving the poor man uncared for, dying in the road.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一个人正走在一条崎岖荒凉的路上。一伙强盗突然扑向他，把他打倒在地，抢走了他的钱财和衣服，将他打得半死丢在路边。一位圣殿祭司恰好路过，看见受伤的人躺在那里，心里暗想：“这条僻静路上没人看见我的所作所为。”于是他从路对面绕过去，欣赏着沿途美景，径直前往圣殿祷告献祭，任由那可怜人无人照料、奄奄一息地躺在路上。

paragraphs.text: A temple-singer also passed that way. When he saw the wounded man lying there he went up to him, looked carefully at his sad condition, and said to himself, "Poor man, I would help him if I were not so busy." So he passed on to his singing in the temple, leaving the poor man uncared for, dying in the road.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位圣殿歌者也经过那里。他看见受伤的人躺在路边，便走上前去仔细察看他的惨状，心里想：“可怜的人啊，要不是我太忙，真想帮他一把。”于是他继续赶路去圣殿献唱，任由那可怜人无人照料、奄奄一息地躺在路上。

paragraphs.text: Shortly afterward a Samaritan, a man who was not a Jew, came along the road. He saw the wounded man bleeding and dying, uncared for in the road, and felt sorry for him. He saw that he was a Jew and not one of his own people, but that made no difference. He went up to him, raised the suffering man, and gently poured soothing oil into his wounds, and gave him strengthening wine. Then he helped him upon his mule and walked by its side, while the man rode until they came to a small hotel, where he spent the night taking good care of him. The next morning he said to the hotel-keeper: "Take care of him until he is well. Here is some money, and if you spend more I will repay you when I come this way again."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久后，一个撒玛利亚人——并非犹太人——沿着这条路走来。他看见受伤的人血流不止、奄奄一息地躺在路边无人照料，便动了恻隐之心。虽然看出对方是犹太人而非本族人，但这并未影响他。他走上前去，扶起受苦的人，轻轻将舒缓的油倒在他的伤口上，又给他喝下提神的酒。然后扶他骑上自己的骡子，自己步行在旁陪伴，直到来到一家小旅店。他整夜悉心照料那人。次日清晨，他对店主说：“请照料他直到痊愈。这些钱先给你，若不够用，我下次路过时再补还。”

paragraphs.text: Jesus said to the lawyer, "Which of these three was a neighbor to the wounded man?" The lawyer said, "The man that showed mercy on him!" Jesus said: "Go, and do you be as good a neighbor to all whom you have the power to aid and to help."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 耶稣问律法师：“这三个人中，谁是那受伤之人的邻舍呢？”律法师回答：“是那个向他施怜悯的人！”耶稣说：“去吧，你也要这样，向所有你有能力帮助的人成为好邻舍。”

entries.title: The Beacon Second Reader: THE LARK AND HER YOUNG ONES

paragraphs.text: A Lark, who had Young Ones in a field of grain which was almost ripe, was afraid that the reapers would come before her young brood were fledged. So every day when she flew off to look for food, she charged them to take note of what they heard in her absence and to tell her of it when she came home.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只云雀在即将成熟的麦田里哺育幼雏，她担心收割者会在小鸟羽翼丰满之前到来。因此每天外出觅食时，她都叮嘱孩子们留心听周围动静，回家后要告诉她听到的一切。

paragraphs.text: One day when she was gone, they heard the owner of the field say to his son that the grain seemed ripe enough to be cut, and tell him to go early the next day and ask their friends and neighbors to come and help reap it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天她外出时，孩子们听见田主对儿子说麦子已经熟透可以收割了，让他明天一早就去请朋友和邻居来帮忙收割。

paragraphs.text: When the old Lark came home, the Little Ones quivered and chirped round her and told her what had happened, begging her to take them away as fast as she could. The mother bade them be easy; "for," said she, "if he depends on his friends and his neighbors, I am sure the grain will not be reaped tomorrow."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 老云雀回家后，小鸟们颤抖着围在她身边叽叽喳喳报告了这件事，恳求她尽快带它们离开。母亲让它们放心：“因为，”她说，“如果他指望朋友和邻居帮忙，我敢肯定明天麦子收不成。”

paragraphs.text: Next day she went out again and left the same orders as before. The owner came, and waited. The sun grew hot, but nothing was done, for not a soul came. "You see," said the owner to his son, "these friends of ours are not to be depended upon; so run off at once to your uncles and cousins, and say I wish them to come early to-morrow morning and help us reap."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天她又出门了，留下了和之前一样的吩咐。主人来了，等着。太阳越来越热，但什么也没发生，因为一个人也没来。“你看，”主人对儿子说，“这些朋友靠不住；你马上去你叔叔和表亲家，告诉他们我希望他们明天一早来帮忙收割。”

paragraphs.text: This the Young Ones, in a great fright, told also to their mother. "Do not fear, children," said she. "Kindred and relations are not always very forward in helping one another; but keep your ears open and let me know what you hear to-morrow."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小崽子们吓坏了，也把这事告诉了妈妈。“别怕，孩子们，”她说。“亲戚们并不总是很乐意互相帮助；但你们竖起耳朵，明天听到什么就告诉我。”

paragraphs.text: The owner came the next day, and, finding his relations as backward as his neighbors, said to his son, "Now listen to me. Get two good sickles ready for to-morrow morning, for it seems we must reap the grain by ourselves."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天主人来了，发现亲戚们和邻居一样不积极，就对儿子说：“现在听我说。明天早上准备好两把好镰刀，看来我们得自己收割庄稼了。”

paragraphs.text: "Then, my dears," said she, "it is time for us to go; for when a man undertakes to do his work himself, it is not so likely that he will be disappointed." She took away her Young Ones at once, and the grain was reaped the next day by the old man and his son.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “那么，亲爱的，”她说，“我们该走了；因为当一个人决定自己动手干活时，就不太可能失望了。”她立刻带走了小崽子们，第二天老人和他的儿子收割了庄稼。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 10. WHY BOYS TAKE OFF THEIR HATS IN CHURCH

paragraphs.text: When the boy Moses was old enough to leave his mother he went to live with his new mother in the king's palace.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 男孩摩西到了离开母亲的年纪，便去王宫与他的新母亲同住。

paragraphs.text: Moses was a good boy. He studied his lessons so well in school that he grew up to be one of the wisest and best young men in all the land. But Moses never forgot his own Hebrew people. He was not careless of the cruel way they were treated as slaves by the king's officers. He tried to improve their sad condition in his own hasty way, but he soon saw that neither his own people nor their masters wanted a princess's son to interfere. They were both ready to kill him for trying to help. So Moses had to flee for his life into the mountains where he became a shepherd. One day as he was leading his sheep up the mountainside, he saw a thorn-bush all aflame; and it kept on burning, but was not burned up. Moses wondered to see so strange a sight. Leaving his sheep he went near. Suddenly a Voice called out of the midst of the fire-bush, "Moses! Moses!" Moses answered, "Here am I." The Voice said, "Take off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground," It was God, in the form of an angel, speaking to him. Moses at once took off his shoes and bowed reverently in the presence of God. Then God told him a better way by which he could help his downtrodden people and set them free from their cruel masters who were beating them and making their life so hard. He told Moses he wanted him to lead his people out of their bondage. At first Moses was afraid he was not able to do what God wanted him to do, but God said, "Certainly, Moses, I will be with thee." Moses obeyed the Voice that spoke that day to him out of the fire-bush, and he became one of the greatest of leaders and lawgivers that this world ever saw. Men and boys take off their hats in church to-day for the same reason that Moses removed his shoes before the fire-bush--to show reverence in the presence of God and respect for his wonderful way of speaking to men.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 摩西是个好孩子。他在学校功课优异，长大后成为全国最聪慧善良的年轻人之一。但他从未忘记自己的希伯来同胞。他无法漠视国王的官员把同胞当作奴隶般残酷虐待。他试图用自己急躁的方式改善他们的悲惨处境，但很快发现，无论是自己的同胞还是他们的主人，都不愿让一位公主的儿子插手。双方都因他想帮忙而要杀他。于是摩西不得不逃命到山中，做了牧羊人。一天，他正赶着羊群上山，看见一丛荆棘燃着火焰，一直烧着却烧不毁。摩西惊异于这奇景，便离开羊群走近。突然，火焰中传出声音：“摩西！摩西！”摩西回答：“我在这里。”那声音说：“脱下你脚上的鞋，因为你所站之地是圣地。”这是上帝化作天使在对他说话。摩西立刻脱鞋，在上帝面前恭敬俯首。随后上帝告诉他一个更好的办法，让他能帮助受压迫的同胞，把他们从那些鞭打他们、使他们生活艰难的残酷主人手中解救出来。上帝要摩西带领百姓脱离奴役。起初摩西害怕自己无法完成上帝的旨意，但上帝说：“摩西，我必与你同在。”摩西听从了那天从荆棘火焰中对他说话的声音，最终成为世上最伟大的领袖和立法者之一。如今人们在教堂里脱帽，与摩西在燃烧的荆棘前脱鞋是同一个道理——都是为了表示在上帝面前的敬畏，以及对他这奇妙的传话方式的尊敬。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 3. THE FIRST TWO BROTHERS

paragraphs.text: The first two brothers in the world were Cain and Abel. They were born after their parents were driven out of their beautiful garden-home in Eden. When these boys grew up, Cain, the elder, became a farmer, and Abel became a shepherd. Their parents brought them up always to ask God to forgive them when they did wrong, and to bring offerings to him of what they had. One day when they came with their gifts, Abel, with a loving heart, carried a lamb, the best of his flock, but Cain brought some fruit in a careless way. God was well pleased with Abel's gift, because of the love that came with it; but not with Cain's, because Cain kept hatred to his brother in his heart. Cain was angry and his face became dark and scowling. God said: "Why are you angry and scowling? If you do well, will you not be happy? If you do not well, hatred in your heart will crouch, like a lion, ready to spring at you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 世上最早的兄弟是该隐和亚伯。父母被逐出伊甸园美丽家园后，他们出生了。兄弟俩长大后，哥哥该隐成了农夫，亚伯做了牧羊人。父母教导他们犯错时要祈求上帝宽恕，并献上自己的收成。一天献礼时，亚伯满怀爱心献上羊群中最好的羔羊，该隐却漫不经心地带来些水果。上帝悦纳了亚伯的礼物，因其中饱含爱意；却不喜悦该隐的，因他心中对兄弟怀恨。该隐恼怒，脸色阴沉。上帝说："为何恼怒变脸？行善岂不快乐？若行不善，心中仇恨会如伏狮伺机扑向你。"

paragraphs.text: But Cain paid no attention to God's loving word. One day he said to Abel, "Come into the field with me." When they were there alone, the crouching lion of hatred in Cain's heart sprang up, and Cain lifted up his hand and slew his brother. Then Cain heard God's voice saying, "Where is thy brother?" He answered untruthfully, "I know not; am I my brother's keeper?" Then as Cain had done this wicked deed, God sent him from his home and parents to become a wanderer on the earth, working even harder than his father and his mother did. Cain's suffering was just what he had brought upon himself, yet he said, "My punishment is greater than I can bear." He was afraid wherever he went men would seek to kill him, for he knew he deserved to be killed. But God gave him a mark by which he could know that God was still watching over him and would not let any one kill him. So Cain went away and built a city and lived unhappily the rest of his life, away from his father and mother, because he had allowed hatred instead of love to live in his heart, and because he had not tried to please his loving Father in heaven.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但该隐无视上帝的慈言。一日他对亚伯说："跟我到田里去。"独处时，该隐心中伏卧的仇恨之狮猛然跃起，他举手杀死了兄弟。随后该隐听见上帝的声音："你兄弟在哪里？"他谎答："我不知道，难道我是看守兄弟的吗？"因该隐行此恶事，上帝将他逐出家园和父母，使他成为地上漂泊者，劳作比父母更艰辛。该隐的苦难实为自招，他却说："我的惩罚太重，难以承受。"他恐惧所到之处会有人杀他，自知罪该万死。但上帝给他立了记号，让他知道上帝仍在看顾他，不许人杀他。于是该隐离去建城，余生远离父母，郁郁寡欢，只因他让仇恨而非爱住在心中，且未努力取悦天上的慈父。

entries.title: How to Tell Stories to Children: THE STORY OF LITTLE TAVWOTS

paragraphs.text: This is the story an Indian woman told a little white boy who lived with his father and mother near the Indians' country; and Tavwots is the name of the little rabbit.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这是一个印第安妇人讲给白人小男孩的故事——男孩和父母住在印第安人领地附近；小兔子的名字叫塔沃茨。

paragraphs.text: But once, long ago, Tavwots was not little,--he was the largest of all four-footed things, and a mighty hunter. He used to hunt every day; as soon as it was day, and light enough to see, he used to get up, and go to his hunting. But every day he saw the track of a great foot on the trail, before him. This troubled him, for his pride was as big as his body.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但很久以前，塔沃茨并不小——他是所有四足动物中最大的，还是个了不起的猎手。他每天天刚亮就去打猎。可每天他都在小径上看见比自己先行的巨大脚印。这让他烦恼，因为他的骄傲和身体一样庞大。

paragraphs.text: "Who is this," he cried, "that goes before me to the hunting, and makes so great a stride? Does he think to put me to shame?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “这是谁?”他喊道,“竟敢抢在我前头去打猎,还迈这么大的步子?难道存心想让我难堪吗?”

paragraphs.text: And the next morning he got up earlier; but still the great footprints and the mighty stride were before him. The next morning he got up still earlier; but there were the mighty foot-tracks and the long, long stride.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天早晨他起得更早,可那巨大的脚印和大步流星的足迹依旧在他前头。再一天早晨他起得更早,可那巨大的足迹和长长的步幅仍在眼前。

paragraphs.text: "Now I will set me a trap for this impudent fellow," said Tavwots, for he was very cunning. So he made a snare of his bowstring and set it in the trail overnight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “现在我要给这个无礼的家伙设个陷阱，”塔沃茨说，因为他非常狡猾。于是他用弓弦做了一个套索，连夜放在小路上。

paragraphs.text: And when in the morning he went to look, behold, he had caught the sun in his snare! All that part of the earth was beginning to smoke with the heat of it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 早晨他去看时，竟发现套索里捉住了太阳！那片土地被太阳的热气烤得开始冒烟。

paragraphs.text: Then Tavwots saw what he had to do, and he drew his sharp hunting-knife and ran to cut the bowstring. But the heat was so great that he ran back before he had done it; and when he ran back he was melted down to half his size! Then the earth began to burn, and the smoke curled up against the sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 塔沃茨明白自己该怎么做了,他拔出锋利的猎刀,跑过去要割断弓弦。可热浪太猛,他还没动手就退了回来;这一退,他被烤得熔化得只剩原来一半大小!大地开始燃烧,烟雾袅袅升向天空。

paragraphs.text: And Tavwots ran again to cut the bowstring. But the heat was so great that he ran back before he had done it, and he was melted down to a quarter of his size!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 塔沃茨再次跑去割弓弦。可热浪太猛,他还没动手又退了回来,这一回他被烤得熔化得只剩原来四分之一大小!

paragraphs.text: And Tavwots ran again; this time he cut the bowstring and set the sun free. But when he got back he was melted down to the size he is now! Only one thing is left of all his greatness: you may still see by the print of his feet as he leaps in the trail, how great his stride was when he caught the sun in his snare.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 塔沃茨第三次冲了上去,这次他割断了弓弦,放走了太阳。可等他退回来,已经被烤得熔化成如今这般大小了!他昔日的高大就只剩下一样痕迹:看他在小径上跳跃时留下的脚印,你仍能看出他当年套住太阳时步子迈得有多大。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: THE LAD WHO RODE SIDESADDLE

paragraphs.text: When Daniel Webster was a child he lived in the country, far from any city. He was not strong enough to work on the farm like his brothers; but he loved books and study.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 丹尼尔·韦伯斯特小时候住在乡下，远离任何城市。他身体不够强壮，不能像哥哥们那样在农场干活，但他热爱书籍和学习。

paragraphs.text: He was very young when he was first sent to school. The schoolhouse was two or three miles from home, but he did not mind the long walk through the woods and over the hills.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他很小就被送去上学。学校离家有两三英里远，但他并不介意穿过树林、翻过山丘的长途跋涉。

paragraphs.text: He soon learned all that his teacher could teach; for he was bright and quick, and had a good memory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他很快学会了老师能教的一切，因为他聪明机敏，记忆力也很好。

paragraphs.text: His father hoped that Daniel would grow up to be a wise and famous man. "But," said he, "no man can rightly succeed without an education."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的父亲希望丹尼尔长大后能成为一位睿智而著名的人。"但是，"他说，"没有教育，没有人能真正成功。"

paragraphs.text: So it was decided that the boy should go to some school where he might be prepared for college.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是决定让这个男孩去一所学校，为上大学做准备。

paragraphs.text: One evening his father said to him, "Daniel, you must be up early in the morning. You are going to Exeter with me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天晚上，父亲对他说："丹尼尔，你明天必须早起。你要和我一起去埃克塞特。"

paragraphs.text: The academy at Exeter was a famous school for preparing boys for college. It is still a famous school. But Daniel's father did not say anything about college.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 埃克塞特的学院是一所著名的预科学校，至今仍享有盛誉。但丹尼尔的父亲并未提及大学的事。

paragraphs.text: There were no railroads at that time, and Exeter was nearly fifty miles away. Daniel and his father would ride there on horseback.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那时还没有铁路，埃克塞特距离将近五十英里。丹尼尔和父亲将骑马前往。

paragraphs.text: Early in the morning two horses were brought to the door. One was Mr. Webster's horse; the other was an old gray nag with a lady's sidesaddle on its back.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 清晨，两匹马被牵到门口。一匹是韦伯斯特先生的马；另一匹是匹老灰马，背上驮着女士用的侧鞍。

paragraphs.text: "I understand," said Mr. Webster. "But our neighbor, Johnson, is sending the nag to Exeter for the use of a lady who is to ride back with me. He does me a favor by allowing you to ride on the animal, and I do him a favor by taking care of it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我明白，”韦伯斯特先生说，“不过，我们的邻居约翰逊正要把这匹老马送去埃克塞特，给一位要和我一起骑马回来的女士用。他允许你骑这匹牲口，是帮了我的忙；我替他照看这匹马，也是帮了他的忙。”

paragraphs.text: And so they set out on their journey to Exeter. Mr. Webster rode in front, and Daniel, on the old gray nag, followed behind. The roads were muddy, and they went slowly. It took them two days to reach Exeter.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是他们踏上了前往埃克塞特的旅程。韦伯斯特先生骑在前面，丹尼尔骑着那匹老灰马跟在后面。道路泥泞，他们走得很慢，花了两天才到达埃克塞特。

paragraphs.text: The people whom they met gazed at them and wondered who they could be. They scarcely noticed the sidesaddle; they noticed only the boy's dark eyes and his strong, noble face.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们遇到的人都盯着他们看，好奇他们是谁。人们几乎没注意到那副侧鞍，只注意到男孩深邃的眼睛和刚毅高贵的面容。

paragraphs.text: His clothes were of homemade stuff; his shoes were coarse and heavy; he had no gloves on his hands; he was awkward and bashful.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他穿着自家织的粗布衣裳，鞋子粗糙笨重，手上没戴手套，举止笨拙又羞怯。

paragraphs.text: Daniel Webster lived to become a famous orator and a great statesman. He was honored at home and abroad.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 丹尼尔·韦伯斯特后来成为著名的演说家和伟大的政治家，在国内外都备受尊敬。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: GOING TO SEA

paragraphs.text: "I should like to be a sailor," said George Washington. "Then I could go to many strange lands and see many wonderful things. And, by and by, I might become the captain of a ship."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我想当水手，”乔治·华盛顿说，“这样我就能去许多陌生的国度，见识无数奇妙的事物。说不定有朝一日，我还能当上船长。”

paragraphs.text: His older brothers were quite willing that he should go to sea. They said that a bright boy like George would not long be a common sailor. He would soon become a captain and then perhaps a great admiral.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的哥哥们很赞成他出海。他们说像乔治这样聪明的孩子不会久居人下，很快就能当上船长，甚至成为伟大的海军上将。

paragraphs.text: And so the matter was at last settled. George's brothers knew the master of a trading ship who was getting ready to sail to England. He agreed to take the boy with him and teach him how to be a good sailor.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 事情终于定了下来。乔治的兄弟们认识一艘商船的船长，那艘船正准备驶往英国。船长同意带上这个男孩，并教他如何成为一名优秀的水手。

paragraphs.text: "Do not let him go to sea. If he begins as a common sailor, he will never be anything else."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “别让他出海。如果他以普通水手起步，就永远只能是个水手了。”

paragraphs.text: But George had made up his mind to go. He was headstrong and determined. He would not listen to any one who tried to persuade him to stay at home. At last the day came for the ship to sail. It was waiting in the river. A boat was at the landing, ready to take him on board. The little chest that held his clothing had been carried down to the bank. George was in high glee at the thought of going.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但乔治已经下定决心要去。他固执而坚决，不听任何劝他留在家里的劝说。终于，船启航的日子到了。船停在河里，一艘小船在码头等着接他上船。装着他衣物的小箱子已经被搬到了岸边。想到即将出海，乔治欣喜若狂。

paragraphs.text: He stood on the doorstep and looked back into the house. He saw the kind faces of those whom he loved. He began to feel very sad.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他站在门阶上，回头望向屋内。他看到了所爱之人的亲切面孔，开始感到非常难过。

paragraphs.text: George saw the tears in his mother's eyes. He saw them rolling down her cheeks. He knew that she did not wish him to go. He could not bear to see her grief.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乔治看到母亲眼中的泪水，看到它们顺着她的脸颊滚落。他知道她不想让他走，他无法忍受她的悲伤。

paragraphs.text: He stood still for a moment, thinking. Then he turned quickly and said, "Mother, I have changed my mind. I will stay at home and do as you wish." Then he called to the black boy, who was waiting at the door, and said, "Tom, run down to the shore and tell them not to put the chest in the boat. Send word to the captain not to wait for me, for I have changed my mind. I am not going to sea."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他站了一会儿，思索着。然后他迅速转身说：“妈妈，我改变主意了。我要留在家里，按你的意愿做。”接着他叫来等在门口的黑人男孩，说：“汤姆，快跑到岸边，告诉他们别把箱子搬上船。捎话给船长，别等我了，我改变主意了，我不出海了。”

paragraphs.text: Who has not heard of George Washington? It has been said of him that he was the "first in war, the first in peace, and first in the hearts of his countrymen." He was our most famous president. He has been called the Father of his Country.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 谁没听说过乔治·华盛顿呢？人们称他为“战争第一人，和平第一人，同胞心中第一人”。他是我们最著名的总统，被称为国父。

entries.title: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: WILLIAM CULLEN BRYANT

paragraphs.text: William Cullen Bryant (1794-1878) was born in the rugged hill country of western Massachusetts. From infancy he showed remarkable powers of mind. He could read by the time he was two years old, wrote verses at nine, and when scarcely eighteen wrote his most noted poem, "Thanatopsis," now one of the world's classics. He had a wonderful memory, and it is said he could repeat "by heart" every poem he had written.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 威廉·卡伦·布莱恩特（1794—1878）出生于马萨诸塞州西部崎岖的山乡。他自幼便展现出非凡的才智：两岁能阅读，九岁能写诗，还不到十八岁就写出了他最著名的诗篇《死亡随想》，如今这首诗已是世界经典之一。他记忆力惊人，据说凡是自己写过的诗，都能一字不差地背诵下来。

paragraphs.text: Bryant removed to New York, where in 1825 he became editor of the Evening Post. Through the remainder of--his long life he devoted his energy and great gifts to building up one of the most forceful of American newspapers, but he found time also to study Nature and to write so many poems that we now think of him as a poet, not as an editor. He was also a student, and we are indebted to him for some excellent translations from old authors. And, finally, he was a public-spirited American, interested in all matters that have to do with the honor of our country. Imagine yourself in New York City during the latter part of the last century. If you were walking up Broadway almost any morning, your attention would be attracted to a venerable looking man, with heavy, flowing, snow-white hair and beard, whom you would be quite likely to meet swinging along at a vigorous pace. You would not need to be told that this man is our first American poet, with whose verses you are already familiar; and you would probably know, too, that he is also the editor of the Evening Post and that, although now past eighty, he is on his way to his office, walking from his home some two miles away, as he has done, rain or shine, for over half a century.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 布莱恩特迁居纽约，1825年成为《晚邮报》编辑。在漫长余生中，他倾注精力与天赋打造美国最具影响力的报纸之一，同时仍抽空研习自然、创作诗歌，以至于如今我们视其为诗人而非编辑。他亦是一位学者，为我们留下了若干优秀的古典译作。最终，他成为一位具有公共精神的美国人，关心一切关乎国家荣誉的事务。想象自己身处上世纪后期的纽约市：几乎每个早晨漫步百老汇时，你都会被一位须发如雪、步履矫健的长者吸引。无需他人告知，你便知这是美国首位诗人，其诗作早已耳熟能详；或许你也知道，他还是《晚邮报》编辑，虽年过八旬，仍每日从两英里外的家中步行上班，风雨无阻，已逾半个世纪。

paragraphs.text: This great man was not too busy with affairs, or too learned, to look for the joy that comes from companionship with Nature. Like Irving he chose American subjects taken from his own surroundings: the scenes of his boyhood, the flowers, birds, and hills of his old New England home. He found pleasure in the simplest things, and he wrote about this pleasure in the simplest way. In this simplicity and the variety of his interests his wealth consisted; a treasure that made rich not only the poet who possessed it but all Americans, to whom he left his life and works for an inheritance.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这位伟人并未因事务繁忙或学识渊博而忽视与自然相伴的乐趣。如同欧文，他选择美国本土题材：童年场景、新英格兰故乡的花鸟山丘。他在最平凡的事物中发现快乐，并以最质朴的方式书写这种快乐。这种质朴与兴趣的多样性构成了他的财富——不仅滋养了拥有它的诗人，更馈赠给所有美国人，成为他们继承其生命与作品的遗产。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 9. THE BOY WHO HATED TREES

paragraphs.text: One night Dick was told by his father to rise early the next morning and help set out some new trees. "I hate trees," said Dick, "I want to go fishing. I wish I lived in a land where there were no trees!" Then Dick fell asleep, and in his dream he heard the queerest rustling noise, and then a voice called out, "Here is a boy who hates trees!" A procession of trees came toward him. The willow was weeping; the poplar was trembling; the aspen was quaking; the pine and elm and maple and oak were followed by the fruit trees, like the apple and pear and cherry, while the walnut and birch and palm slowly brought up the rear. When all was quiet the Pine began: "Here is a boy that hates trees, and says we are of no use!" "Yes," said the Maple, "and this morning he ate some of my sugar." "Yes," said the Willow, "and he made a whistle out of me." "Yes," said the Palm, "and he fanned himself with one of my leaves." "And he got his bicycle tire out of me, and his rubber boots too," said another tree. The Elm said: "I have a plan. The wind will help us. The wind is our friend." So the wind took Dick and hurled him off to a great desert and dropped him down in a land where there were no trees. Dick felt very lonely and was full of fright when he saw a bear coming toward him in the distance, and there was no tree to climb. How glad he was that he was mistaken and that it was not a bear coming, but camels, with men on them. The men beckoned him to get up and ride, which he was glad to do. Soon he saw the men bowing down and thanking God and then waving their hands. Dick looked and saw a spot of green grass, a spring of cool water, and one of the things he hated--a tree. He thought he had never seen anything so beautiful in all his life. He tumbled off the camel, ran toward the tree, and threw his arms about it, saying, "Dear tree, dear tree!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天晚上，迪克的父亲让他第二天早起帮忙种新树。迪克说：“我讨厌树，我想去钓鱼。真希望我住在一个没有树的地方！”接着迪克睡着了，梦中他听到一阵奇怪的沙沙声，随后一个声音喊道：“这儿有个讨厌树的男孩！”一群树朝他走来。柳树在哭泣，白杨在颤抖，山杨在摇晃；松树、榆树、枫树和橡树后面跟着苹果树、梨树、樱桃树等果树，而胡桃树、桦树和棕榈树缓缓殿后。一切安静后，松树开口：“这儿有个讨厌树的男孩，还说我们没用！”“没错，”枫树说，“今早他还吃了我的糖浆。”“是啊，”柳树说，“他还用我做了个哨子。”“对，”棕榈树说，“他还用我的叶子扇风。”“他还用我做了自行车轮胎和橡胶靴，”另一棵树说。榆树说：“我有个主意。风会帮我们，风是我们的朋友。”于是风卷起迪克，把他扔到一片大沙漠里，落在一个没有树的地方。迪克感到非常孤独，当看到远处有只熊朝他走来时，他吓坏了，却没有树可爬。幸好他看错了，来的不是熊，而是骆驼，上面骑着人。那些人招手让他骑上骆驼，他欣然照做。很快他看到那些人鞠躬感谢上帝，然后挥手。迪克望去，看到一片绿草、一汪清泉，还有一样他讨厌的东西——一棵树。他觉得自己从未见过如此美丽的事物。他跳下骆驼，跑向那棵树，张开双臂抱住它，说：“亲爱的树，亲爱的树！”

paragraphs.text: The next morning Dick was glad to help his father plant the trees, and the school-teacher on Arbor Day said, "I think if good care will help the trees, they will get it from Dick."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天早上，迪克很高兴帮父亲种树。植树节那天，老师说：“如果细心照料能帮助树木，那迪克一定会好好照顾它们。”

entries.title: Stories to Tell to Children: THE LARKS IN THE CORNFIELD

paragraphs.text: There was once a family of little Larks who lived with their mother in a nest in a cornfield. When the corn was ripe the mother Lark watched very carefully to see if there were any sign of the reapers' coming, for she knew that when they came their sharp knives would cut down the nest and hurt the baby Larks. So every day, when she went out for food, she told the little Larks to look and listen very closely to everything that went on, and to tell her all they saw and heard when she came home.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有一窝小云雀，和妈妈住在麦田的巢里。麦子成熟时，云雀妈妈非常警惕地观察是否有收割者到来的迹象，因为她知道收割者来时，锋利的镰刀会割倒巢穴，伤到小云雀。所以每天外出觅食时，她都会叮嘱小云雀仔细留意周围的一切，回家后把所见所闻告诉她。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, Mother, dear Mother," they said, "you must move us away to-night! The farmer was in the field to-day, and he said, 'The corn is ready to cut; we must call in the neighbors to help.' And then he told his son to go out to-night and ask all the neighbors to come and reap the corn to-morrow."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "哦，妈妈，亲爱的妈妈，"小云雀们说，"您今晚就得把我们搬走！农夫今天到田里来了，他说：‘麦子该收割了，得请邻居们帮忙。’然后他让儿子今晚去请所有邻居明天来收麦子。"

paragraphs.text: The mother Lark laughed. "Don't be frightened," she said; "if he waits for his neighbors to reap the corn we shall have plenty of time to move; tell me what he says to-morrow."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 云雀妈妈笑了。“别害怕，”她说，“如果他等邻居来收麦子，我们还有足够时间搬家；明天告诉我他怎么说。”

paragraphs.text: The next night the little Larks were quite trembling with fear; the moment their mother got home they cried out, "Mother, you must surely move us to-night! The farmer came to-day and said, 'The corn is getting too ripe; we cannot wait for our neighbors; we must ask our relatives to help us.' And then he called his son and told him to ask all the uncles and cousins to come to-morrow and cut the corn. Shall we not move to-night?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天晚上，小云雀们吓得直发抖；妈妈一回家，他们就喊道：“妈妈，您今晚一定得搬走我们！农夫今天来了，说：‘麦子太熟了，不能再等邻居；得请亲戚帮忙。’然后他叫来儿子，让儿子请所有叔叔和表亲明天来割麦子。我们今晚不搬吗？”

paragraphs.text: "Don't worry," said the mother Lark; "the uncles and cousins have plenty of reaping to do for themselves; we'll not move yet."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "别担心，"云雀妈妈说，"叔叔和表亲们自己也有不少庄稼要收；我们暂时不搬。"

paragraphs.text: The third night, when the mother Lark came home, the baby Larks said, "Mother, dear, the farmer came to the field to-day, and when he looked at the corn he was quite angry; he said, 'This will never do! The corn is getting too ripe; it's no use to wait for our relatives, we shall have to cut this corn ourselves.' And then he called his son and said, 'Go out to-night and hire reapers, and to-morrow we will begin to cut.'"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第三天晚上,云雀妈妈回家时,小云雀们说:“亲爱的妈妈,今天农夫又到田里来了,看着庄稼直生气。他说:‘这可不行!麦子太熟了,等亲戚来帮忙也来不及了,我们只好自己动手割。’然后他叫来儿子说:‘今晚就去雇收割的人,明天我们就开镰。’”

paragraphs.text: "Well," said the mother, "that is another story; when a man begins to do his own business, instead of asking somebody else to do it, things get done. I will move you out to-night."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好啊,”云雀妈妈说,“这就另当别论了。一个人不再指望别人,而是开始亲自动手做自己的事,事情就办得成了。今晚我就把你们搬走。”

entries.title: Stories to Tell to Children: THE TALKATIVE TORTOISE

paragraphs.text: Once upon a time, a Tortoise lived in a pond with two Ducks, who were her very good friends. She enjoyed the company of the Ducks, because she could talk with them to her heart's content; the Tortoise liked to talk. She always had something to say, and she liked to hear herself say it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，一只乌龟和两只鸭子同住在一个池塘里，她们是很好的朋友。乌龟很喜欢和鸭子们作伴，因为可以尽情地聊天；乌龟爱说话，总有说不完的话，也爱听自己说话的声音。

paragraphs.text: After many years of this pleasant living, the pond became very low, in a dry season; and finally it dried up. The two Ducks saw that they could no longer live there, so they decided to fly to another region, where there was more water. They went to the Tortoise to bid her good-by.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这样愉快地过了许多年，池塘在干旱季节里水位变得很低，最后终于干涸了。两只鸭子看出她们无法再住下去，于是决定飞到另一个水更多的地方去。她们去向乌龟告别。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, don't leave me behind!" begged the Tortoise. "Take me with you; I must die if I am left here."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "哦，别丢下我！"乌龟哀求道。"带我走吧；留在这里我会死的。"

paragraphs.text: The Ducks felt so sorry for her that at last they thought of a way to take her. "We have thought of a way which will be possible," they said, "if only you can manage to keep still long enough.
