We will each take hold of one end of a stout stick, and do you take the middle in your mouth; then we will fly up in the air with you and carry you with us. But remember not to talk! If you open your mouth, you are lost."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 鸭子们十分同情她，终于想出了一个带她走的办法。“我们想出了一个或许行得通的法子，”它们说，“前提是你能始终一声不吭。我们俩各叼住一根结实棍子的一端，你呢，把棍子中间咬在嘴里；这样我们就带你飞上天，把你一起带走。可记住，千万别说话！只要你一张嘴，就完了。”

paragraphs.text: The Tortoise said she would not say a word; she would not so much as move her mouth; and she was very grateful. So the Ducks brought a strong little stick and took hold of the ends, while the Tortoise bit firmly on the middle. Then the two Ducks rose slowly in the air and flew away with their burden.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌龟说她一个字也不会说，连嘴都不会动一下，并且万分感激。于是鸭子们找来一根结实的小棍子，各叼住一端，乌龟则紧紧咬住中间。接着两只鸭子缓缓升空，驮着她飞走了。

paragraphs.text: When they were above the treetops, the Tortoise wanted to say, "How high we are!" But she remembered, and kept still. When they passed the church steeple she wanted to say, "What is that which shines?" But she remembered, and held her peace. Then they came over the village square, and the people looked up and saw them. "Look at the Ducks carrying a Tortoise!" they shouted; and every one ran to look. The Tortoise wanted to say, "What business is it of yours?" But she didn't. Then she heard the people shout, "Isn't it strange! Look at it! Look!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当它们飞过树梢时，乌龟想说："我们飞得多高啊！"但她忍住了，保持沉默。当它们经过教堂尖顶时，她想说："那闪闪发光的是什么？"但她忍住了，一言不发。然后它们飞到了村庄广场上空，人们抬头看见了它们。"看！鸭子驮着一只乌龟！"他们喊道；所有人都跑来看。乌龟想说："关你们什么事？"但她没说出口。接着她听到人们喊道："真奇怪！快看！看啊！"

paragraphs.text: The Tortoise forgot everything except that she wanted to say, "Hush, you foolish people!" She opened her mouth,-- and fell to the ground. And that was the end of the Tortoise.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 乌龟把一切都抛到了脑后，一心只想说：“住口，你们这些蠢人！”她一张嘴——便从空中摔落在地。乌龟就这样送了命。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: THE BOY AND THE ROBBERS

paragraphs.text: In Persia, when Cyrus the Great was king, boys were taught to tell the truth. This was one of their first lessons at home and at school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在波斯，居鲁士大帝统治时期，男孩们被教导要诚实。这是他们在家里和学校的第一课之一。

paragraphs.text: When Otanes was twelve years old, his parents wished to send him to a distant city to study in a famous school that was there. It would be a long journey and a dangerous one. So it was arranged that the boy should travel with a small company of merchants who were going to the same place. "Good-by, Otanes! Be always brave and truthful," said his father. "Farewell, my child! Love that which is beautiful. Despise that which is base," said his mother.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯十二岁时，父母想送他去远方城市的一所著名学校学习。路途遥远且危险，于是安排他与一队前往同一地点的商人同行。“再见，奥塔内斯！永远勇敢诚实。”父亲说。“别了，我的孩子！热爱美好，鄙视卑劣。”母亲说。

paragraphs.text: The little company began its long journey. Some of the men rode on camels, some on horses. They went but slowly, for the sun was hot and the way was rough.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 小队开始了漫长的旅程。有人骑骆驼，有人骑马。他们走得很慢，因为太阳炙热，道路崎岖。

paragraphs.text: Suddenly, towards evening, a band of robbers swooped down upon them. The merchants were not fighting men. They could do nothing but give up all their goods and money.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 傍晚时分，一伙强盗突然袭击了他们。商人们并非战士，只能交出所有货物和钱财。

paragraphs.text: "Well, boy, what have you got?" asked one of the robbers, as he pulled Otanes from his horse.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “喂，小子，你有什么？”一个强盗把奥塔内斯从马上拉下来问道。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, well! You can't make me believe that," said the robber; and he hurried away to rob one of the rich merchants.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦，好吧！你骗不了我。”强盗说着，匆匆去抢劫一个富商。

paragraphs.text: "You are a brave lad to be joking with robbers" said the man; and he also hurried on to a more promising field.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你真是个勇敢的小子，敢跟强盗开玩笑。”那人说着，也赶往更有利可图的地方。

paragraphs.text: At length the chief of the band called to Otanes and said, "Young fellow, have you anything worth taking?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最后，匪首转向奥塔内斯问道：“小伙子，你有什么值钱的东西吗？”

paragraphs.text: Otanes answered, "I have already told two of your men that I have forty pieces of gold in my hat. But they wouldn't believe me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯回答：“我已经告诉过你两个手下，我帽子里有四十枚金币，可他们不信。”

paragraphs.text: "Why did you tell us where to find it?" he asked. "No one would have thought that a child like you had gold about him."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你为何主动告诉我们藏金之处？”他问道，“没人会想到你这样的孩子身上有金子。”

paragraphs.text: "If I had answered your questions differently, I should have told a lie," said Otanes; "and none but cowards tell lies"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 奥塔内斯说：“若我回答不同，便是说谎；只有懦夫才说谎。”

paragraphs.text: The robber chief was struck by this answer. He thought of the number of times that he himself had been a coward. Then he said, "You are a brave boy, and you may keep your gold. Here it is. Mount your horse, and my own men will ride with you and see that you reach the end of your journey in safety."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 匪首被这番话触动，想起自己多次懦弱之举，便说：“你是勇敢的孩子，金子归你了。拿好，骑上马，我的人会护送你安全抵达目的地。”

paragraphs.text: Otanes, in time, became one of the famous men of his country. He was the advisor and friend of two of the kings who succeeded Cyrus.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 后来，奥塔内斯成为该国名人，是居鲁士两位继任国王的顾问与挚友。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 1. THE BOY HERO OF HOLLAND

paragraphs.text: Once there was a good boy who had a kind-hearted mother. One afternoon she said: "Here, Peter, are some cakes I want you to take to the poor old blind man who is very ill, and who lives a mile and a half away from town. If you go quickly and do not stop to play, you will be home before it is dark." Peter took the cakes to the poor old blind man, who said, "You are a kind-hearted boy; thank your mother for me." Light-hearted because he had made the blind man happy, Peter was walking home when suddenly he noticed a little stream of water trickling through the great bank on the side of the road. This was in Holland, where much of the land is below the level of the sea, and where dikes are built by the people to keep back the sea. Every boy in Holland knows the danger of even a small leak in the dike. Peter understood at once that this tiny stream would soon make a large hole and the whole city would be flooded. In a moment he saw what he must do. He climbed down the side of the dike and thrust his chubby little hand and finger into the tiny hole and stopped the flowing of the water. Then he cried out for help, but no one heard him; no one came to help. It grew dark, and cold; he was hungry; his arm ached and it began to grow stiff and numb. He shouted again: "O mother! mother!" But his mother thought Peter must be spending the night with the blind man, and did not know of his danger. Peter thought how warm and cozy all at home were sleeping in their beds, and he said to himself, "I will not let them be drowned!" So that good boy stayed there all night long, holding back the water. Early next morning, a minister on his way to visit the sick, heard a groan, saw the boy, and called out to him, "What is the matter, my boy? Are you hurt? Why are you sitting there?" When Peter told him what he had done, the minister said, "I will hold my hand there while you run quickly to the town and get help." Very soon men came and repaired the leak in the dike, but all knew that Peter, by his courage and faithfulness, had saved the town of Haarlem that night.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有个善良的男孩，母亲心肠很好。一天下午，她说：“彼得，把这些蛋糕送给镇外一英里半处那位病重的盲人老爷爷。快去快回，别贪玩，天黑前就能到家。”彼得把蛋糕送给盲人，老人说：“你真是个好孩子，替我谢谢你妈妈。”彼得因让盲人开心而心情轻快，回家路上突然发现路边大堤上渗出一小股水流。这是荷兰，许多土地低于海平面，人们筑堤防海。荷兰男孩都知道堤坝上哪怕一个小洞都极其危险。彼得立刻明白这小水流会很快变成大洞，淹没全城。他瞬间意识到该怎么做：爬下堤坝，用胖乎乎的小手堵住小洞，止住水流。他大声呼救，却无人听见，无人相助。天黑了，又冷又饿，手臂酸痛僵硬麻木。他再次呼喊：“妈妈！妈妈！”但母亲以为他在盲人家过夜，不知他的危险。彼得想着家人都温暖舒适地睡在床上，暗下决心：“我不能让他们被淹死！”于是这个好男孩整夜守在那里，堵住水流。次日清晨，一位去探病的牧师听到呻吟声，发现男孩，喊道：“孩子，怎么了？受伤了吗？为什么坐在这里？”彼得说明原委后，牧师说：“我替你堵着，你快跑回镇上找人帮忙。”很快人们赶来修好了堤坝的漏洞，大家都明白，正是彼得的勇敢和忠诚，拯救了哈勒姆镇那个夜晚。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: HOW A PRINCE LEARNED TO READ

paragraphs.text: A thousand years ago boys and girls did not learn to read. Books were very scarce and very precious, and only a few men could read them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一千年前，孩子们不学识字。书籍稀少珍贵，只有少数人能阅读。

paragraphs.text: Each book was written with a pen or a brush. The pictures were painted by hand, and some of them were very beautiful. A good book would sometimes cost as much as a good house.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每本书都是用钢笔或毛笔写成的。图画是手工绘制的，有些非常精美。一本好书有时甚至价值相当于一栋好房子。

paragraphs.text: In those times there were even some kings who could not read. They thought more of hunting and fighting than of learning.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在那个时代，甚至有些国王都不识字。他们更看重狩猎和战斗，而非学问。

paragraphs.text: There was one such king who had four sons, Ethelbald, Ethelbert, Ethelred, and Alfred. The three older boys were sturdy, half-grown lads; the youngest, Alfred, was a slender, fair-haired child.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有这样一位国王，他有四个儿子：埃塞尔巴德、埃塞尔伯特、埃塞尔雷德和阿尔弗雷德。三个年长的儿子是健壮、半大的少年；最小的阿尔弗雷德则是个纤瘦、金发的孩子。

paragraphs.text: One day when they were with their mother, she showed them a wonderful book that some rich friend had given her. She turned the leaves and showed them the strange letters. She showed them the beautiful pictures, and told them how they had been drawn and painted.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天，他们和母亲在一起时，母亲拿出一本某位富有的朋友赠送的精美书籍。她翻开书页，向他们展示那些奇特的文字，又指着美丽的插图，讲述这些画是如何绘制和上色的。

paragraphs.text: They admired the book very much, for they had never seen anything like it. "But the best part of it is the story which it tells," said their mother. "If you could only read, you might learn that story and enjoy it. Now I have a mind to give this book to one of you"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们非常欣赏这本书，因为从未见过这样的书。“但最精彩的部分是它讲述的故事，”母亲说。“如果你们能识字，就能读懂那个故事并享受其中。现在我打算把这本书送给你们中的一个。”

paragraphs.text: "If I were a priest or a monk" said Ethelbald, "I would learn to read. But I am a prince, and it is foolish for princes to waste their time with such things."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “如果我是牧师或修士，”埃塞尔巴德说，“我会去学认字。可我是王子，王子把时间浪费在这种事上才是愚蠢。”

paragraphs.text: A few weeks passed by. Then, one morning, Alfred went into his mother's room with a smiling, joyous face.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 几周过去了。一天早上，阿尔弗雷德带着微笑、满脸喜悦地走进母亲的房间。

paragraphs.text: Alfred opened it with careful fingers. Then he began with the first word on the first page and read the first story aloud without making one mistake.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 阿尔弗雷德小心翼翼地用手指翻开书。然后他从第一页的第一个词读起，朗声诵读第一个故事，一个错也没有犯。

paragraphs.text: "I asked the monk, Brother Felix, to teach me," said Alfred. "And every day since you showed me the book, he has given me a lesson. It was no easy thing to learn these letters and how they are put together to make words. Now, Brother Felix says I can read almost as well as he."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 阿尔弗雷德说：“我请修士费利克斯兄弟教我。自从你让我看到那本书以来，他每天都给我上课。学会这些字母以及它们如何组合成词并非易事。现在费利克斯兄弟说我的阅读水平几乎和他一样好了。”

paragraphs.text: But his mother kissed him and gave him the beautiful book. "The prize is yours, Alfred," she said. "I am sure that whether you grow up to be a monk or a king, you will be a wise and noble man."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但他的母亲亲吻了他，并把那本漂亮的书给了他。“奖品是你的，阿尔弗雷德，”她说。“我相信无论你长大后成为修士还是国王，你都会成为一个明智而高尚的人。”

paragraphs.text: And Alfred did grow up to become the wisest and noblest king that England ever had. In history he is called Alfred the Great.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 阿尔弗雷德长大后确实成为了英格兰有史以来最明智、最高尚的国王。历史上他被称为阿尔弗雷德大帝。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: THE KING AND THE PAGE

paragraphs.text: Many years ago there was a king of Prussia, whose name was Frederick; and because he was very wise and very brave, people called him Frederick the Great. Like other kings, he lived in a beautiful palace and had many officers and servants to wait upon him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 许多年前，有一位普鲁士国王，名叫腓特烈；因为他非常聪明且勇敢，人们称他为腓特烈大帝。和其他国王一样，他住在美丽的宫殿里，有许多官员和仆人侍奉他。

paragraphs.text: Among the servants there was a little page whose name was Carl. It was Carl's duty to sit outside of the king's bedroom and be ready to serve him at any time.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在仆人中有个小侍从名叫卡尔。卡尔的职责是坐在国王卧室外，随时准备为他服务。

paragraphs.text: One night the king sat up very late, writing letters and sending messages; and the little page was kept busy running on errands until past midnight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天晚上，国王熬夜写信、发送消息；小侍从一直忙着跑腿，直到午夜过后。

paragraphs.text: The next morning the king wished to send him on another errand. He rang the little bell which was used to call the page, but no page answered.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 第二天早上，国王想派他去办另一件事。他摇了摇用来召唤侍从的小铃铛，但没有人应答。

paragraphs.text: "I wonder what can have happened to the boy," he said; and he opened the door and looked out. There, sitting in his chair, was Carl, fast asleep. The poor child was so tired after his night's work that he could not keep awake.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我想知道这孩子出了什么事，”他说着打开门向外看。卡尔正坐在椅子上，睡得很熟。可怜的孩子经过一夜的工作后太累了，无法保持清醒。

paragraphs.text: The king was about to waken him roughly, when he saw a piece of paper on the floor beside him. He picked it up and read it.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王正要粗暴地摇醒他，却看见他身旁地上有张纸。他捡起来读道：

paragraphs.text: _Dearest Carl; You are a good boy to send me all your wages, for now I can pay the rent and buy some warm clothing for your little sister. I thank you for it, and pray that God will bless you. Be faithful to the king and do your duty._
paragraphs.translation_zh: 最亲爱的卡尔:你真是个好孩子,把工钱全寄给了我。这下我既能付房租,又能给你妹妹添置些暖和的衣裳了。妈妈谢谢你,愿上帝保佑你。要忠于国王,尽心尽职。

paragraphs.text: The king went back to the room on tiptoe. He took ten gold pieces from his table and wrapped them in the little letter. Then he went out again, very quietly, and slipped them all into the boy's pocket.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 国王踮着脚走回房间，从桌上取了十枚金币，用那封短信包好。随后他悄无声息地再次出门，将金币轻轻塞进男孩的口袋。

paragraphs.text: He put his hand in his pocket, and was surprised to find the gold pieces wrapped in his mother's letter. Then his eyes overflowed with tears, and he fell on his knees before the king.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 卡尔把手伸进口袋,惊讶地摸到那几枚金币,正裹在母亲的信里。他顿时热泪盈眶,跪倒在国王面前。

paragraphs.text: "Oh, your Majesty!" cried Carl. "Have mercy on me. It is true that I have been asleep, but I know nothing about this money. Some one is trying to ruin me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "陛下！"卡尔哭喊道，"请宽恕我。我确实睡着了，但这钱的事我一无所知。有人想陷害我。"

paragraphs.text: "Have courage, my boy," said the king. "I know how you must have been overwearied with long hours of watching. And people say that fortune comes to us in our sleep. You may send the gold pieces to your mother with my compliments; and tell her that the king will take care of both her and you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "鼓起勇气，孩子，"国王说，"我知道你定是值夜太久累坏了。人们常说好运会在睡梦中降临。把这些金币寄给你母亲，代我向她致意；告诉她国王会照顾你们母子。"

entries.title: The Silent Readers: Sixth Reader: FRANKLIN WRITES FOR THE NEWSPAPER

paragraphs.text: Here is Benjamin Franklin's own account of his first attempt at writing. You see, he did not have a chance to learn to write, as you do, by practice in composition in school.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 以下是本杰明·富兰克林对自己初次写作尝试的自述。你看，他并没有像你们这样，有机会在学校通过作文练习来学习写作。

paragraphs.text: My brother had, in 1720 or 1721, begun to print a newspaper. It was the second that appeared in America, and was called the "New England Courant". The only one before it was the "Boston News-Letter". I remember his being _dissuaded_ by some of his friends from the undertaking, as not likely to succeed, one newspaper being, in their judgment, enough for America. At this time (1771) there are not less than five and twenty. He went on, however, with the undertaking; and after having worked in composing the types and printing off the sheets, I was employed to carry the papers through the streets to the customers.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 1720年或1721年间，我哥哥开始办报。那是美洲出现的第二份报纸，名为《新英格兰报》。此前仅有《波士顿新闻信札》。我记得有些朋友劝他放弃，认为不会成功——在他们看来，美洲有一份报纸就够了。而如今（1771年）报纸已不下二十五份。但他坚持办了下去；我排完字、印完报后，还要负责把报纸送到街上的订户手中。

paragraphs.text: He had some ingenious men among his friends, who amused themselves by writing little pieces for this paper, which gained it credit and made it more in demand; and these gentlemen often visited us. Hearing their conversations, and their accounts of the _approbation_ their papers were received with, I was excited to try my hand among them; but being still a boy, and suspecting that my brother would object to printing anything of mine in his paper if he knew it to be mine, I contrived to _disguise_ my hand; and, writing an _anonymous_ paper, I put it in at night under the door of the printing-house. It was found in the morning and communicated to his writing friends when they called in as usual. They read it, and commented on it in my hearing; and I had the _exquisite_ pleasure of finding it met with their approbation, and that in their different guesses at the author, none were named but men of some _character_ among us for learning and _ingenuity_. I suppose now that I was rather lucky in my judges and that perhaps they were not really so very good ones as I then _esteemed_ them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他的朋友中有几位才子，常为这份报纸写些小文章自娱，这些文章为报纸赢得了声誉，也使其更受欢迎；这些先生们时常来访。听他们交谈，以及他们讲述自己文章如何受到好评，我跃跃欲试，想在他们中间一展身手。但当时我还是个孩子，担心哥哥若知道文章是我所写，会反对在报上刊登，于是便设法伪装笔迹，写了一篇匿名文章，趁夜塞进印刷所的门缝。次日清晨文章被发现，当他的文友照常来访时，他便拿给他们看。他们读后，当着我的面评论起来；我欣喜地发现文章得到了他们的赞许，而他们猜测作者时，提到的都是些在我们中间以学识和才智著称的人。如今想来，我不过是运气好，遇到了这些评判者，或许他们并不像我当时认为的那样高明。

paragraphs.text: Encouraged, however, by this, I wrote and conveyed in the same way to the press several more papers which were equally approved; and I kept my secret till my small fund of sense for such performances was pretty well exhausted, and then I _discovered_ it, when I began to be considered a little more by my brother's acquaintance, and in a manner that did not quite please him, as he thought, probably with reason, that it tended to make me too vain.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 受此鼓舞，我以同样方式向报社投了更多文章，均获好评。我严守秘密，直到这类创作的小小才思几乎耗尽才公开真相。此后，哥哥的熟人开始对我另眼相看，但这并未让他完全高兴——他大概有理由认为，这会使我过于自负。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 9. AUDUBON, NATURALIST

paragraphs.text: Every boy who loves out-of-door life should know the story of John James Audubon. He was born on a farm in Louisiana, May 4, 1780. His parents were French and when very young he was taken to France where he attended school. His favorite study was of animals and birds. He often roamed the woods, bringing home birds' nests and eggs, curious rocks, and bits of moss. His father bought him a picture-book of birds. The delighted boy painted these copies, but saw they were not like real birds. Later he took lessons of the great French painter, David, who taught him to draw and paint things as they are. Returning to America, his father gave him a large farm in Pennsylvania where his studies of birds led him to decide to write a book on bird life, and illustrate it by his own drawings. This was a great task, but when this young man decided to do anything he never allowed difficulties to stand in his way. So he began his work and studied and painted year after year. He had to live much of the time in the woods, studying how the birds lived and built their nests. Sometimes he went by boat down the river; sometimes he went on horseback. Often he tramped alone through the trackless woods. Many nights he slept out-of-doors. He lost all his money and was obliged to stop his work and paint portraits and sell his choice drawings for a living. His heroic wife took up school-teaching to help him out with his work. One day while traveling he left his paintings of nearly a thousand birds in a wooden box in the home of a friend. Two months later, when he returned and opened the box, he found two large rats had got into the box and cut up all the paintings with their sharp teeth, making a nest for their young among the gnawed pieces. He said, "I will make better paintings!" It took him four long years to complete his pictures, but at last the great book was completed and published and praised throughout France, England, and the United States.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 每个热爱户外生活的男孩都应了解约翰·詹姆斯·奥杜邦的故事。他于1780年5月4日出生在路易斯安那州的一个农场。父母是法国人，他幼年被带到法国上学，最爱研究动物和鸟类。他常漫步林中，带回鸟巢、鸟蛋、奇石和苔藓。父亲送他一本鸟类画册，他欣喜地临摹，却发觉不像真鸟。后来师从法国大画家大卫，学习写实绘画。回到美国后，父亲在宾夕法尼亚州给了他一个大农场。对鸟类的研究促使他决定写一本关于鸟类生活的书，并用自己画的插图。这是一项艰巨的任务，但这位年轻人一旦决定做某事，就绝不让困难阻挡。于是他年复一年地研究和绘画。他大部分时间住在森林里，研究鸟类如何生活和筑巢。有时乘船沿河而下，有时骑马，常常独自徒步穿越无路可循的森林。许多夜晚露宿户外。他花光了所有钱，不得不停下工作，靠画肖像和出售精选画作谋生。他英勇的妻子以教书来资助他的事业。一次旅行中，他将近千幅鸟类画作放在木箱里寄存在朋友家。两个月后回来打开箱子，发现两只大老鼠钻进箱子，用利齿咬碎所有画作，在碎纸片中筑巢育雏。他说：“我会画出更好的画！”他花了整整四年完成画作，最终这部巨著完成出版，在法国、英国和美国广受赞誉。

paragraphs.text: The Society for the Protection of Our Feathered Friends was organized by this great naturalist, who spent the rest of his life for this great object. At present there are few places where boys and girls have not heard of Audubon. He died at his beautiful home on the Hudson River, greatly honored and beloved in France, England, and the United States.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这位伟大的博物学家组织成立了‘羽翼之友保护协会’，并为此倾尽余生。如今，几乎各地的孩子都听说过奥杜邦的名字。他在哈德逊河畔的美丽寓所中去世，深受法国、英国和美国的敬仰与爱戴。

entries.title: English Fairy Tales: HOW JACK WENT TO SEEK HIS FORTUNE

paragraphs.text: Once on a time there was a boy named Jack, and one morning he started to go and seek his fortune.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有个男孩叫杰克，一天早晨他出发去闯荡世界。

paragraphs.text: Well, they went on till it was about dark, and they began to think of some place where they could spend the night. About this time they came in sight of a house, and Jack told them to keep still while he went up and looked in through the window. And there were some robbers counting over their money. Then Jack went back and told them to wait till he gave the word, and then to make all the noise they could. So when they were all ready Jack gave the word, and the cat mewed, and the dog barked, and the goat bleated, and the bull bellowed, and the rooster crowed, and all together they made such a dreadful noise that it frightened the robbers all away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们一直走到天黑，开始寻找过夜的地方。这时他们看到一座房子，杰克让大家安静，自己上前透过窗户往里看——只见几个强盗正在数钱。杰克回去让大家等他下令，然后一起制造噪音。等一切就绪，杰克一声令下，猫喵喵叫，狗汪汪吠，山羊咩咩，公牛哞哞，公鸡喔喔，它们发出的可怕声响把强盗全吓跑了。

paragraphs.text: And then they went in and took possession of the house. Jack was afraid the robbers would come back in the night, and so when it came time to go to bed he put the cat in the rocking-chair, and he put the dog under the table, and he put the goat upstairs, and he put the bull down cellar, and the rooster flew up on to the roof, and Jack went to bed.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们进屋占据了房子。杰克担心强盗夜里回来，于是安排猫坐在摇椅上，狗躲在桌下，山羊上楼，公牛下地窖，公鸡飞上屋顶，然后自己上床睡觉。

paragraphs.text: By-and-by the robbers saw it was all dark and they sent one man back to the house to look after their money. Before long he came back in a great fright and told them his story.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久，强盗见四周漆黑，便派一人回屋查看钱财。那人很快惊慌失措地跑回来，讲述了遭遇。

paragraphs.text: "I went back to the house," said he, "and went in and tried to sit down in the rocking-chair, and there was an old woman knitting, and she stuck her knitting-needles into me." That was the cat, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我回到屋里，”他说，“想坐在摇椅上，结果有个老妇人在织毛衣，她用织针扎了我。”——那其实是猫。

paragraphs.text: "I went to the table to look after the money and there was a shoemaker under the table, and he stuck his awl into me." That was the dog, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我去桌边找钱，桌下有个鞋匠用锥子扎我。”——那其实是狗。

paragraphs.text: "I started to go upstairs, and there was a man up there threshing, and he knocked me down with his flail." That was the goat, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我正要上楼，楼上有个打谷的人用连枷把我打了下来。”——那其实是山羊。

paragraphs.text: "I started to go down cellar, and there was a man down there chopping wood, and he knocked me up with his axe." That was the bull, you know.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我刚开始下地窖，有个男人在下面劈柴，他一斧子把我撂倒了。”你知道，那是公牛干的。

paragraphs.text: "But I shouldn't have minded all that if it hadn't been for that little fellow on top of the house, who kept a-hollering, 'Chuck him up to me-e! Chuck him up to me-e!'" Of course that was the cock-a-doodle-do.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “要不是房顶上那个小家伙一直喊‘把他扔给我！扔给我！’，我倒也不在乎这些。”当然，那是公鸡在打鸣。

entries.title: The Pig Brother, and Other Fables and Stories: THE PATIENT CAT

paragraphs.text: When the spotted cat first found the nest, there was nothing in it, for it was only just finished. So she said, "I will wait!" for she was a patient cat, and the summer was before her. She waited a week, and then she climbed up again to the top of the tree, and peeped into the nest. There lay two lovely blue eggs, smooth and shining.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 花猫第一次发现鸟巢时，里面空空的，因为巢刚筑好。于是她说：“我会等的！”她很有耐心，夏天还长着呢。等了一周，她又爬上树顶，朝巢里窥视。里面躺着两枚漂亮的蓝蛋，光滑闪亮。

paragraphs.text: The spotted cat said, "Eggs may be good, but young birds are better. I will wait." So she waited; and while she was waiting, she caught mice and rats, and washed herself and slept, and did all that a spotted cat should do to pass the time away.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 花猫说：“蛋虽好，但小鸟更美味。我再等等。”于是她继续等待；等待期间，她捉老鼠、洗身子、睡觉，做着花猫该做的一切来消磨时光。

paragraphs.text: When another week had passed, she climbed the tree again and peeped into the nest. This time there were five eggs. But the spotted cat said again, "Eggs may be good, but young birds are better. I will wait a little longer!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 又过了一周，她再次爬上树窥视鸟巢。这次有五枚蛋。但花猫又说：“蛋虽好，但小鸟更美味。我再多等一会儿！”

paragraphs.text: So she waited a little longer and then went up again to look. Ah! there were five tiny birds, with big eyes and long necks, and yellow beaks wide open. Then the spotted cat sat down on the branch, and licked her nose and purred, for she was very happy. "It is worth while to be patient!" she said.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她又等了一阵，再次爬上去看。啊！有五只小鸟，大眼睛、长脖子，黄嘴张得大大的。花猫在树枝上坐下，舔舔鼻子，发出咕噜声，她非常开心。“耐心是值得的！”她说。

paragraphs.text: But when she looked again at the young birds, to see which one she should take first, she saw that they were very thin,--oh, very, very thin they were! The spotted cat had never seen anything so thin in her life.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 可当她再看小鸟，想先挑哪只时，发现它们太瘦了——哦，瘦得不得了！花猫这辈子从没见过这么瘦的东西。

paragraphs.text: "Now," she said to herself, "if I were to wait only a few days longer, they would grow fat. Thin birds may be good, but fat birds are much better. I will wait!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “现在，”她自言自语，“如果我再等几天，它们就会长胖。瘦鸟虽好，但胖鸟更棒。我再等等！”

paragraphs.text: So she waited; and she watched the father-bird bringing worms all day long to the nest, and said, "Aha! they must be fattening fast! they will soon be as fat as I wish them to be. Aha! what a good thing it is to be patient."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她等着，整天看着鸟爸爸往巢里叼虫子，心想：“哈哈！它们肯定在飞快长胖！很快就能胖到我满意的程度。哈哈！有耐心真是件好事。”

paragraphs.text: At last, one day she thought, "Surely, now they must be fat enough! I will not wait another day. Aha! how good they will be!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 终于有一天，她想：“现在它们肯定够肥了！我一天也不想再等了。哈哈！它们该有多美味啊！”

paragraphs.text: So she climbed up the tree, licking her chops all the way and thinking of the fat young birds. And when she reached the top and looked into the nest, it was empty!!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是她爬上树，一路舔着嘴巴，想着那些肥嘟嘟的小鸟。可等她爬到树顶往巢里一看——巢里空空如也！！

paragraphs.text: Then the spotted cat sat down on the branch and spoke thus, "Well, of all the horrid, mean, ungrateful creatures I ever saw, those birds are the horridest, and the meanest, and the most ungrateful! Mi-a-u-ow!!!!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 花猫坐在树枝上说道：“哼，在我见过的所有可恶、卑鄙、忘恩负义的家伙里，这些鸟最可恶、最卑鄙、最忘恩负义！喵——嗷——呜！！！”

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 20. THE HEROINE OF OHIO

paragraphs.text: In a little village in Ohio, on the banks of the Ohio River, there was a large fort called Fort Henry, which belonged to the colonists. When the Indians, who were fighting on the side of the English, attacked the village, all the men, women, and children fled to the fort. The Indians then attacked the fort, and all the men who went out to fight them were killed or taken prisoners. At last only twelve men were left in the fort to protect the women and children. When the colonists began to prepare for the second attack, they found their supply of powder almost exhausted, and without powder they knew death was near. Captain Zane called the twelve men together and said: "In my house there is a keg of powder. I do not wish to order any man to go for it, as it is a very dangerous thing to do, but I would like to have some one offer to go." Several young men at once volunteered. "It means almost certain death," said the captain. "I know that," replied one young man, "but we must have the powder. To stay means death to all." Just then the captain's sister, Elizabeth, a girl of fourteen, stepped forward. "I will go for the powder," she said; "you cannot spare one of the men, they are all wanted to protect the fort. If we are captured by the Indians, I shall surely be killed. So please let me go."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 俄亥俄河畔的一个小村庄里，有座属于殖民者的大型堡垒——亨利堡。当与英军联手的印第安人袭击村庄时，所有男女老幼都逃入堡垒。印第安人随即围攻堡垒，外出迎战的男人非死即俘。最后堡内只剩十二名男子保护妇孺。殖民者准备抵御第二次进攻时，发现火药几乎耗尽——没有火药就意味着死亡临近。赞恩上尉召集十二名男子说："我屋里有一桶火药。我不愿命令任何人去取，这太危险了，但希望有人自愿前往。"几名青年立刻请命。"这几乎必死无疑，"上尉说。"我知道，"一名青年回答，"但我们必须拿到火药。坐守只会全军覆没。"这时上尉十四岁的妹妹伊丽莎白走上前："让我去取火药吧，"她说，"你们不能抽调任何一名男子，他们都需守卫堡垒。若被印第安人俘虏，我必死无疑。请让我去吧。"

paragraphs.text: At that Captain Zane said, "No! No!" But he soon saw she was right; not a man could be spared. The gate of the fort opened and the girl ran quickly out. The Indians saw her and cried in surprise, "A squaw! a squaw!" but no Indian tried to shoot her. She entered the house and found the keg of powder, but it was too heavy for her to carry, so, girllike, she emptied the powder into her apron, and started back amid the firing of the Indians, but although their arrows whistled over her head, she ran swiftly on and reached the gate in safety.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 听了这话,赞恩上尉连声说:“不行!不行!”可他很快明白她说得对——实在抽不出一个人来。堡门一开,姑娘飞快地跑了出去。印第安人看见她,惊讶地喊道:“一个女人!一个女人!”却没有一个印第安人朝她放箭。她进了屋,找到那桶火药,可它太重,搬不动,于是她像女孩子家那样,把火药倒进围裙兜里,在印第安人的枪火中往回跑;尽管箭矢从她头顶呼啸而过,她仍飞奔不停,平安地跑到了堡门口。

paragraphs.text: With the help of the powder the colonists were able to keep the Indians away that night. The next morning more men came and the Indians were driven away, and so the colonists of Ohio won a great victory.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 凭借这些火药，殖民者当晚击退了印第安人。次日清晨援军赶到，印第安人被驱散，俄亥俄殖民者赢得了一场大胜。

paragraphs.text: The story of this fight at Fort Henry is often told, and the name of Elizabeth Zane, the brave girl who carried the apronful of powder to the men in the fort, will be remembered as long as brave deeds shall be told.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 亨利堡之战的故事代代相传，那位用围裙为堡内勇士运送火药的勇敢女孩伊丽莎白·赞恩，将永载英勇事迹的史册。

entries.title: How to Tell Stories to Children: THE STAR DOLLARS

paragraphs.text: There was once a little girl who was very, very poor. Her father and mother had died, and at last she had no little room to stay in, and no little bed to sleep in, and nothing more to eat except one piece of bread. So she said a prayer, put on her little jacket and her hood, and took her piece of bread in her hand, and went out into the world.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有个非常非常贫穷的小女孩。父母双亡后，她最终连栖身的小屋和睡觉的小床都没有了，只剩下一片面包。于是她做完祈祷，穿上小夹克和兜帽，手里拿着那片面包，走进了茫茫世界。

paragraphs.text: When she had walked a little way, she met an old man, bent and thin. He looked at the piece of bread in her hand, and said, "Will you give me your bread, little girl? I am very hungry." The little girl said, "Yes," and gave him her piece of bread.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她走了一小段路，遇见一位弯腰驼背、瘦骨嶙峋的老人。老人看着她手里的面包说：“小姑娘，能把你的面包给我吗？我很饿。”小姑娘回答：“好的。”便把面包给了他。

paragraphs.text: When she had walked a little farther she came upon a child, sitting by the path, crying. "I am so cold!" said the child. "Won't you give me your little hood, to keep my head warm?" The little girl took off her hood and tied it on the child's head. Then she went on her way.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她又走了一会儿，看见路边坐着一个哭泣的孩子。孩子说：“我好冷！你能把你的小帽子给我，让我暖暖头吗？”小姑娘摘下帽子，系在孩子的头上，然后继续赶路。

paragraphs.text: After a time, as she went, she met another child. This one shivered with the cold, and she said to the little girl, "Won't you give me your jacket, little girl?" And the little girl gave her her jacket. Then she went on again.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 又走了一段路，她遇见另一个孩子。这孩子冷得直发抖，对小姑娘说：“小姑娘，你能把你的外套给我吗？”小姑娘便把外套给了她，然后继续前行。

paragraphs.text: By-and-by she saw another child, crouching almost naked by the wayside. "O little girl," said the child, "won't you give me your dress? I have nothing to keep me warm." So the little girl took off her dress and gave it to the other child. And now she had nothing left but her little shirt. It grew dark, and the wind was cold, and the little girl crept into the woods, to sleep for the night. But in the woods a child stood, weeping and naked. "I am cold," she said, "give me your little shirt!" And the little girl thought, "It is dark, and the woods will shelter me; I will give her my little shirt"; so she did, and now she had nothing left in all the world.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 不久，她又看见一个孩子，几乎赤裸地蜷缩在路边。“哦，小姑娘，”那孩子说，“你能把你的裙子给我吗？我没什么能保暖的东西。”于是小姑娘脱下裙子给了那个孩子。现在她只剩下一件小衬衫。天黑了，风很冷，小姑娘钻进树林里过夜。但在树林中，一个孩子赤身裸体地站着哭泣。“我好冷，”她说，“把你的小衬衫给我吧！”小姑娘想：“天黑了，树林能为我遮风挡雨；我把衬衫给她吧。”于是她照做了，现在她一无所有了。

paragraphs.text: She stood looking up at the sky, to say her night-time prayer. As she looked up, the whole skyful of stars fell in a shower round her feet. There they were, on the ground, shining bright, and round. The little girl saw that they were silver dollars. And in the midst of them was the finest little shirt, all woven out of silk! The little girl put on the little silk shirt, and gathered the star dollars; and she was rich, all the days of her life.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 她站着仰望天空，准备做睡前祈祷。当她抬头时，满天繁星如雨点般洒落在她脚边。星星落在地上，闪闪发光，圆润明亮。小姑娘发现它们都是银币。在银币中间，有一件最精美的小衬衫，全是用丝绸织成的！小姑娘穿上丝绸衬衫，捡起星币；从此她一生富足。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 7. THE LADDER THAT REACHED TO HEAVEN

paragraphs.text: Isaac and Rebecca had two boys, Esau and Jacob. Esau became a hunter, and Jacob a shepherd. One day Esau came home from hunting very hungry. He asked Jacob to give him some of the red broth that he had just cooked. Jacob knew that Esau cared nothing for his birthright (that is, all that he would receive as the eldest son). But Jacob wanted that more than anything else in the world. So Jacob said, "Will you give me your birthright if I do?" Esau said, "Yes, I am starving; give me the broth for the blessing." Jacob could not believe Esau meant it; but he did mean it, and so sold his birthright for something to eat. Not long after, Jacob received the birthright blessing from his father, Isaac. Then Esau was sorry and angry, and hated his brother, and planned to kill him. Rebecca told Jacob what Esau was planning to do, and sent him to her brother's home to save Jacob's life.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 以撒和利百加有两个儿子：以扫和雅各。以扫成了猎人，雅各成了牧羊人。一天，以扫打猎回来，饿极了。他让雅各给他一些刚煮好的红豆汤。雅各知道以扫不在乎自己的长子权（即作为长子应得的一切），但雅各最渴望得到的就是长子权。于是雅各说：“如果我给你汤，你愿意把长子权给我吗？”以扫说：“好，我快饿死了；把汤给我，祝福归你。”雅各不敢相信以扫是认真的，但以扫确实当真了，就这样为了一顿饭卖掉了长子权。不久后，雅各从父亲以撒那里得到了长子的祝福。以扫又后悔又愤怒，恨自己的弟弟，并计划杀他。利百加告诉雅各以扫的打算，把他送到自己兄弟家，以保全雅各的性命。

paragraphs.text: So Jacob had to leave his father and mother and home and start alone on a long journey with nothing but a long cloak to wrap about him at night. When the sun went down, as he was thinking of the great wrong he had done his brother, tired and sad at heart, he lay down to sleep on a stony hillside, placing one of the stones under his head for a pillow. At last he fell asleep, and in his dream he saw a ladder reaching from earth to heaven. He saw beautiful shining angels coming down the ladder and going back. At the top he saw God looking down on him, saying, "I am the Lord, the God of Abraham and thy father Isaac." God promised if he would do what was right, that he would forgive all his wrong--be with him in all his journey and give him the wonderful promises made to Abraham and Isaac.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是雅各不得不离开父母和家，独自踏上漫长的旅程，除了一件夜里裹身的长袍外一无所有。太阳落山时，他想起自己对哥哥犯下的大错，心中疲惫而悲伤，便躺在石头遍布的山坡上，拿一块石头当枕头。他终于睡着了，梦中看见一架梯子从地面直通天堂。他看见美丽闪亮的天使沿着梯子上下往来。梯子顶端，上帝俯视着他说：“我是耶和华，你祖父亚伯拉罕和你父亲以撒的上帝。”上帝应许，只要他行正义之事，就会赦免他所有的过错——在他整个旅程中与他同在，并赐予他应许给亚伯拉罕和以撒的奇妙恩典。

paragraphs.text: Early in the morning, when Jacob awoke, he knelt beside that stone, promising God that he would be a better man. He lived to be an aged man--one hundred and forty-seven years old--but he never forgot that place which he called "The House of God," from which he saw the ladder that reached to heaven, showing him that God was near him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 清晨雅各醒来时，跪在那块石头旁向神承诺要成为更好的人。他活到高龄一百四十七岁，却从未忘记那个称为“神殿”的地方——他曾在那里看见通天的梯子，明白神与他同在。

paragraphs.text: From this story the beautiful lines of the hymn, which have been such a comfort to many upon battlefields and in the hour of death, were written:
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从这个故事中诞生了那首赞美诗的优美诗句，这些诗句曾在战场和临终时刻给许多人带来安慰：

paragraphs.text: Though like a wanderer, The sun gone down, Darkness be over me, My rest a stone, Yet, in my dreams I'd be, Nearer, my God, to thee, Nearer to thee.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 虽如流浪者，夕阳西沉，黑暗笼罩我，石头作枕，梦中我仍愿，更近我神，更近你身。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 9. THE BABY BROTHER IN A BASKET-BOAT

paragraphs.text: Long, long ago, a little boy was born in a Hebrew home, at a time when a cruel king of Egypt ordered all Hebrew boys that were born, to be thrown to the crocodiles in the great river Nile. But this little babe was so beautiful that his mother hid him in the house and prayed God to keep him safe. She hid him carefully for three months. Then, being afraid some one might hear him, she went to the river and gathered some long, strong grasses that grew there and braided them together, making a small basket and shaping it like a boat. To make it warm and dry inside, and to keep it from sinking when placed in the water, she painted it with black paint inside and out. Early one morning, when all was ready, the mother took her baby boy quietly sleeping in the basket-boat, and went down to the river Nile, the little baby's sister, Miriam, following closely behind her. The mother hid the basket among the tall grasses near the shore, and again prayed God to keep her baby safe. Miriam was left hiding in the tall grass near-by to see what would happen to her little brother in his new bed. Very soon the princess, the daughter of the cruel king of Egypt, with her maids, came down to the river to bathe. Quickly she spied the basket-boat and cried, "What is that floating on the water among the tall grasses? Bring it to me." One of her maids ran and picked up the basket and brought it to the princess. When she opened it, there was the most beautiful baby boy she had ever seen! The child was wide awake, and seeing the strange face, began to cry. "It is one of the Hebrew babies that my father ordered drowned!" she said. "But I have found him, and I will keep him as my own little baby boy. I will call his name 'Moses.'"
paragraphs.translation_zh: 很久以前，在埃及残暴的法老下令把所有出生的希伯来男婴扔进尼罗河喂鳄鱼的时代，一个希伯来家庭生下了一个男婴。这婴孩实在俊美，母亲便把他藏在家中，祈求神保佑他平安。她小心翼翼地藏了三个月，后来担心有人听见哭声，就到河边采来生长在那里的坚韧长草，编成一个小船模样的篮子。为了让篮子里面保暖干燥、放进水里又不会下沉，她在篮子内外都涂上了黑漆。一天清晨，一切准备就绪，母亲把仍在熟睡的男婴放进草篮，带着他来到尼罗河边，婴孩的姐姐米利暗紧紧跟在后面。母亲把篮子藏在岸边的芦苇丛中，再次祈求神保佑孩子。米利暗则藏在附近的草丛里，看弟弟会有什么遭遇。不久，残暴法老的女儿带着侍女们来到河边沐浴。她一眼瞥见草篮，惊呼道：“那芦苇丛里漂着的是什么？给我拿来。”一个侍女跑去取来篮子，递给公主。公主打开一看，里面竟是她从未见过的最俊美的男婴！孩子正醒着，一见到陌生的面孔便哭了起来。公主说：“这是我父亲下令淹死的希伯来男婴！可既然让我捡到了，我就要把他当作自己的孩子养，给他取名叫‘摩西’。”

paragraphs.text: Miriam was watching from her hiding-place in the tall grasses. She ran out and said, "Shall I bring a nurse for the baby?" "Yes," said the princess. Miriam ran home as fast as she could, and whom do you suppose she brought? The baby's own mother! And the princess told her to take him home and nurse him and care for him for her, for she loved him as her very own, and the king would not harm him.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 米利暗从藏身处跑出来问：“我去找个奶妈来好吗？”公主应允。米利暗飞奔回家，你猜她带谁来？正是婴孩的亲生母亲！公主吩咐她抱回家代为哺育照料，因为公主已视如己出，法老也不会加害。

paragraphs.text: So the prayer that Moses' mother made to God to take care of her little baby boy in the basket-boat was answered. And Moses grew up to be a great and good man.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 摩西母亲祈求神保佑草篮中婴孩的祷告得到了回应。摩西长大后成为伟大而良善的人。

entries.title: The Prince: CHAPTER XXII. CONCERNING THE SECRETARIES OF PRINCES

paragraphs.text: The choice of servants is of no little importance to a prince, and they are good or not according to the discrimination of the prince. And the first opinion which one forms of a prince, and of his understanding, is by observing the men he has around him; and when they are capable and faithful he may always be considered wise, because he has known how to recognize the capable and to keep them faithful. But when they are otherwise one cannot form a good opinion of him, for the prime error which he made was in choosing them.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 选择仆人对君主至关重要，其好坏取决于君主的辨别力。判断君主及其智慧的首要依据，就是观察他身边的人。若他们能干且忠诚，君主便被视为明智，因为他懂得识别能人并保持其忠诚。反之，则无法对君主产生好感，因为他最大的错误就在于选择不当。

paragraphs.text: There were none who knew Messer Antonio da Venafro as the servant of Pandolfo Petrucci, Prince of Siena, who would not consider Pandolfo to be a very clever man in having Venafro for his servant. Because there are three classes of intellects: one which comprehends by itself; another which appreciates what others comprehended; and a third which neither comprehends by itself nor by the showing of others; the first is the most excellent, the second is good, the third is useless. Therefore, it follows necessarily that, if Pandolfo was not in the first rank, he was in the second, for whenever one has judgment to know good and bad when it is said and done, although he himself may not have the initiative, yet he can recognize the good and the bad in his servant, and the one he can praise and the other correct; thus the servant cannot hope to deceive him, and is kept honest.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 凡知晓安东尼奥·达·韦纳夫罗为锡耶纳君主潘多尔福·佩特鲁奇效力之人，无不认为潘多尔福能得此仆从实属睿智。盖因智慧分三等：其一能自悟，其二能明辨他人所悟，其三既不能自悟亦不能借他人点拨而悟。第一等最为卓越，第二等尚可，第三等则毫无用处。由此必然推论：若潘多尔福未居第一等，必属第二等——因凡能于言行间辨明善恶者，纵无开创之才，亦能识仆从之优劣，褒善而纠恶。如此，仆从既无欺主之望，自当恪守忠诚。

paragraphs.text: But to enable a prince to form an opinion of his servant there is one test which never fails; when you see the servant thinking more of his own interests than of yours, and seeking inwardly his own profit in everything, such a man will never make a good servant, nor will you ever be able to trust him; because he who has the state of another in his hands ought never to think of himself, but always of his prince, and never pay any attention to matters in which the prince is not concerned.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然君主欲察仆从之心，有一万无一失之试：若见仆从谋己利甚于谋君利，事事暗求私益，则此人永难成良仆，亦永不可信。盖受托执掌他人邦国者，当忘己身而常念君主，凡与君主无关之事，皆不足挂怀。

paragraphs.text: On the other hand, to keep his servant honest the prince ought to study him, honouring him, enriching him, doing him kindnesses, sharing with him the honours and cares; and at the same time let him see that he cannot stand alone, so that many honours may not make him desire more, many riches make him wish for more, and that many cares may make him dread chances. When, therefore, servants, and princes towards servants, are thus disposed, they can trust each other, but when it is otherwise, the end will always be disastrous for either one or the other.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 另一方面，为使臣仆忠诚，君主应当研究他、尊敬他、厚待他、施恩于他，与他共享荣誉与职责；同时让他明白自己无法独立，以免过多的荣誉使他贪求更多，过多的财富使他渴望更多，过多的职责使他畏惧变故。因此，当臣仆与君主彼此如此相待时，他们便能互相信任；否则，结局对任何一方都将是灾难性的。

entries.title: The Blue Fairy Book: The King o' the Cats

paragraphs.text: One winter's evening the sexton's wife was sitting by the fireside with her big black cat, Old Tom, on the other side, both half asleep and waiting for the master to come home. They waited and they waited, but still he didn't come, till at last he came rushing in, calling out, "Who's Tommy Tildrum?" in such a wild way that both his wife and his cat stared at him to know what was the matter.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个冬夜，教堂司事的妻子坐在炉火旁，对面是她的大黑猫“老汤姆”。一人一猫半睡半醒，等着主人回家。他们等啊等，始终不见人影。最后司事冲进门来，狂喊道：“谁是汤米·蒂尔德鲁姆？”那副失魂落魄的模样让妻子和猫都瞪大眼睛，不知出了什么事。

paragraphs.text: "Why, what's the matter?" said his wife, "and why do you want to know who Tommy Tildrum is?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哎呀，怎么了？”妻子问道，“你干嘛要知道谁是汤米·蒂尔德鲁姆？”

paragraphs.text: "Oh, I've had such an adventure. I was digging away at old Mr. Fordyce's grave when I suppose I must have dropped asleep, and only woke up by hearing a cat's _Miaou_."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “哦，我遇到了一件怪事。我在挖老福代斯先生的坟时，大概睡着了，直到听见一声猫叫才醒过来。”

paragraphs.text: "Yes, just like that! So I looked over the edge of the grave, and what do you think I saw?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “对，就像这样！我往坟边一看，你猜我看见了什么？”

paragraphs.text: "Why, nine black cats all like our friend Tom here, all with a white spot on their chestesses. And what do you think they were carrying? Why, a small coffin covered with a black velvet pall, and on the pall was a small coronet all of gold, and at every third step they took they cried all together, _Miaou_--"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “九只黑猫，都跟咱们的汤姆一样，胸口有块白斑。你猜它们抬着什么？一口小棺材，盖着黑天鹅绒棺罩，上面还有一顶小金冠。它们每走三步就齐声喵喵叫——”

paragraphs.text: "Yes, just like that!" said the Sexton; "and as they came nearer and nearer to me I could see them more distinctly, because their eyes shone out with a sort of green light. Well, they all came towards me, eight of them carrying the coffin, and the biggest cat of all walking in front for all the world like--but look at our Tom, how he's looking at me. You'd think he knew all I was saying."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “对，就像这样！”司事说，“它们越走越近，我看得越清楚，因为它们的眼睛闪着绿光。它们都朝我走来，八只猫抬着棺材，最大那只走在最前面，活像——你看咱们汤姆，正盯着我呢。你准以为它听懂了我的话。”

paragraphs.text: "Well, as I was a-saying, they came towards me slowly and solemnly, and at every third step crying all together, _Miaou!_--"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我刚才说到，它们缓缓庄严地朝我走来，每走三步就齐声喵喵叫——”

paragraphs.text: "Yes, just like that, till they came and stood right opposite Mr. Fordyce's grave, where I was, when they all stood still and looked straight at me. I did feel queer, that I did! But look at Old Tom; he's looking at me just like they did."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “对,就是这样,直到它们走到福代斯先生的墓前,正对着我站定。它们全都停下来,直勾勾地盯着我。我当时真觉得浑身不自在,真的!可你瞧瞧老汤姆——它正像它们那样盯着我呢。”

paragraphs.text: "Where was I? Oh, they all stood still looking at me, when the one that wasn't carrying the coffin came forward and, staring straight at me, said to me--yes, I tell 'ee, _said_ to me, with a squeaky voice, 'Tell Tom Tildrum that Tim Toldrum's dead,' and that's why I asked you if you knew who Tom Tildrum was, for how can I tell Tom Tildrum Tim Toldrum's dead if I don't know who Tom Tildrum is?"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我说到哪儿了?哦,它们全都站着望我,这时那只没抬棺材的猫走上前来,直直地盯着我,对我说——没错,我跟你说,它真的开口对我说话了,用尖细的声音说:‘告诉汤姆·蒂尔德鲁姆,蒂姆·托尔德鲁姆死了。’我这才问你知不知道汤姆·蒂尔德鲁姆是谁,因为我要是不知道汤姆·蒂尔德鲁姆是谁,又怎么去告诉他蒂姆·托尔德鲁姆死了呢?”

paragraphs.text: And well he might look, for Tom was swelling and Tom was staring, and at last Tom shrieked out, "What--old Tim dead! then I'm the King o' the Cats!" and rushed up the chimney and was never more seen.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 它确实有理由这么瞪着——只见汤姆浑身鼓胀,双眼圆睁,最后猛地尖叫起来:“什么——老蒂姆死了!那我就是猫王了!”说着便冲上烟囱,从此再没露过面。

entries.title: Fifty Famous People: SAVING THE BIRDS

paragraphs.text: One day in spring four men were riding on horseback along a country road. These men were lawyers, and they were going to the next town to attend court.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 春天的一天，四个男人骑马沿着乡间小路前行。他们是律师，正要去邻镇开庭。

paragraphs.text: There had been a rain, and the ground was very soft. Water was dripping from the trees, and the grass was wet.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 刚下过雨，地面很软。水从树上滴落，草地湿漉漉的。

paragraphs.text: The four lawyers rode along, one behind another; for the pathway was narrow, and the mud on each side of it was deep. They rode slowly, and talked and laughed and were very jolly.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 四个律师一个接一个地骑着马，因为小路狭窄，两边的泥很深。他们慢慢骑行，说说笑笑，十分快活。

paragraphs.text: As they were passing through a grove of small trees, they heard a great fluttering over their heads and a feeble chirping in the grass by the roadside.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 当他们穿过一片小树林时，听到头顶传来一阵扑棱声，路边的草丛里还有微弱的叽叽声。

paragraphs.text: "What is the matter here?" asked the first lawyer, whose name was Speed. "Oh, it's only some old robins!" said the second lawyer, whose name was Hardin. "The storm has blown two of the little ones out of the nest. They are too young to fly, and the mother bird is making a great fuss about it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “这是怎么回事？”第一位律师问道，他名叫斯皮德。“哦，不过是几只知更鸟罢了！”第二位律师说，他名叫哈丁，“暴风雨把两只雏鸟吹出了巢。它们太小，还不会飞，母鸟正为这事闹得不可开交呢。”

paragraphs.text: "What a pity! They'll die down there in the grass," said the third lawyer, whose name I forget.
paragraphs.translation_zh: "真可怜！它们会死在草丛里的，"第三位律师说道，他的名字我忘了。

paragraphs.text: The three men, as they passed, looked down and saw the little birds fluttering in the cold, wet grass. They saw the mother robin flying about, and crying to her mate.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 三人经过时低头看去，只见雏鸟在冰冷潮湿的草丛里扑腾。他们看见母知更鸟焦急地飞来飞去，朝着它的伴侣哀鸣。

paragraphs.text: Then they rode on, talking and laughing as before. In a few minutes they had forgotten about the birds.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 随后他们继续策马前行，像之前一样谈笑风生。没过几分钟，他们就把小鸟的事抛诸脑后。

paragraphs.text: But the fourth lawyer, whose name was Abraham Lincoln, stopped. He got down from his horse and very gently took the little ones up in his big warm hands.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但第四位名叫亚伯拉罕·林肯的律师停了下来。他翻身下马，用温暖的大手轻轻捧起小鸟。

paragraphs.text: "Never mind, my little fellows," said Mr. Lincoln "I will put you in your own cozy little bed."
paragraphs.translation_zh: "别怕，小家伙们，"林肯先生说，"我送你们回温暖的小窝。"

paragraphs.text: Then he looked up to find the nest from which they had fallen. It was high, much higher than he could reach.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他抬头寻找小鸟掉落的巢。巢很高，远非他伸手可及。

paragraphs.text: But Mr. Lincoln could climb. He had climbed many a tree when he was a boy. He put the birds softly, one by one, into their warm little home. Two other baby birds were there, that had not fallen out. All cuddled down together and were very happy.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 但林肯先生会爬树。他小时候爬过许多树。他轻轻地把小鸟一只只放回温暖的小窝。巢里还有两只没掉出来的雏鸟。它们全都依偎在一起，十分快活。

paragraphs.text: "Do you remember those birds?" said Mr. Speed. "Very likely he has stopped to take care of them."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你还记得那些鸟吗？”斯皮德先生说，“他很可能停下来照顾它们了。”

paragraphs.text: In a few minutes Mr. Lincoln joined them. His shoes were covered with mud; he had torn his coat on the thorny tree.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 几分钟后，林肯先生赶上了他们。他的鞋上沾满泥浆，外套也被带刺的树枝刮破了。

paragraphs.text: Then all three of them laughed heartily. They thought it so foolish that a strong man should take so much trouble just for some worthless young birds.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 然后三个人都开怀大笑。他们觉得一个强壮的男人为几只不值一提的小鸟费这么大劲，真是太傻了。

paragraphs.text: "Gentlemen," said Mr. Lincoln, "I could not have slept to-night, if I had left those helpless little robins to perish in the wet grass."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “先生们，”林肯先生说，“如果我把那些无助的小知更鸟丢在湿草地上不管，今晚我会睡不着的。”

paragraphs.text: Abraham Lincoln afterwards became very famous as a lawyer and statesman. He was elected president. Next to Washington he was the greatest American.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 亚伯拉罕·林肯后来成为著名的律师和政治家，并当选总统。他是仅次于华盛顿的最伟大的美国人。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 1. SAINT CHRISTOPHER

paragraphs.text: Once there was a very strong man who could carry such heavy loads that he was called "Offero," meaning "The Bearer." He was very proud of his strength and said, "I will serve only the greatest king on earth." He found a rich and powerful king and served him, until one day he saw his master tremble. "Why do you tremble, O king?" he asked. "Because I fear Satan, who is too strong for me." "Then I will serve him," said Offero. He went at once and served Satan, until one day he noticed his new master tremble before a cross, the cross on which Christ hung to overcome the strength of Satan. Then Offero went everywhere in search of the stronger master, Christ. He found a boy who said: "Yes, Christ is the strongest King on earth or in heaven. But to find him you must cross a broad river whose current is so swift that men are drowned in trying to cross. If you serve Christ by carrying over on your strong shoulders the weak and the little ones, you shall find the Christ of your search on the other side." Offero built a hut beside the swift-flowing river, and whenever he saw a poor traveler trying to cross the stream, he bore him on his strong shoulders. Well was he named "The Bearer," for he carried many across, and not one was lost. His staff was a great palm tree which he had plucked up by the roots. One night as he was resting in his hut he heard the cry of a little child, calling, "Offero, will you carry me over this night?" A weak little child stood near the river. Offero helped him on his strong shoulders and, staff in hand, began to cross the flood. But the wind blew furiously, the waves rose high, and there was a roaring in his ears as if a great ocean were let loose. The weight upon his shoulders bore him down until he feared he would sink. But he held firmly to his strong staff and at last reached the other bank and placed the child safely on the ground. "What have I borne?" cried Offero; "it could not have been simply a young child, for the weight was too great!" Just then the child suddenly changed into the form of the strong Christ-King, who said: "Offero, as thou didst wish to serve me, I accepted thee as my servant. Thou hast borne, not the weight of a child, but the weight of a world. Thou shalt be called 'Christ-Offer--the Christ-Bearer,' and shalt serve me always. Plant thy staff in the ground, and it shall put forth leaves and fruit." Christopher did so, and the dry staff flourished as the palm tree, and was covered with clusters of fruit; but the Christ-Child had vanished from his sight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前有个力大无穷的人，能扛起极重的负担，人称“奥菲罗”，意为“背负者”。他因自己的力气而骄傲，宣称：“我只侍奉世上最伟大的国王。”他找到一位富有的国王并为其效力，直到某天看见主人颤抖。“陛下为何颤抖？”他问。“因为我惧怕撒旦，他比我强大。”“那我去侍奉他，”奥菲罗说。他立刻投靠撒旦，直到发现新主人在十字架前颤抖——那是基督为战胜撒旦而悬挂的十字架。于是奥菲罗四处寻找更强大的主人基督。他遇见一个男孩说：“是的，基督是天地间最强大的王。但你要找到他，必须先渡过一条湍急的河，许多人试图渡河时溺亡。若你以强壮肩膀背负弱小者过河来侍奉基督，便能在彼岸寻见你所寻的基督。”奥菲罗在急流边搭起小屋，每当看见穷苦旅人试图渡河，便用肩膀扛起他们。他无愧于“背负者”之名，载渡无数人，无一失丧。他的拐杖是一棵连根拔起的大棕榈树。一夜，他在屋中歇息时听见孩童哭喊：“奥菲罗，今夜能背我过河吗？”一个孱弱的孩子站在河边。奥菲罗将他扛上肩，手执拐杖踏入洪流。但狂风怒号，巨浪滔天，耳畔轰鸣如汪洋倾泻。肩上的重量压得他几乎沉没，但他紧握拐杖，终于抵达对岸，将孩子安全放下。“我背的是什么？”奥菲罗喊道，“绝非凡童，这重量太沉了！”霎时，孩子化作基督之王的形象，说：“奥菲罗，你既愿侍奉我，我便收纳你为仆。你背负的不是孩童，而是整个世界的重量。你将被称为“基督背负者”，永远侍奉我。将拐杖插地，它必生叶结果。”克里斯托弗照做，枯杖如棕榈般繁茂，结满累累果实；但基督圣婴已从他眼前消失。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE PEACOCK AND THE CRANE

paragraphs.text: A Peacock, puffed up with vanity, met a Crane one day, and to impress him spread his gorgeous tail in the Sun.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 有一天，一只自负的孔雀遇到了一只鹤，为了给鹤留下深刻的印象，孔雀在阳光下展开了它华丽的尾巴。

paragraphs.text: "Look," he said. "What have you to compare with this? I am dressed in all the glory of the rainbow, while your feathers are gray as dust!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你瞧，”它说，“你有什么能跟这相比？我浑身披着彩虹般的绚丽光彩，你的羽毛却灰得像尘土！”

paragraphs.text: "Follow me if you can," he said. But the Peacock stood where he was among the birds of the barnyard, while the Crane soared in freedom far up into the blue sky.
paragraphs.translation_zh: “有本事就跟上来吧，”鹤说道。可孔雀只能待在农家场院的家禽中间，眼看着鹤自由地振翅高飞，直上蔚蓝的天空。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 7. THE FOX AND THE GRAPES

paragraphs.text: A Fox who was hungry saw some large, juicy grapes on a vine high up in a tree. "How good they will taste," said he; "I am going to have some of them." Then he gave a run and leaped as high as he could, but the grapes were still far above his head. He could not reach them no matter how high he jumped. At last he trotted off in a rage, muttering, "I know those are sour grapes and not worth eating."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只饥饿的狐狸看见高处树上的藤蔓上挂着一串又大又多汁的葡萄。“这些葡萄一定很好吃，”他说，“我得弄几颗来尝尝。”于是他助跑一阵，使尽全力往上跳，可葡萄仍高高地够不着。无论他跳得多高，都碰不到那些葡萄。最后他怒气冲冲地跑开了，嘟囔着：“我看那些葡萄是酸的，根本不值得吃。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND HIS SHADOW

paragraphs.text: A Wolf left his lair one evening in fine spirits and an excellent appetite. As he ran, the setting sun cast his shadow far out on the ground, and it looked as if the wolf were a hundred times bigger than he really was.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一天傍晚,一只狼兴致勃勃、食欲大开地离开了巢穴。它一路奔跑,夕阳把它的影子远远地投在地上,看上去那狼仿佛比它本身大了足足一百倍。

paragraphs.text: "Why," exclaimed the Wolf proudly, "see how big I am! Fancy _me_ running away from a puny Lion! I'll show him who is fit to be king, he or I."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “嘿,”狼得意地嚷道,“瞧我这身板多大!我居然还要躲着一只瘦小的狮子!我倒要让它瞧瞧,究竟谁才配做王——是它,还是我。”

paragraphs.text: Just then an immense shadow blotted him out entirely, and the next instant a Lion struck him down with a single blow.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时,一道巨大的影子把它整个罩住,紧接着,一头狮子一爪就把它打翻在地。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FARMER AND HIS SONS

paragraphs.text: A rich old farmer, who felt that he had not many more days to live, called his sons to his bedside.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位富有的老农觉得自己的日子不多了，就把儿子们叫到床边。

paragraphs.text: "My sons," he said, "heed what I have to say to you. Do not on any account part with the estate that has belonged to our family for so many generations. Somewhere on it is hidden a rich treasure. I do not know the exact spot, but it is there, and you will surely find it. Spare no energy and leave no spot unturned in your search."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我的儿子们，”他说，“请记住我要对你们说的话。无论如何都不要变卖这片我们家族世代相传的田产。它里头某个地方藏着一大笔财宝。我不知道确切的地点，但它就在那儿，你们一定能找到。寻找时务必不遗余力，不放过任何一寸土地。”

paragraphs.text: The father died, and no sooner was he in his grave than the sons set to work digging with all their might, turning up every foot of ground with their spades, and going over the whole farm two or three times.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 父亲去世了，他刚一入土，儿子们便使出全身力气挖了起来，用铁锹把每一寸土地都翻了个遍，整座农场来回翻掘了两三趟。

paragraphs.text: No hidden gold did they find; but at harvest time when they had settled their accounts and had pocketed a rich profit far greater than that of any of their neighbors, they understood that the treasure their father had told them about was the wealth of a bountiful crop, and that in their industry had they found the treasure.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他们没有找到隐藏的金子；但到了收割的季节，当他们结清账目，获得了远远超过邻居的丰厚利润时，他们才明白，父亲告诉他们的宝藏是五谷丰登的财富，他们是在自己的勤劳中找到了宝藏。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE LION AND THE MOUSE

paragraphs.text: A Lion lay asleep in the forest, his great head resting on his paws. A timid little Mouse came upon him unexpectedly, and in her fright and haste to get away, ran across the Lion's nose. Roused from his nap, the Lion laid his huge paw angrily on the tiny creature to kill her.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一只狮子睡在森林里，硕大的脑袋枕在前爪上。一只胆小的小老鼠冷不防撞见了它，惊慌之下急着逃开，从狮子的鼻子上跑了过去。狮子从睡梦中惊醒，怒气冲冲地一巴掌按住这个小不点，要弄死它。

paragraphs.text: "Spare me!" begged the poor Mouse. "Please let me go and some day I will surely repay you."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “饶了我吧！”可怜的老鼠哀求道，“请放我走，总有一天我一定会报答你。”

paragraphs.text: The Lion was much amused to think that a Mouse could ever help him. But he was generous and finally let the Mouse go.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狮子觉得一只老鼠竟然能帮上自己，十分好笑。但它生性宽厚，最后还是放走了老鼠。

paragraphs.text: Some days later, while stalking his prey in the forest, the Lion was caught in the toils of a hunter's net. Unable to free himself, he filled the forest with his angry roaring. The Mouse knew the voice and quickly found the Lion struggling in the net. Running to one of the great ropes that bound him, she gnawed it until it parted, and soon the Lion was free.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 几天后，狮子在森林里追踪猎物，落进了猎人的罗网。它怎么也挣脱不开，愤怒的吼声响彻森林。老鼠听出了这声音，很快找到了在网里挣扎的狮子。它跑到捆住狮子的一根粗绳旁，一直啃咬，直到绳子断开，狮子很快就脱了身。

paragraphs.text: "You laughed when I said I would repay you," said the Mouse. "Now you see that even a Mouse can help a Lion."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “我说要报答你的时候，你还笑话我，”老鼠说，“现在你可看到了，就连一只老鼠也能帮上狮子。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE CAT AND THE FOX

paragraphs.text: Once a Cat and a Fox were traveling together. As they went along, picking up provisions on the way--a stray mouse here, a fat chicken there--they began an argument to while away the time between bites. And, as usually happens when comrades argue, the talk began to get personal.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 从前，一只猫和一只狐狸结伴同行。它们一路觅食——这儿逮只落单的老鼠，那儿抓只肥鸡——又趁着吃食的间隙找些话题打发时间，争论起来。而正如同伴间争论时常有的那样，话越说越冲，渐渐针对起对方来。

paragraphs.text: "You think you are extremely clever, don't you?" said the Fox. "Do you pretend to know more than I? Why, I know a whole sackful of tricks!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “你自以为聪明绝顶，是不是？”狐狸说，“难道你以为懂得比我还多？哼，我会的把戏一整麻袋都装不下！”

paragraphs.text: "Well," retorted the Cat, "I admit I know one trick only, but that one, let me tell you, is worth a thousand of yours!"
paragraphs.translation_zh: “好吧，”猫反驳道，“我承认我只会一招，但我告诉你，这一招比你的一千招还值钱！”

paragraphs.text: Just then, close by, they heard a hunter's horn and the yelping of a pack of hounds. In an instant the Cat was up a tree, hiding among the leaves.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时，附近传来了猎人的号角声和一群猎犬的吠叫声。瞬间，猫就爬上了树，躲在树叶之中。

paragraphs.text: But the Fox had so many plans for escape he could not decide which one to try first. He dodged here and there with the hounds at his heels. He doubled on his tracks, he ran at top speed, he entered a dozen burrows,--but all in vain. The hounds caught him, and soon put an end to the boaster and all his tricks.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 可狐狸逃跑的法子太多，一时竟拿不定主意先试哪一个。它带着紧追不舍的猎犬东躲西窜，沿原路兜圈折返，拼命狂奔，一连钻进十几个洞穴——却全都没用。猎犬还是逮住了它，转眼就结果了这个吹牛大王，连同它的满肚子花招。

entries.title: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: Charles Sumner

paragraphs.text: There is the national flag. He must be cold indeed who can look upon its folds, rippling in the breeze, without pride of country. If he be in a foreign land, the flag is companionship and country itself, with all its endearments. Its highest beauty is in what it symbolizes. It is because it represents all, that all gaze at it with delight and reverence.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 那是国旗。谁若能望着它在微风中招展的褶皱而不为祖国感到自豪，那他的心肠定是冷漠至极。身在异国他乡时，国旗便是陪伴，便是祖国本身，连同祖国一切令人眷恋之处。它最高的美，在于它所象征的意义。正因为它代表着一切，人们才满怀喜悦与敬意地凝望着它。

paragraphs.text: It is a piece of bunting lifted in the air; but it speaks sublimely, and every part has a voice. Its stripes of alternate red and white proclaim the original union of thirteen states to maintain the Declaration of Independence. Its stars of white on a field of blue proclaim that union of states constituting our national constellation, which receives a new star with every new state. The two together signify union past and present.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 它不过是高举在空中的一方旗布，却诉说着崇高，每一处都自有声音。红白相间的条纹，昭示着十三个州当初为捍卫《独立宣言》而结成的联邦。蓝色底面上的白星，昭示着各州联合而成的国家星群，每加入一个新州，就增添一颗新星。二者合在一起，象征着联邦的过去与现在。

paragraphs.text: The very colors have a language which was officially recognized by our fathers. White is for purity, red for valor, blue for justice; and all together, bunting, stripes, stars, and colors, blazing in the sky, make the flag of our country to be cherished by all our hearts, to be upheld by all our hands.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就连这些颜色也自有一套语言，曾得到我们先辈的正式认可。白色象征纯洁，红色象征英勇，蓝色象征正义；旗布、条纹、星辰与色彩交相辉映，在天空中熠熠生辉，让我们祖国的这面旗帜为我们万众一心所珍爱、为我们万千双手所扞卫。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE WOLF AND THE ASS

paragraphs.text: An Ass was feeding in a pasture near a wood when he saw a Wolf lurking in the shadows along the hedge. He easily guessed what the Wolf had in mind, and thought of a plan to save himself. So he pretended he was lame, and began to hobble painfully.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一头驴在树林旁的牧场里吃草,忽然瞧见一只狼正潜伏在树篱边的阴影里。它一下子就猜出了狼的心思,想出一条自救的计策:它假装瘸了腿,一瘸一拐、痛苦万分地走起路来。

paragraphs.text: When the Wolf came up, he asked the Ass what had made him lame, and the Ass replied that he had stepped on a sharp thorn.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狼走上前来，问驴子为什么瘸了，驴子回答说是踩到了一根尖刺。

paragraphs.text: "Please pull it out," he pleaded, groaning as if in pain. "If you do not, it might stick in your throat when you eat me."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “求你把刺拔出来吧,”它哼哼唧唧、装出痛苦的样子央求道,“不然你吃我的时候,这刺没准会卡进你喉咙里。”

paragraphs.text: The Wolf saw the wisdom of the advice, for he wanted to enjoy his meal without any danger of choking. So the Ass lifted up his foot and the Wolf began to search very closely and carefully for the thorn.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狼觉得这话很有道理,它可不想美餐一顿时被噎着。于是驴抬起脚,狼便凑上前去,仔仔细细地找那根刺。

paragraphs.text: Just then the Ass kicked out with all his might, tumbling the Wolf a dozen paces away. And while the Wolf was getting very slowly and painfully to his feet, the Ass galloped away in safety.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 就在这时，驴子用力一踢，把狼踢飞了十几步。当狼缓慢而痛苦地站起来时，驴却安全地飞奔而去。

paragraphs.text: "Serves me right," growled the Wolf as he crept into the bushes. "I'm a butcher by trade, not a doctor."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “活该,”狼一边钻进灌木丛一边咆哮,“我本是干屠夫的,又不是当大夫的。”

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE FOX AND THE PHEASANTS

paragraphs.text: One moonlight evening as Master Fox was taking his usual stroll in the woods, he saw a number of Pheasants perched quite out of his reach on a limb of a tall old tree. The sly Fox soon found a bright patch of moonlight, where the Pheasants could see him clearly; there he raised himself up on his hind legs, and began a wild dance. First he whirled 'round and 'round like a top, then he hopped up and down, cutting all sorts of strange capers. The Pheasants stared giddily. They hardly dared blink for fear of losing him out of their sight a single instant.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一个月光皎洁的夜晚，狐狸先生像往常一样在林中散步，看见一群雉鸡栖息在一棵高大老树的枝头，那高度他怎么也够不着。狡猾的狐狸很快找了一处明亮的月光地，好让雉鸡们能清清楚楚地看见他；他在那儿用后腿直立起来，跳起了疯狂的舞。他先像陀螺似的团团打转，又上蹿下跳，做出各种古怪的滑稽动作。雉鸡们看得眼花缭乱，几乎不敢眨一下眼，生怕一瞬间把他看丢了。

paragraphs.text: Now the Fox made as if to climb a tree, now he fell over and lay still, playing dead, and the next instant he was hopping on all fours, his back in the air, and his bushy tail shaking so that it seemed to throw out silver sparks in the moonlight.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 狐狸一会儿假装要爬树，一会儿又仰面倒下、一动不动地装死，转眼间却又四脚着地蹦跳起来，弓起脊背，毛茸茸的大尾巴不停甩动，在月光下仿佛溅出点点银色的火花。

paragraphs.text: By this time the poor birds' heads were in a whirl. And when the Fox began his performance all over again, so dazed did they become, that they lost their hold on the limb, and fell down one by one to the Fox.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 这时，可怜的鸟儿们早已晕头转向。等狐狸把那套把戏从头再演一遍，它们更是昏乱得抓不住树枝，一只接一只地跌落到狐狸跟前。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 23. KIT CARSON AND THE BEARS

paragraphs.text: Kit Carson knew all about wild animals. He was a great hunter and a good guide to soldiers and settlers. On a march one day, as he was dragging an elk he had just shot for supper, he saw two bears running toward him. His gun was empty. He threw it down, ran as fast as he could, and reached a tree just as the bears reached him. He caught hold of a branch and swung himself up in the tree just in time. Bears know how to climb trees, and soon they were climbing up in the lower branches. Kit Carson broke off a limb, and from the highest branch, where he hung, he began clubbing the bears over the nose, their tender spot. "Whack! Whack!" The stick hurt, and the bears whined and growled with pain. First one bear and then the other tried to get at him, but each got his nose hurt. When their noses felt better they tried again. But Kit Carson pounded faster and harder than ever. One of the bears cried like a baby. Then both bears got down and went away and never came back again. They were too busy rubbing their noses.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 基特·卡森对野生动物了如指掌。他是个出色的猎人，也是士兵和拓荒者的好向导。一天行军途中，他正拖着刚射来当晚饭的一头麋鹿，忽然看见两只熊朝他冲来。他枪里没了子弹，便把枪一扔，拼命奔跑，刚跑到一棵树下，熊也追到了跟前。他一把抓住树枝，及时荡身上了树。熊是会爬树的，很快就攀上了低处的枝桠。基特·卡森折下一根树枝，吊在最高的枝头，开始用棍子去敲熊的鼻子——那是它们的痛处。啪！啪！棍子打得它们生疼，两只熊又是哀叫又是咆哮。先是一只、接着另一只想要够到他，可每只都被打伤了鼻子。等鼻子不那么疼了，它们又来试，但基特·卡森打得比先前更快、更狠。其中一只熊像婴儿似的哭叫起来。最后两只熊都爬下树走开了，再也没有回来——它们光顾着揉鼻子呢。

entries.title: The Aesop for Children: THE MICE AND THE WEASELS

paragraphs.text: The Weasels and the Mice were always up in arms against each other. In every battle the Weasels carried off the victory, as well as a large number of the Mice, which they ate for dinner next day. In despair the Mice called a council, and there it was decided that the Mouse army was always beaten because it had no leaders. So a large number of generals and commanders were appointed from among the most eminent Mice.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 黄鼠狼和老鼠一向势不两立，争战不休。每一仗黄鼠狼都大获全胜，还掳走大批老鼠，第二天当作晚餐吃掉。老鼠们走投无路，便召开大会，会上认定鼠军之所以屡战屡败，是因为群龙无首。于是，它们从最出众的老鼠里选出了一大批将军和统帅。

paragraphs.text: To distinguish themselves from the soldiers in the ranks, the new leaders proudly bound on their heads lofty crests and ornaments of feathers or straw. Then after long preparation of the Mouse army in all the arts of war, they sent a challenge to the Weasels.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 为了和行伍中的士兵区别开来，这些新将领得意地在头上扎起高高的盔冠，插上羽毛或麦秸做的饰物。鼠军操练各种战术许久之后，便向黄鼠狼下了战书。

paragraphs.text: The Weasels accepted the challenge with eagerness, for they were always ready for a fight when a meal was in sight. They immediately attacked the Mouse army in large numbers. Soon the Mouse line gave way before the attack and the whole army fled for cover. The privates easily slipped into their holes, but the Mouse leaders could not squeeze through the narrow openings because of their head-dresses. Not one escaped the teeth of the hungry Weasels.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 黄鼠狼们急不可待地应了战，因为眼看有美餐可吃，它们随时都乐意厮杀一场。它们当即倾巢而出，扑向鼠军。鼠军防线很快在攻势下崩溃，全军四散奔逃，各寻藏身之处。小兵们轻易就钻进了洞里，可那些鼠将却因头上的盔饰，挤不过狭窄的洞口。没有一个逃得过饥饿黄鼠狼的利齿。

entries.title: The Beacon Second Reader: In honor of the Spartans was another column--

paragraphs.text: On the little hillock of the last resistance was placed the figure of a stone lion, in memory of Leonidas, so fitly named the lion-like; and Simonides, at his own expense, erected a pillar to his friend, the seer Megistias--
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在最后抵抗的小山丘上，立起了一尊石狮像，以纪念列奥尼达——他的名字恰如其分，正含“狮子般的勇士”之意；西摩尼得斯则自掏腰包，为他的朋友、先知墨吉斯提阿斯竖起一根石柱——

paragraphs.text: "The great Megistias' tomb you here may view, Who slew the Medes, fresh from Spercheius fords; Well the wise seer the coming death foreknew, Yet scorn'd he to forsake his Spartan lords."
paragraphs.translation_zh: “伟大的墨吉斯提阿斯之墓，你在此可以瞻仰，他斩杀了刚渡过斯佩尔基俄斯河而来的米底人；这位睿智的先知早已预知死期将至，却不屑抛下他的斯巴达主君独自逃生。”

paragraphs.text: Lion, pillars, and inscriptions have all long since passed away, even the very spot itself has changed; new soil has been formed, and there are miles of solid ground between Mount Oeta and the gulf, so that the Hot Gates no longer exist. But more enduring than stone or brass--nay, than the very battle-field itself--has been the name of Leonidas. Two thousand three hundred years have sped since he braced himself to perish for his country's sake in that narrow, marshy coast road, under the brow of the wooded crags, with the sea by his side. Since that time how many hearts have glowed, how many arms have been nerved at the remembrance of the Pass of Thermopylae, and the defeat that was worth so much more than a victory!
paragraphs.translation_zh: 石狮、石柱与碑铭早已荡然无存，连那地方本身也变了模样；新的泥沙淤积成陆，俄塔山与海湾之间如今隔着数英里坚实的土地，于是温泉关已不复存在。但比石头或青铜更长久——不，比那战场本身更长久的，是列奥尼达的英名。自他在那条狭窄泥泞的海岸小道上、在林木葱郁的峭壁脚下、身畔大海相伴，毅然为国捐躯以来，两千三百年已匆匆流逝。从那时起，每当人们追忆温泉关之役，追忆那场远比胜利更可贵的败仗，多少颗心为之激荡，多少条臂膀为之振奋！

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 6. THE INDIANS' GUNPOWDER HARVEST

paragraphs.text: At first the Indians were very kind to the white men; but after the white men began to be cruel and hard to them, they too grew hard and cruel, and nothing was too terrible for the Indians to do in revenge. They had very strange ways of carrying on their battles. They never came out and met their enemy face to face, but would skulk around behind trees in swamps or in the high grass. When the white men used guns and gunpowder, the Indians were terribly frightened, but it was not long before they themselves learned to use them. One day an old Indian chief begged some gunpowder from a white man, and ran away to his wigwam with it. The white man watched to see what he would do with it. When he reached his wigwam he called some of his friends about him, and, after a long council together, they began to plant the powder. They thought it would grow like corn and beans. Later a French trader persuaded some Indians living near the Missouri River to give him skins and furs in exchange for gunpowder, telling them it was a seed, which would grow if sown in the ground. The innocent Indians sowed all they bought, and placed a guard to protect the fields from wild beasts, going out to the field from time to time to see if the powder was growing. When they found out the trick that had been played on them they waited until the trader's partner came to exchange more goods. Then the Indians who had been tricked into sowing gunpowder gathered, went into his tent, and each helped himself to what goods he wanted. Soon the whole stock disappeared. The Frenchman, in anger, went to the chief, who said, "Yes, you shall have justice as soon as the gunpowder harvest is gathered." The Frenchman said, "Gunpowder grows in France, but your Missouri land is not good to produce it."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 起初,印第安人对白人十分友善;可自从白人对他们变得残忍刻薄之后,他们也跟着变得冷酷无情,为了报复,再可怕的事印第安人也做得出来。他们打仗的方式十分奇特:从不正面迎敌,而是躲在沼泽的树后或高高的草丛里悄悄潜行。起初白人用枪炮火药时,印第安人吓坏了,但没过多久,他们自己也学会了使用。一天,一位年迈的印第安酋长向一个白人讨来一些火药,带着它跑回了自己的棚屋。那白人就在一旁看他要拿火药做什么。酋长回到棚屋,把几个朋友召到身边,一番长谈商议之后,竟动手把火药种了下去——他们以为火药会像玉米、豆子一样长出来。后来,一个法国商人哄骗住在密苏里河附近的一些印第安人,用皮毛跟他换火药,还骗他们说这是一种种子,撒到地里就能长出来。天真的印第安人把买来的火药全撒进了地里,还派人看守田地、防备野兽,时不时到地里查看火药有没有发芽。等他们发觉自己上了当,便一直等到那商人的同伙又来交换货物。于是这些被骗去种火药的印第安人聚拢过来,走进他的帐篷,各人想要什么就拿什么。不一会儿,全部存货就被拿了个精光。法国人怒气冲冲地去找酋长,酋长却说:“好啊,等火药一收成,就还你一个公道。”法国人说:“火药在法国是长得出来的,可你们密苏里的土地却种不出来。”

paragraphs.text: All his arguments were in vain. The Indians said, "When the gunpowder harvest is reaped then the Frenchman shall have back his goods."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 他怎么争辩都是白费口舌。印第安人说:“等火药收割上来,法国人自然就能拿回他的货物。”

paragraphs.text: So the French trader returned with less goods and less money than he went, finding out, when too late, that Indians, like some other men, can be deceived but once.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 于是这位法国商人回去时,货物和钱都比来时少了;等他明白过来,已经太迟——原来印第安人和别的人一样,只能上一回当。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 17. THE LITTLE BOY KING

paragraphs.text: There were troublous times in a king's palace when a little prince was born. He was only two months old when his father, the king, was killed in battle and this little baby boy had to be hidden away by his aunt in a storeroom in the sacred temple to save his life. For seven years he was hidden there and very few knew that the little boy, who should be the king, was alive. His grandmother, a very wicked and cruel woman, Athaliah, became queen. She first ordered all the royal children she could find to be put to death and then she did many such cruel and evil things so that her people became worse and worse. After seven long years, one day a good man in the temple told five brave captains his secret, and showed them the young king and asked their help to crown him king in the place of the wicked, cruel grandmother. They promised. Soon many other soldiers came to know the secret, and on a day they decided upon, these men armed themselves with swords and spears and shields, and gathered in the temple to crown the little boy king. His granduncle, the high priest, brought him out from his hiding-place, set him upon a high platform, put a little crown of gold upon his head, while all the men clapped their hands and cried, "Long live the king!" When the queen-grandmother heard the shouts she came to the temple and looked in, and behold, there was the little boy king standing on the platform with the crown upon his head and all the captains and guards and trumpeters and people rejoicing and blowing trumpets. The queen tore her clothes in anger and cried out, "Treason! Treason!" But all her soldiers and people were sick and tired of her cruel reign. So the captains seized her, led her out of the temple, and slew her near the horse-gate of her palace. So this little boy only seven years old was crowned king because he was of the family of King David and because God took care of him in his hiding-place during all those seven long years. He reigned as King of Judah many, many years, doing great good for God and for his people. His name was Joash.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 一位小王子出生时，王宫正值动荡时期。他父亲国王在战斗中阵亡时，他才两个月大，这个小婴孩不得不被姑母藏进圣殿的储藏室里，以保住性命。他在那里藏了七年，很少有人知道这个本该做国王的小男孩还活着。他的祖母亚他利雅是个极其邪恶残忍的女人，她当上了女王。她先是下令处死所有能找到的王室子嗣，接着又做了许多类似的残忍恶事，使治下的百姓处境每况愈下。漫长的七年过后，有一天，圣殿里一位好心人把这个秘密告诉了五位勇敢的军官，让他们见了小国王，并请他们相助，拥立他为王，取代那邪恶残忍的祖母。他们答应了。很快，许多别的士兵也知道了这个秘密。到了商定的那一天，这些人佩上刀剑、长矛和盾牌，聚集到圣殿里，要拥立小男孩为王。他的叔祖——大祭司，把他从藏身处领了出来，扶他站上高台，给他头上戴上一顶小小的金冠，众人无不拍手高呼：“国王万岁！”太后听见呼喊，来到圣殿往里一看，只见小国王头戴王冠站在高台上，众军官、卫兵、号手和百姓全都欢欣鼓舞，吹响号角。太后气得撕裂衣裳，高喊：“叛乱！叛乱！”可她手下的士兵和百姓早已厌倦了她的残暴统治。于是军官们擒住她，把她押出圣殿，在她宫殿的马门附近将她处死。这个年仅七岁的小男孩就这样加冕为王，因为他出自大卫王的家族，也因为在那漫长的七年里上帝始终在藏身处护佑着他。他做了许多许多年的犹大王，为上帝、为百姓做了大量善事。他的名字叫约阿施。

entries.title: For the Children's Hour: 15. NATHAN HALE

paragraphs.text: Nathan Hale, a young man of twenty, had just graduated from Yale College, and was a school-teacher in Connecticut when the war of Independence began. At once he enlisted in the American army, saying, "Let us not lay down our arms till we have gained independence." When General Washington moved his army from Boston to Brooklyn, Nathan Hale, early one morning, when it was darkest, rowed out with a few friends to an English supply ship and sailed it away from the man-of-war that was guarding it and brought it in safety to the American camp. For this brave deed he was promoted to become "Captain" Hale. Soon after this a call was made for a volunteer for a most dangerous service. The British held possession of the lower part of New York City, and were planning a further advance, and Washington greatly needed to know their plans. It was agreed to send a spy to get these. But who would volunteer? "I will undertake it," said Captain Hale. He walked fifty miles up Long Island Sound, along the Connecticut shore and then rowed over to Long Island. Disguised as a traveling school-teacher, he visited all the English camps, making drawings and notes which he hid in his shoes. On his way back he was betrayed by a man who knew him, a traitor to the American cause. He was taken on board the English ship, carried back to New York and led before General Howe. The English general said: "You shall be pardoned and receive money and a fine position in the English army if you will give up the American cause." He said, "I cannot turn against my country!" "Then you can die for her," said the general, and sentenced him to die at daybreak. He listened to his sentence without a word, erect and fearless. At break of day the young spy, brave as a lion, faced his death without a tremor.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 内森·黑尔是个二十岁的年轻人，刚从耶鲁学院毕业，独立战争爆发时正在康涅狄格州当教师。他当即参军入伍，说道：“在赢得独立之前，我们决不放下武器。”华盛顿将军把军队从波士顿调往布鲁克林时，内森·黑尔趁一天天色最暗的清晨，与几个朋友划船登上一艘英军补给船，避开守卫它的军舰把船开走，平安带回了美军营地。凭这一勇举，他被晋升为黑尔上尉。不久，军中征召志愿者去执行一项极其危险的任务。英军占据着纽约城的南部，正策划进一步推进，华盛顿急需弄清他们的计划，于是决定派一名间谍去探取。可是谁愿意去呢？“这件事我来做，”黑尔上尉说。他沿着康涅狄格海岸顺长岛海峡步行五十英里，再划船渡到长岛。他装扮成四处教书的游方先生，走遍英军各处营地，一路绘图、记录，再把它们藏进鞋里。返回途中，他被一个认识他的人出卖了——那是个背叛美国大业的叛徒。他被押上英舰，带回纽约，送到豪将军面前。这位英国将军说：“只要你放弃美国的事业，就可获得赦免，还能得到钱财和英军里的一个好职位。”他答道：“我不能背叛自己的祖国！”“那你就为她去死吧，”将军说着，判他于破晓时处决。他一言不发地听完判决，身姿挺拔，毫无惧色。破晓时分，这位年轻的间谍勇猛如狮，面对死亡没有一丝颤抖。

paragraphs.text: In City Hall Park, New York City, not far from the jail in which he was kept that last night, and as near as possible to the spot where he died, is the statue of this noble, young patriot-martyr, who in such heroic unselfishness laid down all he possessed for his country. Upon the statue you will read his last words: "I regret that I have but one life to give for my country."
paragraphs.translation_zh: 在纽约市政厅公园里，离他生前最后一夜被关押的监狱不远，又尽量靠近他殉难的地方，矗立着这位高贵的年轻爱国烈士的雕像——他怀着何等英勇无私的胸怀，为祖国献出了自己所拥有的一切。雕像上刻着他的遗言：“我唯一的遗憾，是只能为祖国献出一次生命。”

entries.title: The Ontario Readers: Third Book: HENRY WADSWORTH LONGFELLOW

paragraphs.text: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow (1807-1882) was a native of Maine and a graduate of Bowdoin College, in the same class with Nathaniel Hawthorne. Longfellow came of early New England ancestry, his mother being a daughter of General Wadsworth of the Revolutionary War.
paragraphs.translation_zh: 亨利·沃兹沃思·朗费罗（1807—1882）是缅因州人，毕业于鲍登学院，与纳撒尼尔·霍桑是同班同学。朗费罗祖上是新英格兰的早期移民世家，他的母亲是独立战争时期沃兹沃思将军的女儿。

paragraphs.text: After his graduation from college he spent several years abroad and upon his return to America held professorships first in Bowdoin and later in Harvard College.
